Lesson 1.3: The Paradigm Shift - Your 5-Year Roadmap and 90-Day Sprint

Module 1: The Aracely Effect

Lesson Objectives

  • Identify and demolish 5 limiting beliefs that have held you back
  • Understand the real pathways from where you are to aerospace leadership
  • Create a concrete, year-by-year 5-year roadmap tailored to your situation
  • Commit to a 90-day action plan with specific, measurable milestones

Five Limiting Beliefs, Demolished

Belief 1: My English isn't perfect, so I'm not ready. Truth: Franklin Chang-Díaz speaks with a noticeable Costa Rican accent. He's led NASA programs. Your accent is your story. Companies that care about accent are companies you don't want to work for. Bilingual engineers are worth 30% more in aerospace. Stop waiting for perfect English. Start using the English you have.

Belief 2: I need an Ivy League degree to work in aerospace. Truth: Of our 10 case studies, only Ellen Ochoa went to Stanford. Rodolfo went to UNAM. Franklin went to MIT for grad school, but his undergraduate was at other universities. José went to UC Santa Barbara. Katya got her jobs through JPL work, not her degree. Strong fundamentals plus real projects beat prestige every single time. Many aerospace companies explicitly prefer engineers from diverse educational backgrounds because varied perspectives drive better engineering.

Belief 3: I'm too old or too young to start an aerospace career. Truth: José Hernández became an astronaut at 41. Franklin was in his 30s when he founded Ad Astra. Katya was 31 when she flew to space. Age is not currency in aerospace. Expertise and impact are. If anything, maturity brings problem-solving perspective that fresh graduates don't have.

Belief 4: Latin America doesn't produce world-class talent. Truth: UNAM graduates 40,000 engineers every year. Universidad Nacional in Argentina has world-leading physics. Tec de Monterrey produces aerospace engineers competing globally. The visibility gap is marketing, not quality. Your university might not be famous internationally, but your education can be excellent. Prove it through projects and networks. One well-executed portfolio project proves more than a diploma.

Belief 5: I don't have connections, so I can't break in. Truth: None of our 10 case studies had aerospace family connections. All of them built their networks intentionally. In 2026, you have GitHub, LinkedIn, Discord, YouTube, Medium, Reddit, aerospace conferences, webinars. The barrier to connection isn't access. It's initiative. Send one thoughtful message per week to an aerospace professional. Attend one webinar per month. After 6 months, you'll have 20+ genuine relationships. After a year, 50+. Network isn't built in a day. It's built by showing up consistently.

Your 5-Year Roadmap: Foundation to Expert

Year Career Milestone Specific Actions
Year 1: Build Foundation STEM Fluency Plus Aerospace Awareness English language to TOEFL 100+ if not native speaker. Complete 2 coding projects (Python, MATLAB, or C++) with code on GitHub. Network with 10 aerospace professionals via LinkedIn with thoughtful messages, not automated connections. Volunteer with a STEM program or local aerospace company. Read 12 aerospace research papers, one per month, and write 1-page summaries.
Year 2: Signal Specialization Niche Expertise Emerging Take an advanced course in your target specialty (propulsion, controls, thermal systems, AI for aerospace, materials). Publish 1 technical blog post or paper on something you've learned. Contribute meaningful code or documentation to an open-source aerospace project. Secure a 3-6 month internship at SpaceX, Blue Origin, Boeing, Northrop, or similar. Attend 2 industry conferences and have 1-on-1 conversations with engineers. Expand network to 25+ professionals.
Year 3: Deep Specialization Recognized Expert in Your Niche Complete a Master's degree OR earn an advanced professional certification (equivalent to GS-13 level). Deliver 1 major portfolio project that is either published, open-source, or internal to a company. Present at a conference or give a technical talk. Establish 2-3 mentors in your specialty. Gain recognition as someone who knows a specific thing very deeply.
Year 4: Industry Role Full-Time Engineer at Tier-1 Company Secure a full-time engineering role at a major aerospace company (GS-12 equivalent or higher, $120K+). Lead technical sub-projects. Mentor 2-3 junior engineers. Stay sharp: take one specialized course per year. Build reputation as someone who delivers.
Year 5: Leadership and Multiplication Expert, Mentor, Leader Deep specialization established (patents, publications, recognized expertise). Lead a major project or initiative. Mentor 5+ engineers. Speak at conferences. Give back through mentoring, teaching, community work, or open-source contributions.

Your 90-Day Action Plan: The Accelerant

The first 90 days determine trajectory. Treat this as non-negotiable. You're not aiming for perfection. You're aiming for consistency and visibility.

Timeline Specific Actions Success Metrics
Weeks 1-2: Foundation Enroll in English course (formal or online platform like Coursera, if not fluent). Connect with 5 aerospace professionals on LinkedIn with personalized messages (not template). Join 1 online aerospace community (Reddit r/aerospace, Discord aerospace server, local aerospace club meetup). English enrollment confirmed. 5 LinkedIn connection requests sent with specific, thoughtful notes about their work. 1 community joined with intro posted.
Weeks 3-4: Build Visible Work Start 1 coding project (Python: orbit calculator, reentry simulator, or propulsion data analysis). Read 1 aerospace research paper. Watch 3 aerospace YouTube channels (10-15 min each). Begin documenting your learning. Code pushed to GitHub with README. Paper read and 1-page summary written. YouTube playlist created with notes on 3 channels.
Weeks 5-12: Sustain and Expand Code 30 minutes daily, 5 days per week (150 hours over 10 weeks). Attend 1 webinar per week on aerospace, engineering, or relevant tech. Comment thoughtfully on 3 posts per week in aerospace communities (not spam, real engagement). Schedule 1 networking coffee chat per month (virtual or in-person). Apply to 2-3 internships or entry-level roles per month. 30+ hours of code completed and visible on GitHub. 8 webinars attended with notes. 24+ substantive comments posted in communities. 2-3 real conversations held. 6-9 applications submitted.

Accountability Framework: Your Support System

Don't do this alone. Accountability accelerates progress by 3x. Here's how to build it.

  • Weekly Check-In (Every Sunday): Update your 90-day tracker: Hours coded this week. Networking conversations held. Applications submitted. Webinars attended. Write it down. Tracking creates awareness.
  • Monthly Retrospective (First Sunday of Month): Reflect in writing: What went well? What blocked me? What's working? What needs to change? Share this with someone, even if just a friend.
  • Peer Accountability Group: Find 2-3 others (from this course or elsewhere) pursuing aerospace careers. Set up a monthly video call to share progress. Knowing others are watching makes you show up.
  • Mentor Connection: By week 8 of your 90 days, identify ONE aerospace professional willing to take a 15-minute call once per quarter to advise on your roadmap. Send a respectful, specific request. Most say yes if you're serious.

Video Lesson: Your 5-Year Roadmap Walkthrough

Video Script (for Aracely) - 1100 words, approximately 7-8 minutes of spoken content

I want to walk through something deeply personal with you. My five-year path wasn't a straight line. It was full of detours, rejections, and moments where I questioned if I was making the right choice. But looking back, I can see the pattern. I can see the choices that mattered. Let me walk you through my path, and then I'll show you how to build your own. When I was starting out, I was focused on one thing: building expertise. I didn't have connections in the aerospace industry. I didn't have family in NASA. I didn't have prestige from a famous university. What I had was determination. And I had access to learning. I studied constantly. I worked hard to improve my English. I got a degree. Then another. Then another. Seven degrees total. Not because I'm exceptional. I did it because that's what was needed to open doors. In Year 1, I was focused on fundamentals. Getting my English to a level where I could communicate technical concepts. Getting my engineering foundation solid. Reading research papers. Trying to understand what people in the field actually worked on. Year 2, I specialized. I decided I was interested in systems engineering and space operations. I took advanced courses. I volunteered. I looked for opportunities to get experience. Year 3, I pursued my master's. I started to develop deep knowledge in one area. I networked more intentionally. I wasn't just reading papers. I was reaching out to researchers. Asking questions. Trying to get informational interviews. Year 4, I got my first real aerospace job. It wasn't at NASA yet. But it was in the industry. I worked hard. I learned systems. I stayed humble. I asked good questions. Year 5, I had moved to a more senior role. I was leading small projects. I was starting to mentor others. I could see that my expertise mattered. I could see myself building something. And then, over the next years, I kept going. More degrees. More responsibility. More missions. Now I lead operations on multiple NASA missions. I've worked on TRMM, LRO, JWST. I'm the first Latin American woman to lead three major missions concurrently. But here's the thing: I didn't know that would happen when I was starting out. I just knew I was going to show up and do the work. I knew I was going to build expertise. I knew I was going to be serious about it. Now, your path will be different. You might specialize in propulsion. You might focus on AI and autonomous systems. You might become a researcher. You might start a company. You might do something I can't even imagine yet. But the pattern is the same. Year 1: Build foundation. Get good at basics. Network intentionally. Show up. Year 2: Signal your specialty. Start becoming known for something. Get real experience. Read deeply. Year 3: Deepen that expertise. Become the person who knows a thing very well. Year 4: Get a real job doing that thing. Lead projects. Prove yourself. Year 5: Become a leader and mentor others. This is your roadmap. These are not rules. These are patterns I've seen work. And I know they can work for you. Now let me talk about the 90 days. The 90 days are your accelerant. If you do the first 90 days right, everything that comes after is easier. Here's why: because you build visible work, you build real relationships, you prove to yourself that you can do this. The first 90 days are about establishing a rhythm. You're not trying to be perfect. You're trying to be consistent. Code 30 minutes a day. That's 150 hours in 10 weeks. That's real progress. Send one message a week to an aerospace professional. That's 13 genuine conversations by the end of 90 days. Apply to jobs and internships relentlessly. You're probably going to get rejected a lot. That's normal. It's not personal. It's feedback. After 90 days, if you've done this, you'll have visible portfolio work, a growing network, and applications in the system. You'll be different. You'll be someone who's moving. And movement attracts opportunity. So here's my ask: commit to the 90 days. Not because it's going to be easy. But because it's going to be worth it. Your story matters. Your background is an asset. Your perspective is valuable. And space needs you. Make the commitment. Do the work. And come back to this course after 90 days and tell me what changed.

Make your commitment public. Write it here. Your name, your goal, your timeline. This community will hold you accountable. Which limiting belief are you demolishing first, and why?

Knowledge Check Quiz

Question 1: Which statement about English fluency in aerospace is TRUE?
A) You need perfect English before applying | B) Bilingual engineers earn 30% more | C) Accents disqualify you | D) Only native speakers can lead programs

Question 2: How many of the 10 case studies attended Ivy League universities?
A) All 10 | B) Most (7-9) | C) About half (5) | D) Only 1 (Ellen Ochoa at Stanford)

Question 3: At what age did José Hernández become an astronaut?
A) 25 | B) 31 | C) 41 | D) 51

Question 4: According to the 5-year roadmap, what should you prioritize in Year 1?
A) Getting a master's degree immediately | B) Building STEM core and foundation network | C) Leading major projects | D) Starting your own company

Question 5: What is the recommended daily time commitment for coding in Weeks 5-12 of your 90-day plan?
A) 1-2 hours | B) 15-30 minutes | C) 1 hour | D) 2-3 hours

Question 6: How many networking messages should you send per week according to the 90-day plan?
A) 1 thoughtful message | B) 5 messages | C) 10 messages | D) Daily messages

Question 7: What is the primary purpose of the first 90 days?
A) Get a job immediately | B) Earn a degree | C) Build visible work, real relationships, and prove to yourself you can do this | D) Become an expert

Complete quiz: +30 XP

Answer Key: 1=B, 2=D, 3=C, 4=B, 5=B, 6=A, 7=C

Answer 1: B. The lesson explicitly states that bilingual engineers are worth 30% more in aerospace. Accent is not a barrier; it's part of your identity.

Answer 2: D. Only Ellen Ochoa among the 10 case studies attended an Ivy League school (Stanford). Others attended regional, national, and state universities.

Answer 3: C. José Hernández became an astronaut at age 41, proving that age is not a barrier to starting an aerospace career.

Answer 4: B. Year 1 is about building STEM foundation, English fluency if needed, and establishing your network with 10 aerospace professionals.

Answer 5: B. The 90-day plan recommends 30 minutes of daily coding, 5 days per week, which totals approximately 150 hours over 10 weeks.

Answer 6: A. Send ONE thoughtful message per week to an aerospace professional. That's 13 genuine conversations by the end of 90 days.

Answer 7: C. The primary purpose of 90 days is to build visible portfolio work, establish real relationships, and prove to yourself that you can execute on a plan.

Module 2: English Rocket Fuel
ENGLISH
← Previous: Lesson 1.3Course index

Lesson 2.1: Language Strategy + 90-Day English Plan

Module 2: English Rocket Fuel

Lesson Objectives

  • Understand why English fluency is the number-one career multiplier for aerospace professionals
  • Learn TOEFL/IELTS requirements by university and employer (MIT, SpaceX, NASA)
  • Master a structured 90-day plan: Foundation, Production, Technical phases
  • Build a resource toolkit and measure ROI of English investment
  • Discover how Aracely navigated English as a second language immigrant engineer

Video Lesson: 90-Day English Roadmap

Watch Aracely share her English learning path and walk you through the three-phase strategy (approximately 8 minutes).

Video Script (for Aracely)

Hi. I'm Aracely Quispe Neira, and I want to tell you something that might surprise you. When I arrived in the United States at eighteen years old from Lambayeque, Peru, I spoke almost no English. Not a little bit. Almost none. I had studied it in school, sure, but the English I knew was grammar textbook English, memorized verb conjugations. It was not the English of daily life, of technical meetings, of survival.

I came here to study engineering. I was accepted to a good program, but there was a problem. My classes were entirely in English. My professors spoke fast. American professors do not slow down for international students. My engineering textbooks were in English. The library was in English. The study groups were in English. I sat in lectures understanding maybe forty percent. I went home and cried.

That was 1999. I did not have YouTube. I did not have iTalki. I did not have language apps. I had a dictionary, a lot of stubbornness, and a grandmother's voice in my head saying, "Mija, there is always a way." So I built my own system. I will share it with you because I know that what I did then, refined now with modern tools, will work for you.

The first thing you need to understand is why English matters in aerospace. It is not just nice to have. It is essential. Every major aerospace organization on Earth operates in English. NASA operates in English. SpaceX operates in English. The European Space Agency operates in English. When astronauts from ten different countries work on the International Space Station, they communicate in English. When engineers write specifications for rockets, they write in English. When you sit in a technical interview with a NASA flight director, you will be interviewed in English.

The data backs this up. Engineers with strong English certification earn on average twenty-eight percent higher salaries across aerospace, technology, and energy. That is not a small number. That is a house, a car, student loan forgiveness. The most valuable engineers in aerospace are not just brilliant. They are brilliant and they can communicate. They can write emails that are clear. They can present technical ideas in meetings without hesitation. They can understand native speakers on video calls and not feel lost.

When you apply for a NASA clearance, they test your English. When you apply for an H-1B visa, they want documented English proficiency. When you move into leadership at any aerospace company, you are managing meetings in English. Writing proposals in English. Mentoring junior engineers in English. Your accent does not matter. Your clarity matters. Your confidence matters.

So here is the plan. It is a ninety-day plan because ninety days is achievable. It is not a year. It is not five years. It is a quarter. If you commit fully to these three phases, in ninety days your English will be transformed. You will understand more. You will speak with more confidence. You will be ready to take the TOEFL or IELTS and score high enough to matter.

Phase One is Foundation. Days one through thirty. Your job in this phase is to fill your ears with English. You are building what linguists call receptive vocabulary. You are not trying to speak yet. You are listening. You are absorbing the rhythm of English, the intonation, the accent of aerospace professionals.

I want you to listen to aerospace podcasts and YouTube channels every single day. Scott Manley. Everyday Astronaut. NASA's official channels. Listen while you commute. Listen while you cook. Listen while you exercise. One to two hours daily. Do not take notes. Do not pause and look up words. Just let it flow into your ears. Your brain is more powerful than you think. It will pick up patterns. It will learn.

At the same time, hire a tutor through iTalki. Two sessions per week, thirty minutes each. Find a tutor with aerospace background or TOEFL teaching experience. Your tutor's job is not to teach you grammar. It is to listen to you speak and correct your pronunciation and your patterns. The tutor is your feedback mirror. You will stumble. You will make mistakes. The tutor catches them and shows you the right way.

Use Anki for vocabulary. Anki is a flashcard tool. It uses something called spaced repetition. Every card you see, Anki knows when you are most likely to forget it. It shows it to you just before you forget. You can find pre-built aerospace decks online. Twenty minutes daily. Focus on the words that matter in your field. Not just common English. Technical English.

By the end of Phase One, you should understand seventy percent of technical aerospace YouTube videos without subtitles. Not perfectly. But enough that you catch the main ideas and the vocabulary starts feeling familiar.

Phase Two is Production. Days thirty-one through sixty. Now you start speaking more. You use the Tandem app. Tandem connects you with native English speakers and near-native speakers from around the world. You have thirty-minute conversations in English every single day. You talk about aerospace. You talk about your engineering background. You talk about your dreams. You stumble. You laugh. You try again.

You also escalate your iTalki tutoring. Four sessions per week now instead of two. You are speaking more. You are getting feedback more. You are refining your accent and your sentence structures. You are building muscle memory in your mouth for English sounds that might not exist in Spanish.

In this phase, start writing. Medium. Dev.to. Write one technical post per week. Three hundred words about an aerospace topic you love. Get your tutor to read it. Get feedback. Rewrite it. This forces you to think in English, not translate from Spanish.

By the end of Phase Two, you should be able to give a five to ten-minute technical presentation on your field with minimal hesitation. Not perfectly. But with growing confidence. You are no longer performing English. You are becoming an English speaker.

Phase Three is Technical and Certification. Days sixty-one through ninety. Now it is exam time if you need a formal credential. TOEFL iBT or IELTS Academic. Take the official ETS practice tests. Every three days, take a full-length practice exam. This is not fun. This is work. But this is what prepares you to score high.

At the same time, drill specialized aerospace vocabulary. Propellant grain. Avionics suite. Gimbal control. Aerothermal loads. These are the words that will appear in technical interviews. Thirty minutes of Anki daily focused on advanced aerospace terms.

Schedule mock interview practice through iTalki. Hire a tutor to ask you NASA-style and SpaceX-style questions. Technical interviews in aerospace are not about being perfect. They are about showing that you can think under pressure and communicate your thinking clearly.

By Day Ninety, you will take the official TOEFL iBT or IELTS exam. You will have your score in hand. Most universities and employers recognize TOEFL scores over one hundred as strong evidence of English fluency. Most sponsorships and clearances want IELTS at seven-point-zero or higher. This exam is not the finish line. It is a checkpoint. It proves to employers and visa officers that you can do this.

I know this sounds intense. It is. Language is not learned passively. It is learned through deliberate practice, repetition, and courage to fail in front of people. But I did it without YouTube. Without apps. Without iTalki. You have all the tools. You have no excuses.

Your accent will probably not disappear. Mine did not. I have a Costa Rican colleague at NASA named Franklin Chang-Díaz. He is a PhD physicist from MIT. He leads major projects. He speaks with a Costa Rican accent. No one cares. They care that he is brilliant and he can articulate his ideas clearly.

So here is my challenge to you. Pick today as Day One. Download Anki tonight. Watch a NASA YouTube video for thirty minutes. Schedule your first iTalki session. Tomorrow, do it again. On Day Thirty, you will be amazed at yourself. On Day Sixty, you will be unstoppable. On Day Ninety, you will be ready.

English is not a barrier. English is your rocket fuel. It is the thing that launches your career into orbit. Let's go.

English: The Non-Negotiable Aerospace Currency

NASA, SpaceX, Boeing, Blue Origin, and the European Space Agency all operate in English. Not Spanish, not Portuguese, not Mandarin-English. Your technical brilliance means nothing if you cannot communicate it clearly and with confidence.

The hard numbers:

  • Salary multiplier: Engineers with C1/C2 English certification earn on average 28% higher salaries across aerospace, tech, and energy sectors compared to conversational-only peers.
  • SpaceX hiring requirement: All engineering roles require conversational fluency plus the ability to read technical documentation. Non-negotiable for employment.
  • NASA security clearance: Security clearance investigations require documented English proficiency. No clearance means no ISS work, no classified projects, no federal contract access.
  • Conference participation: 89% of major aerospace conferences are conducted in English. Missing presentations equals missing innovation trends and professional networking.
  • Visa sponsorship: H-1B visa strength depends on demonstrated English plus specialized knowledge. English fluency raises visa priority and reduces sponsorship cost to employers.
  • Leadership pathway: Every leadership role in aerospace requires managing meetings, writing proposals, and mentoring in English. You cannot reach flight director or principal engineer roles without fluency.

The multiplier effect: Strong English transforms you from "qualified engineer" to "strategic asset." Companies compete for bilingual talent who can bridge LATAM markets and communicate with global teams.

University and Employer English Requirements

Institution/Employer TOEFL iBT Minimum IELTS Minimum Alternative Requirement
MIT (Graduate Engineering) 100+ 7.0+ Native speaker or undergraduate degree from English-speaking country
Caltech (Graduate Engineering) 100+ 7.0+ Same as MIT
Stanford (Aerospace Engineering) 99+ 7.0+ Same as MIT
Purdue (Aeronautical Engineering) 80+ 6.5+ Same as MIT
NASA (Engineer Job Code GS-12+) N/A N/A Conversational fluency (must pass verbal interview and clearance assessment)
SpaceX (All Engineering Positions) N/A N/A Conversational fluency plus technical vocabulary (read specs, write emails, understand video calls)
Blue Origin (All Engineering Positions) N/A N/A Conversational fluency minimum for hire
H-1B Visa (Any Employer) 90+ 6.5+ Documented English ability for visa petition and employer sponsorship request

Reality check: Most Latin American engineers plateau at conversational English and cap career growth at 70-80% of potential. Universities and employers demand evidence. Formal certification matters because it proves your commitment and capability to visa officers, security investigators, and hiring managers.

The 90-Day Plan: Three Phases to Fluency

English fluency is not an accident. It is a project with phases, milestones, and measurable output. Here is the structure that works.

Phase 1: Foundation (Days 1-30)

Goal: Build receptive vocabulary and passive comprehension of aerospace English. Target: understand 70% of technical YouTube videos without subtitles.

  • Shadowing technique (1-2 hours daily): Listen to aerospace content every single day. NASA Johnson Space Center channel. Everyday Astronaut. Scott Manley. Real Engineers React series. Do NOT take notes. Do NOT pause to look up words. Let your brain absorb rhythm, intonation, and technical accent naturally.
  • Live tutoring (2 sessions per week, 30 minutes each): Schedule sessions through iTalki with tutors who have aerospace background or TOEFL certification. Focus on pronunciation correction and listening comprehension feedback. Budget: 10-15 dollars per week.
  • Anki spaced repetition (20 minutes daily): Start with "Aerospace English 1000" pre-built deck available on Anki.com. Focus on verbs, nouns, and collocations used in aerospace. Spaced repetition ensures you remember what matters.
  • Passive exposure: Change your phone language to English. Watch aerospace documentaries with subtitles. Read aerospace Reddit threads. Read NASA blog posts even if you understand only half. Your brain learns from context.

Phase 1 output: By Day 30, you understand main ideas in technical aerospace videos. You can recognize 500+ aerospace-specific English words. You have a tutor relationship established and daily study routine locked in.

Phase 2: Production (Days 31-60)

Goal: Build speaking fluency and writing accuracy for technical communication. Target: deliver a 5-10 minute technical presentation with minimal hesitation.

  • Daily speaking practice via Tandem (30 minutes daily): Use the Tandem language exchange app to talk with native speakers and near-native speakers. Seek out aerospace engineers, pilots, and space enthusiasts. Speak about your background, your interests, your projects. Stumble. Laugh. Try again. Cost: 5-8 dollars per week. Free version available but limited.
  • Tutoring intensification (4 sessions per week, 30-45 minutes each): Move to 4 tutoring sessions per week on iTalki. Hire tutors with aerospace or TOEFL teaching background. Speak your field. Practice technical storytelling. Record yourself and listen back to identify patterns. Budget: 40-60 dollars per week.
  • Technical writing (1 post per week, 300 words): Start a blog on Medium, Dev.to, or Hashnode. Write one post per week about an aerospace topic you love. Topics: "How Gimbal Control Works," "Why Rocket Bodies Fall Slower Than You Think," "The Physics of Orbital Mechanics." Get your tutor to review for grammar and clarity. Rewrite based on feedback.
  • Speaking milestones: By Day 45, record a 5-minute explanation of a technical concept in your field. By Day 60, present that same topic live to your iTalki tutor and ask for feedback on delivery, not just grammar.

Phase 2 output: By Day 60, you are conversational in aerospace English. You can explain technical ideas. You can ask questions in meetings. You can handle basic professional communication. You are starting to think in English instead of translating from Spanish.

Phase 3: Technical and Certification (Days 61-90)

Goal: Master specialized aerospace vocabulary and pass formal TOEFL iBT or IELTS exam with strong score. Target: TOEFL 100+ or IELTS 7.0+ to prove fluency to employers and visa officers.

  • TOEFL iBT or IELTS preparation (practice exam every 3 days): Download official ETS TOEFL iBT practice tests. Take full-length practice exams every three days. This phase is exam-focused and intense. You are not learning English anymore. You are learning how to perform English under time pressure. Cost: 25 dollars per practice test, approximately 150 dollars for 6 tests.
  • Advanced aerospace vocabulary (30 minutes daily Anki): Create or find advanced aerospace Anki deck. Focus on technical collocations and specialized terms: "propellant grain," "avionics suite," "gimbal control," "aerothermal loads," "structural analysis," "thermal protection system," "guidance and navigation." These are the words that appear in technical interviews.
  • Mock interviews (2-3 sessions, 30-45 minutes each): Schedule mock interview practice through iTalki with tutors experienced in technical interviews. Ask NASA-style technical questions. SpaceX-style behavioral questions. Practice thinking and speaking under mild pressure. Record sessions and review for clarity and pacing.
  • Exam date lock-in: Schedule your official TOEFL iBT or IELTS Academic exam for Day 90. This creates accountability. You cannot skip. The exam forces you to perform.

Phase 3 output: By Day 90, you have an official TOEFL iBT score (100+) or IELTS Academic score (7.0+). You have mastered aerospace technical vocabulary. You can handle technical interviews with growing confidence. You have proof in hand that you are a serious English speaker.

Essential Resources and Budget Breakdown

Tool Cost/Month Time/Week Why Use It
Anki (Aerospace deck) Free 2-3 hours Spaced repetition for vocabulary retention. Custom aerospace deck beats generic apps. Proven to work by thousands of medical students, lawyers, and engineers.
iTalki (live tutoring) 40-60 3-4 hours Live correction from native or near-native tutors. Hire tutors with aerospace background or TOEFL expertise. Non-negotiable for speaking improvement. You get immediate feedback.
Tandem (language exchange) 6-8 3-5 hours Daily speaking partner from around the world. Free version available but limited to one chat per day. Premium worth it for unlimited conversations with native speakers.
ETS TOEFL Practice (official) 25 per test 1-2 hours per test Official test prep from the test makers. Only use official tests for accurate score prediction. Six practice tests equals 150 dollars but worth every cent.
YouTube Premium (optional) 13 10+ hours Ad-free immersion in aerospace content. Enables background play. Not essential but removes friction from daily listening habit.
Medium or Dev.to (optional) 5-10 2 hours Platform for your technical writing. Free accounts available. Membership helps support other writers and gives you ad-free reading.
TOEFL iBT Exam (official, one-time) 245 3 hours Official certification accepted globally. Required for university grad school applications and visa petitions. Schedule by Day 85 to test by Day 90.

90-Day Total Budget: 400-500 dollars (exam plus tutoring plus apps). Compare this to salary boost: 28% higher earnings on entry-level aerospace salary of 70,000 dollars equals 19,600 dollars per year. One year of raises pays back 40 times your investment. ROI is 40:1.

Why Latin American Engineers Struggle with English and How to Avoid It

Your advantage is mathematical and technical rigor. Your challenge is speaking confidence and exam pressure. Here is what trips up Spanish speakers:

  • Cognate confidence trap: "Hospital" looks like "hospital." You skip grammar checking. But aerospace demands precision. "Pressure vessel" is not "presión vasija." False cognates are dangerous. Study these: "constipated" (not constipado), "embarrassed" (not embarazada), "actually" (not actualmente), "success" (not suceso).
  • Pronunciation shyness: Spanish speakers often whisper English sounds. Aerospace requires confident, clear communication. Practice volume and clarity deliberately. Speak louder than feels natural. Your voice matters.
  • Exam timing anxiety: TOEFL reading is timed. Spanish readers process English text slower due to language processing overhead. Practice timed sections starting from Day 30. You need speed endurance, not just comprehension.
  • Presentation anxiety: You understand more than you speak. This gap creates anxiety. Practice speaking BEFORE you feel ready. Discomfort equals growth zone. Record yourself and listen back. Feedback is love.
  • Accent preservation: Franklin Chang-Díaz, a Costa Rican-born MIT PhD and NASA astronaut who leads major aerospace programs, speaks with a clear Costa Rican accent. Your accent is not a barrier. Clarity is. Confidence is. Stop trying to sound American. Sound like yourself, clearly.

Download: 90-Day English Plan Tracker and Resources

Use these tools to structure your 90-day path.

  • 90-Day English Tracker Spreadsheet (XLSX) - Daily checklist for all three phases, progress tracking, and weekly reflection prompts.
  • STEM Vocabulary Flashcard Reference (PDF) - 200 aerospace terms with definitions and example sentences.
  • TOEFL Practice Schedule (PDF) - 30-day exam prep calendar with daily targets.
  • Mock Interview Question Bank (PDF) - 25 NASA-style and SpaceX-style technical and behavioral questions.

Community Discussion Prompt

Share with your cohort:

What is your honest English level right now (A2, B1, B2, C1)? What is your biggest English challenge: speaking confidence, listening speed, writing precision, or exam pressure? Name one aerospace YouTube channel or podcast that has helped you learn English. What did it teach you?

This is a vulnerable share. Your peers have the same fears. Honesty here builds trust.

Knowledge Check Quiz

Q1: What is the primary financial ROI of strong English fluency in aerospace?

A) Better accent and pronunciation
B) 28% higher average salary plus visa sponsorship priority and security clearance advantage
C) Easier immigration process only
D) More job interview offers only

Answer: B. Higher salary, visa advantages, and clearance qualification. English fluency opens doors financially and in security access.

Q2: The 90-day plan divides into which three phases?

A) Speaking, Writing, Reading
B) Foundation, Production, Technical and Certification
C) TOEFL, IELTS, and Tutoring
D) Listening, Speaking, and Exam Prep

Answer: B. Foundation (1-30): listening. Production (31-60): speaking and writing. Technical (61-90): vocabulary and exam prep.

Q3: In Phase 1 Foundation, what is the primary learning method?

A) Grammar textbook study
B) Shadowing aerospace YouTube content daily without note-taking
C) Conversation practice with tutors
D) TOEFL exam preparation

Answer: B. Shadowing (passive listening) builds receptive vocabulary and accent ear. Notes interrupt the learning flow.

Q4: What is the minimum TOEFL iBT score recommended for NASA and university graduate programs?

A) 80
B) 90
C) 100
D) 110

Answer: C. MIT, Caltech, and Stanford all require 99-100+. NASA requires conversational fluency (no formal test) but 100+ strengthens your candidacy significantly.

Q5: Why does Aracely recommend NOT taking notes during Phase 1 shadowing?

A) Note-taking is a waste of time
B) Your brain learns patterns and rhythm better through uninterrupted listening absorption
C) Tutors will teach you vocabulary later
D) You will forget the words anyway

Answer: B. Uninterrupted listening builds receptive vocabulary and accent recognition naturally. Pausing and noting disrupts the flow your brain needs to absorb rhythm and intonation.

Q6: Franklin Chang-Díaz is mentioned as an example of what?

A) How to remove your accent
B) How to get a PhD in aerospace
C) How accent does not prevent leadership in aerospace if you speak clearly
D) How to become an astronaut

Answer: C. Franklin Chang-Díaz arrived in the US at 18 speaking only Spanish, earned an MIT PhD, flew 7 space shuttle missions, and leads major aerospace programs. His Costa Rican accent is unchanged. He proves accent is not a barrier.

Q7: What is the approximate 90-day investment in the plan (tutoring, apps, exam)?

A) 100-200 dollars
B) 250-350 dollars
C) 400-500 dollars
D) 600-800 dollars

Answer: C. 400-500 dollars covers tutoring, apps, and exam. The TOEFL exam alone is 245 dollars. Compare to 28% salary boost: 40:1 ROI in first year.

Gamification and Streak Tracking

Watch Video Lesson: +50 XP
Complete Knowledge Check: +30 XP
Community Discussion Post: +20 XP
Download Resources: +10 XP

Total Available This Lesson: 110 XP

Streak Bonus: Language Igniter Badge

Complete lessons on 3 consecutive days and open up the Language Igniter badge. Earn 2x XP multiplier for 7 days. Your commitment matters. Your streak matters.

ENGLISH
← Previous: Lesson 2.1Course index

Lesson 2.2: STEM Vocabulary and TOEFL/IELTS Mastery

Module 2: English Rocket Fuel

Lesson Objectives

  • Master 100 core aerospace technical terms in English and Spanish
  • Understand TOEFL iBT and IELTS Academic exam structure
  • Learn the vocabulary and collocations that appear most in aerospace job interviews
  • Identify and overcome false cognates that trip up Spanish speakers
  • Build a six-week vocabulary sprint schedule with spaced repetition

Video Lesson: 100 Aerospace Terms You Must Know

Aracely breaks down essential vocabulary, pronunciation, and real-world usage in aerospace engineering (approximately 9 minutes).

Video Script (for Aracely)

When I first sat down to work on TRMM, the Tropical Rainfall Measuring Mission, I opened the technical specifications. I understood maybe thirty percent. Why? Because I knew English. I did not know aerospace English. There is a difference.

Technical vocabulary is not just bigger words. It is a language within the language. Aerospace has its own grammar, its own collocations, its own way of saying things. You cannot just translate from Spanish word-for-word. It does not work.

For example, in Spanish you might say "presión del combustible." In aerospace English, we say "propellant pressure." In Spanish, "temperatura del motor." In aerospace, "engine nozzle temperature." The words matter. The order matters. The collocation matters.

There are one hundred terms you absolutely must know. Not to pass a test. To do your job. To understand emails from colleagues. To read specifications. To present technical work. To survive in a meeting where five native speakers are throwing around aerospace jargon at conversation speed.

These terms fall into seven categories. Propulsion. Avionics. Thermodynamics. Orbital mechanics. Guidance and navigation. Aerodynamics. Structural analysis and materials science.

Let me start with propulsion because it is where most of your career will start. A rocket engine burns fuel. The fuel is called propellant. There is liquid propellant and solid propellant. Liquid propellant is stored in tanks called tanks. Solid propellant comes as blocks called propellant grains. The burn rate of the propellant grain determines how long the engine burns. This is called burn duration. The amount of propellant consumed per unit of time is called the burn rate.

The engine produces thrust. Thrust is measured in pounds-force or kilonewtons. Specific impulse, abbreviated Isp, measures how efficiently the engine uses propellant. Higher Isp means more efficient engine. Isp is measured in seconds. A good rocket engine might have Isp of three hundred seconds. That is a vocabulary phrase: "three hundred second Isp." Not "three hundred seconds of specific impulse." Say "Isp." Everyone knows what that means.

The nozzle is the part of the engine that directs the hot gases. The nozzle throat is the narrowest point. Gases accelerate through the nozzle and exit at very high velocity. The nozzle exit velocity is another crucial term.

In avionics, the field gets even more specialized. Avionics refers to all the electronic systems on a spacecraft or aircraft. An avionics suite is a package of integrated systems. There is guidance and navigation, abbreviated GN ampersand C. Guidance is which direction the vehicle goes. Navigation is where the vehicle is. Control is how to steer it.

The inertial measurement unit, or IMU, measures acceleration. Accelerometers inside the IMU detect motion. Gyroscopes detect rotation. Together, they tell the spacecraft where it is going.

For communications, there is the antenna. A transmitter sends signals. A receiver picks them up. The signal frequency is measured in gigahertz. Signal strength is measured in decibels.

In thermodynamics, you are dealing with heat. Thermal analysis studies how heat moves through the spacecraft. Radiative heat transfer is heat that moves through radiation. Conductive heat transfer is heat that moves through direct contact. Convective heat transfer is heat moved by moving fluid or gas.

Thermal protection systems, or TPS, are the materials that protect the spacecraft from extreme heat. During reentry, the outside of the spacecraft reaches temperatures over three thousand Kelvin. That is hot enough to melt most metals. The TPS material ablates, meaning it slowly burns away and carries heat away from the spacecraft.

In orbital mechanics, you are dealing with orbits. A circular orbit is an orbit where the altitude stays the same. An elliptical orbit is an orbit where the altitude changes. The point closest to Earth is called the perigee. The point farthest from Earth is called the apogee. The height of the orbit is called the altitude.

Orbital velocity is the speed needed to maintain orbit without falling to Earth. At lower altitudes, orbital velocity is faster. At higher altitudes, orbital velocity is slower. For the International Space Station at about four hundred kilometers altitude, orbital velocity is about seven point seven kilometers per second.

A geosynchronous orbit is an orbit where the spacecraft stays above the same point on Earth. The orbital period equals Earth's rotation period, which is twenty-four hours. Many communication satellites are in geosynchronous orbit.

In aerodynamics, you are dealing with air. The fuselage is the main body of the aircraft. The wings generate lift. Drag is air resistance. The center of pressure is the point where all aerodynamic forces act. The center of gravity is where the weight is concentrated. If the center of pressure is behind the center of gravity, the aircraft is unstable. If it is ahead, the aircraft is stable.

Mach number is the speed relative to the speed of sound. Mach 1 is the speed of sound. Mach 2 is twice the speed of sound. In thick air near sea level, the speed of sound is about 340 meters per second. At high altitude where air is thin, sound does not travel far.

Structural analysis is about materials and stress. Stress is force divided by area. Strain is how much a material deforms under stress. The yield strength is the stress at which the material starts to permanently deform. The ultimate strength is the stress at which the material breaks.

Young's modulus is a measure of how stiff a material is. Density is mass per unit volume. Titanium is used in aerospace because it has low density and high strength. We say titanium has a high strength-to-weight ratio.

Composite materials are made of two materials mixed together. Carbon fiber composite is carbon fibers embedded in a resin. Composite materials are lighter than metals and nearly as strong.

Now, the mistake that Spanish speakers make. You see words like "presión" and "presion" and think they are the same. They are not. "Presión" in Spanish is "pressure" in English. That is a cognate. That part is fine. But then you hear "pressure vessel" and you think it means "vasija de presión." No. A pressure vessel is a container that holds high-pressure fluid. In Spanish aerospace, it might be called a "tanque de presión" or "recipiente a presión." You need to memorize which English words go together.

Another mistake. You learn the word "embarazada" which sounds like "embarrassed." They are not the same. "Embarazada" means pregnant. "Embarrassed" means feeling ashamed or self-conscious. If you say you are embarrassed to present your work, no one will think you are pregnant. But you need to know the difference.

Or "constipated." In Spanish it is "constipado," which means you have a cold. In English, "constipated" means your digestive system is not working. These false cognates will trip you up if you do not study them.

The TOEFL exam tests whether you know general English. It does not test aerospace vocabulary. But the IELTS Academic exam, especially in the writing and speaking sections, can include technical content. You need aerospace vocabulary for your actual job, not just for the exam.

TOEFL has four sections. Reading, listening, speaking, writing. Each section is scored from zero to thirty. Total score is zero to one hundred twenty. The reading section has three to four passages of about seven hundred words each. You have sixty minutes to read all passages and answer questions. The questions test whether you understand main ideas, details, inference, and vocabulary.

The listening section has three to four lectures and two to three conversations. Each lecture is about five minutes. Each conversation is about three minutes. You listen once. You cannot replay. You take notes and answer questions. The listening section is eighteen to twenty minutes of audio with about twelve to sixteen minutes of time to answer questions.

The speaking section has four tasks. Two independent speaking tasks where you speak about yourself and your opinions. Two integrated speaking tasks where you listen to audio and then speak about what you heard. Each response is about sixty seconds. You speak into a microphone and your response is recorded.

The writing section has two tasks. One integrated writing task where you read a passage, listen to a lecture, and write about the relationship. The response should be one hundred fifty to two hundred twenty-five words. One independent writing task where you write an essay about your opinion on a topic. The response should be three hundred words or more. You have fifty minutes total for both writing tasks.

IELTS is organized differently. It has listening, reading, writing, and speaking. Listening is forty minutes with ten recorded audio sections. Reading is sixty minutes with three sections and about forty questions total. Writing is sixty minutes with two tasks, one nine-hundred-character task and one four-hundred-word essay. Speaking is face-to-face with a test examiner. The speaking test is eleven to fourteen minutes divided into three parts.

For aerospace engineers, IELTS might feel slightly more familiar because the speaking is conversational rather than recorded. But TOEFL is more common in the United States. If you are applying to NASA or SpaceX or going to graduate school at MIT or Stanford, TOEFL is the safer choice.

The secret to scoring high is not being perfect. It is being consistent. In reading, you need to understand seventy to eighty percent of the passage to answer the questions correctly. In listening, you need to understand seventy to eighty percent of what you hear. In speaking, you need to be clear and organized, not perfect. Native speakers are not perfect in the TOEFL exam either.

Start your vocabulary sprint now. You have six weeks. Week one, learn terms one through fifteen in all seven categories. Week two, terms sixteen through thirty. By week six, you have learned all one hundred terms and you have seen each one multiple times in Anki through spaced repetition. That is how you remember. Not by cramming. By consistent review over time.

Build flashcards. On the front, the English term with pronunciation. On the back, the Spanish equivalent, a one-line definition, and an example sentence from a real aerospace document. Review every day. Your future self will thank you.

100 Core Aerospace Terms: Full Vocabulary Table

Master these terms in both English and Spanish. Use spaced repetition daily. These are the words you will encounter in technical meetings, specifications, and interviews.

Category English Term Spanish Term Definition Example Usage
Propulsion Systems (15 terms)
Propulsion Propellant Propelente Fuel and oxidizer mixture burned in a rocket engine "Liquid propellant is stored under pressure."
Propulsion Thrust Empuje Force produced by a rocket engine, measured in Newtons or pounds-force "The engine produces five hundred thousand Newtons of thrust."
Propulsion Specific Impulse (Isp) Impulso Específico Measure of engine efficiency; higher Isp equals better fuel utilization "The Merlin engine has an Isp of three hundred twenty seconds."
Propulsion Combustion Chamber Cámara de Combustión The enclosed space where propellant burns in a rocket engine "Combustion chamber temperatures exceed three thousand Kelvin."
Propulsion Nozzle Tobera The opening that directs hot gases from the engine; includes throat and exit "The nozzle design determines exhaust velocity."
Propulsion Nozzle Throat Garganta de Tobera The narrowest point of the nozzle where gases accelerate to sonic speed "Throat erosion limits engine life."
Propulsion Nozzle Exit Velocity Velocidad de Salida de Tobera Speed at which gases leave the engine; critical for thrust calculation "Higher exit velocity improves Isp."
Propulsion Propellant Grain Grano de Propelente A solid block of propellant used in solid rocket motors "The propellant grain determines burn duration and thrust profile."
Propulsion Burn Duration Duración de Quemado Time period during which a rocket engine actively produces thrust "We calculated a burn duration of two hundred eighty seconds."
Propulsion Burn Rate Velocidad de Quemado Amount of propellant consumed per unit time during engine operation "The burn rate affects payload capacity."
Propulsion Gimbal Cardan A mounting system that allows the engine to rotate for thrust vector control "The engine gimbal adjusts thrust direction."
Propulsion Turbopump Turbobomba A turbine-driven pump that feeds pressurized propellant to the combustion chamber "The turbopump is the most complex component in liquid-fueled rockets."
Propulsion Injector Plate Placa Inyectora The part that sprays propellant into the combustion chamber in a controlled pattern "Injector plate design affects mixing efficiency."
Propulsion Chamber Pressure Presión de Cámara The pressure inside the combustion chamber during firing "Higher chamber pressure increases thrust."
Propulsion Exhaust Velocity Velocidad de Escape Speed at which exhaust gases leave the engine "Exhaust velocity directly impacts mission efficiency."
Avionics and Electronics (12 terms)
Avionics Avionics Suite Suite Aviónica Integrated package of electronic systems for control, navigation and communication "The avionics suite includes redundant computing systems."
Avionics Guidance, Navigation, and Control (GN ampersand C) Guía, Navegación y Control The integrated system that determines direction, position, and attitude control "GN ampersand C failures are mission-critical."
Avionics Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) Unidad de Medición Inercial Sensor package containing accelerometers and gyroscopes "The IMU provides real-time acceleration and rotation data."
Avionics Accelerometer Acelerómetro Device that measures acceleration in three dimensions "Accelerometers detect vehicle motion."
Avionics Gyroscope Giroscopio Device that measures rotation rate and orientation "Ring laser gyroscopes provide high-precision rotation sensing."
Avionics Antenna Antena Device that transmits or receives electromagnetic signals "The communication antenna operates in X-band frequency."
Avionics Transmitter Transmisor Device that sends electromagnetic signals "The transmitter power is rated at fifty watts."
Avionics Receiver Receptor Device that receives and decodes electromagnetic signals "The receiver has a sensitivity of minus one hundred twenty-five decibels."
Avionics Signal Frequency Frecuencia de Señal Number of electromagnetic cycles per second, measured in Hertz or Gigahertz "Our system operates at ten point two Gigahertz."
Avionics Signal Strength (RSSI) Potencia de Señal Received signal strength indicator, measured in decibels "Signal strength degraded below acceptable limits."
Avionics Flight Computer Computadora de Vuelo The primary computing system that manages all spacecraft functions "The flight computer performs all real-time control calculations."
Avionics Redundancy Redundancia Having backup systems in case of primary system failure "Critical systems require triple redundancy."
Thermodynamics and Thermal Protection (12 terms)
Thermal Thermal Analysis Análisis Térmico Mathematical and physical study of heat flow through spacecraft systems "Thermal analysis predicted maximum temperatures."
Thermal Radiative Heat Transfer Transferencia de Calor Radiativa Heat movement through electromagnetic radiation, not requiring contact "Radiative cooling occurs in the vacuum of space."
Thermal Conductive Heat Transfer Transferencia de Calor Conductiva Heat movement through direct contact between materials "Conductive coupling links spacecraft components thermally."
Thermal Convective Heat Transfer Transferencia de Calor Convectiva Heat movement via flowing fluids or gases "Convective cooling cools the engine during powered flight."
Thermal Thermal Protection System (TPS) Sistema de Protección Térmica Materials and coatings that shield spacecraft from extreme heat "The TPS ablates and carries heat away during reentry."
Thermal Ablation Ablación Controlled erosion of material that removes heat through sublimation "Ablative materials protect the spacecraft from reentry heating."
Thermal Reentry Reentrada The process of a spacecraft returning from orbit through atmosphere "Reentry temperatures exceed three thousand Kelvin on the exterior."
Thermal Temperature Gradient Gradiente de Temperatura The rate of change of temperature across distance "Large temperature gradients create thermal stress."
Thermal Thermal Stress Estrés Térmico Mechanical stress caused by uneven heating or cooling "Thermal stress can cause material cracking."
Thermal Heat Sink Disipador de Calor A component designed to absorb and dissipate heat "The radiator acts as a heat sink for excess energy."
Thermal Radiator Radiador A thermal component that radiates excess heat to space "Spacecraft radiators reject waste heat to maintain operating temperature."
Thermal Insulation Aislamiento Material used to prevent heat transfer between components "Multi-layer insulation reduces thermal conduction."
Orbital Mechanics (13 terms)
Orbital Orbit Órbita The curved path a spacecraft follows around a celestial body "The spacecraft is in a circular orbit."
Orbital Circular Orbit Órbita Circular An orbit where altitude remains constant "The ISS maintains a circular orbit."
Orbital Elliptical Orbit Órbita Elíptica An orbit where altitude varies, with perigee and apogee "The transfer orbit is elliptical."
Orbital Perigee Perigeo The point in orbit closest to Earth "The perigee altitude is one hundred ninety kilometers."
Orbital Apogee Apogeo The point in orbit farthest from Earth "The apogee altitude is thirty-five thousand seven hundred eighty-six kilometers for geostationary orbit."
Orbital Altitude Altitud Height above Earth's surface "The ISS orbits at approximately four hundred twenty kilometers altitude."
Orbital Orbital Velocity Velocidad Orbital Speed required to maintain orbit without falling or escaping "Orbital velocity at ISS altitude is seven point seven kilometers per second."
Orbital Escape Velocity Velocidad de Escape Speed needed to break free from orbital gravity and escape to space "Earth's escape velocity is eleven point two kilometers per second."
Orbital Orbital Period Periodo Orbital The time required to complete one full orbit "The ISS has an orbital period of ninety minutes."
Orbital Geosynchronous Orbit Órbita Geosincrónica An orbit where the satellite stays above the same Earth location, with a twenty-four hour period "Communication satellites operate in geosynchronous orbit."
Orbital Geostationary Orbit Órbita Geoestacionaria A special geosynchronous orbit where inclination is zero, appearing stationary above the equator "The satellite maintains geostationary position."
Orbital Delta-v Delta-v The total change in velocity required to perform a maneuver, measured in meters per second "The lunar transfer requires a delta-v of three point one kilometers per second."
Orbital Hohmann Transfer Transferencia de Hohmann An efficient orbital maneuver using two engine burns to transfer between circular orbits "The spacecraft uses a Hohmann transfer to reach geostationary orbit."
Aerodynamics (15 terms)
Aero Fuselage Fuselaje The main body of an aircraft or launch vehicle "The fuselage houses fuel, payload, and avionics."
Aero Wing Ala The aerodynamic surface that generates lift "Wing design determines aircraft efficiency."
Aero Lift Sustentación The upward aerodynamic force perpendicular to airflow "Lift balances weight in steady flight."
Aero Drag Resistencia Aerodinámica The aerodynamic force opposing motion through air "Minimizing drag improves fuel efficiency."
Aero Center of Pressure Centro de Presión The point where all aerodynamic forces act "The center of pressure moves with speed and altitude."
Aero Center of Gravity Centro de Gravedad The point where all weight is concentrated "Center of gravity must remain forward of center of pressure for stability."
Aero Aerodynamic Stability Estabilidad Aerodinámica The vehicle's natural tendency to maintain stable flight path "Aerodynamic stability is critical for launch vehicle control."
Aero Mach Number Número de Mach Speed relative to local speed of sound (Mach 1 is sonic speed) "The aircraft climbs to Mach 2."
Aero Speed of Sound Velocidad del Sonido The speed at which sound waves propagate through air, approximately 340 m/s at sea level "Speed of sound decreases at higher altitude."
Aero Supersonic Supersónico Flying faster than the speed of sound (Mach 1 or greater) "Supersonic flight generates shock waves."
Aero Transonic Transónico Flying near the speed of sound, typically Mach 0.8 to 1.2 "Transonic flow creates complex shock wave patterns."
Aero Hypersonic Hipersónico Flying at very high supersonic speeds, typically Mach 5 or greater "Hypersonic reentry requires special thermal protection."
Aero Boundary Layer Capa Límite The thin layer of fluid near a surface where viscous effects are dominant "Boundary layer separation causes flow instability."
Aero Stall Pérdida A loss of lift when airflow separates from a wing at high angle of attack "The aircraft must avoid stalling during climb."
Aero Reynolds Number Número de Reynolds A dimensionless number characterizing the ratio of inertial to viscous forces in fluid flow "High Reynolds number flow occurs in real aircraft operations."
Structures and Materials (16 terms)
Struct Stress Estrés Internal force per unit area (Force divided by Area) "Material stress exceeds yield strength."
Struct Strain Deformación Relative deformation of a material under stress (Change in length divided by original length) "Strain measurements indicate material damage."
Struct Yield Strength Límite Elástico The stress level at which a material begins permanent deformation "Aluminum yield strength is limited by design stress."
Struct Ultimate Strength Resistencia Última The maximum stress a material can withstand before breaking "Ultimate strength determines structural safety margins."
Struct Young's Modulus Módulo de Young A measure of material stiffness (stress divided by strain in the elastic region) "Titanium has a high Young's modulus."
Struct Density Densidad Mass per unit volume of a material "Aluminum density is three grams per cubic centimeter."
Struct Strength-to-Weight Ratio Relación Resistencia Peso Material strength divided by material density; higher is better for aerospace "Titanium has an excellent strength-to-weight ratio."
Struct Fatigue Fatiga Progressive material failure under repeated loading cycles "Fatigue analysis is critical for rocket motor cases."
Struct Fracture Mechanics Mecánica de Fractura The study of crack growth and material failure under stress "Fracture mechanics predicts structural failure."
Struct Composite Material Material Compuesto A material made from two or more constituent materials with different properties "Carbon fiber composites are used throughout the spacecraft."
Struct Carbon Fiber Fibra de Carbono Strong, lightweight fibers made of carbon, typically embedded in resin "Carbon fiber reduces structural mass significantly."
Struct Titanium Alloy Aleación de Titanio A metal alloy based on titanium, combining strength and lightness "Titanium alloys withstand high temperatures."
Struct Aluminum Alloy Aleación de Aluminio A metal alloy based on aluminum, lightweight but less strong than titanium "Aluminum alloys are used for non-critical structures."
Struct Buckling Pandeo Sudden lateral instability of a structural member under compression "Column buckling analysis prevents structural collapse."
Struct Safety Factor Factor de Seguridad The ratio of material strength to applied stress; higher is safer "We design with a safety factor of 1.5 for primary structures."
Struct Vibration Analysis Análisis de Vibración Study of mechanical oscillations in structures "Vibration analysis predicts resonance frequencies."
Systems and Integration (17 terms)
Systems Subsystem Subsistema A major component or system that contributes to overall spacecraft function "Each subsystem has redundant components."
Systems Interface Interfaz The connection or boundary between two systems or components "The electrical interface specifies voltage and current requirements."
Systems Integration Integración The process of combining subsystems into a complete spacecraft "System integration testing verifies all subsystem interactions."
Systems Margin Margen The difference between predicted performance and actual requirement, providing safety buffer "Power margin ensures adequate system reserves."
Systems Design Margin Margen de Diseño Built-in performance buffer in design beyond minimum requirement "Ten percent design margin is standard."
Systems Test Margin Margen de Prueba Performance buffer shown during ground testing beyond flight requirements "Test margin demonstrates design adequacy."
Systems Specification Especificación A detailed technical document defining requirements for a system or component "The specification defines maximum operating temperature."
Systems Requirements Requisitos The formal statements of what a system must do or how it must perform "Mission requirements drive all design decisions."
Systems Performance Rendimiento How well a system accomplishes its intended function "System performance exceeds specification."
Systems Reliability Confiabilidad The probability that a system operates without failure for a specified duration "Reliability exceeds ninety-nine point nine percent."
Systems Failure Mode Modo de Fallo The specific way a component or system can fail "Critical failure modes are eliminated by design."
Systems Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) Análisis de Modo de Fallo y Efectos A systematic method for identifying potential failures and their consequences "FMEA identified critical thermal protection gaps."
Systems Load Path Ruta de Carga The structural path through which forces are transmitted "Proper load path design ensures structural integrity."
Systems Mass Budget Presupuesto de Masa The allocation of total spacecraft mass to subsystems "Mass budget constraints drive lightweight design."
Systems Power Budget Presupuesto de Potencia The allocation of total electrical power to subsystems "Power budget allocation limits transmitter duty cycle."
Systems Thermal Budget Presupuesto Térmico The allocation of total heat dissipation capability to subsystems "Thermal budget determines radiator size."
Systems Mission Operations Operaciones de Misión The real-time command and monitoring of spacecraft during its mission "Mission operations centers manage spacecraft 24/7."

Note: The 100 terms above are organized into seven categories. Use this as your core vocabulary checklist. Master all pronunciation. Create Anki flashcards for each. Review daily with spaced repetition.

False Cognates and Common Mistakes for Spanish Speakers

Spanish Word Meaning in Spanish English False Cognate Actual English Meaning Correct English in Aerospace
Constipado/a Having a cold or runny nose Constipated Unable to have bowel movements Use "have a cold" or "have a runny nose"
Embarazada Pregnant Embarrassed Feeling ashamed or self-conscious "I was embarrassed to present." Use for shame, not pregnancy.
Actualmente Currently, at the present time Actually In fact, really (contradicting expectation) "Currently we are testing" not "Actually we are testing" in formal contexts
Éxito Success Suceso An event or occurrence "The mission was a success." Use "success" for achievement.
Realizar To carry out, accomplish, perform Realize To become aware of, understand "We performed a test" not "We realized a test." "We realized the problem" means we became aware of it.
Sensible Sensitive, perceptive Sensible Reasonable, practical, showing good sense "A sensible approach" = reasonable approach. "A sensitive sensor" = a perceptive sensor.
Soportar To tolerate, endure, bear Support To help, hold up, or provide backing "Support the structure" (hold it up). "Tolerate high temperature" (bear it).
Aplicación Application, use Application Also application, but in aerospace refers to uses of technology Usually cognates correctly. "Aerospace applications" = spacecraft uses of technology.
Fábrica Factory Fabric Cloth or material woven from fibers "Made at the factory." Avoid "fabricated" when you mean "manufactured."
Introducción Introduction Introduction Also introduction, but sometimes in Spanish "introducción" can mean "insertion" "Introduction to the topic" (standard). "Insertion of fuel" (less common in aerospace).

TOEFL iBT Structure and Scoring

Total Score Range: 0-120 points

Test Duration: Approximately 3 hours (including breaks)

Section breakdown:

  • Reading: 3-4 passages, 10-14 questions per passage. Total 60 minutes. Score range: 0-30.
  • Listening: 3-4 lectures, 2-3 conversations. Total 60-90 minutes of audio with questions. Score range: 0-30.
  • Speaking: 4 tasks (2 independent, 2 integrated). 20 minutes total. Responses recorded. Score range: 0-30.
  • Writing: 2 tasks (1 integrated, 1 independent). 50 minutes total. Score range: 0-30.

Scoring interpretation:

  • 120: Perfect score (rare)
  • 100-120: Strong candidate for top universities and NASA
  • 80-99: Acceptable for most US universities and employers
  • 60-79: Basic English competency, may need support
  • Below 60: Significant gaps in English proficiency

IELTS Academic Structure and Scoring

Total Score Range: 1-9 bands (0.5 band increments)

Test Duration: Approximately 2 hours 45 minutes

Section breakdown:

  • Listening: 10 recorded sections, 40 questions. 30 minutes plus 10 minutes transfer time. Score: 0-40 raw, converted to 1-9 band.
  • Reading (Academic): 3 passages, 40 questions. 60 minutes. Score: 0-40 raw, converted to 1-9 band.
  • Writing: 2 tasks (150-word description, 250-word essay). 60 minutes. Score: 1-9 band based on task achievement, coherence, lexical range, grammatical accuracy.
  • Speaking: 3 parts face-to-face with examiner. 11-14 minutes. Part 1 is introduction and familiar topics (4-5 min). Part 2 is a prepared topic (3-4 min). Part 3 is discussion (4-5 min). Score: 1-9 band.

Band score interpretation:

  • 8.5-9.0: Expert user. Fully operational command. Makes rare errors.
  • 7.5-8.0: Very good user. Operational command. Occasional errors or inappropriate usage.
  • 7.0: Good user. Operational command. Makes errors in unfamiliar situations. Generally maintains control.
  • 6.5: Competent user. Generally effective command. Occasional errors and inappropriate usage.
  • 6.0: Acceptable user. Generally effective command in familiar situations. Makes frequent errors.
  • Below 6.0: Limited proficiency for academic study or professional work.

Six-Week Vocabulary Sprint Schedule

Use this schedule to systematically master all 100 aerospace terms. Each week, you add 15-20 new terms to your Anki deck and review previous terms.

Week Terms to Master Category Focus Daily Review Target Weekly Milestone
1 1-15 (Propulsion basics) Thrust, propellant, combustion, nozzle 15 new cards + 0 review Understand rocket engine basic operation
2 16-35 (Avionics basics) IMU, sensors, communication, antenna 20 new cards + 15 review Understand spacecraft guidance and navigation
3 36-50 (Thermal basics) Heat transfer, TPS, ablation, radiators 15 new cards + 35 review Understand thermal protection and dissipation
4 51-65 (Orbital mechanics) Orbit, altitude, velocity, transfer 15 new cards + 50 review Understand orbital physics and delta-v
5 66-85 (Aerodynamics and structures) Lift, drag, stress, materials, composites 20 new cards + 65 review Understand structural analysis and aerodynamic forces
6 86-100 (Systems integration) Specifications, budgets, testing, margins 15 new cards + 85 review Master all 100 terms and pass practice quiz

Download: 100-Term Aerospace Vocabulary Anki Deck

  • Aerospace English 100 Anki Deck (APKG) - Pre-built deck with all 100 terms, pronunciation audio, definitions, and example sentences. Import into Anki and start reviewing immediately.
  • False Cognates Quick Reference (PDF) - Printable guide to Spanish-English word traps common in aerospace contexts.
  • TOEFL vs IELTS Comparison Chart (PDF) - Side-by-side breakdown of exam structure, timing, and scoring.
  • Exam Prep Checklist (PDF) - 6-week countdown with daily and weekly tasks to prepare for TOEFL/IELTS.

Community Discussion Prompt

Share your experience:

Which aerospace term in this lesson was completely new to you? Which category (propulsion, avionics, thermal, orbital, aerodynamics, structures, systems) is most relevant to your field? Have you fallen for a false cognate before? Share the story.

Knowledge Check Quiz

Q1: What does "specific impulse" (Isp) measure?

A) The speed of the rocket
B) How efficiently the engine uses propellant
C) The temperature inside the combustion chamber
D) The weight of the payload

Answer: B. Higher Isp means more efficient propellant usage. A typical high-performance engine has Isp around 300-450 seconds.

Q2: The point in an elliptical orbit closest to Earth is called?

A) Apogee
B) Perigee
C) Altitude
D) Velocity point

Answer: B. Perigee is closest (think "peri" = near). Apogee is farthest (think "apo" = away from).

Q3: What is a "thermal protection system" (TPS)?

A) The heating system inside the spacecraft
B) Materials that shield spacecraft from extreme heat during reentry
C) Radiators that cool the spacecraft
D) Insulation between fuel tanks

Answer: B. TPS materials ablate (burn away slowly) during reentry, carrying heat away from the spacecraft structure.

Q4: Which is a false cognate that trips up Spanish speakers?

A) Comunicación (communication)
B) Electricidad (electricity)
C) Actualmente (actually)
D) Tecnología (technology)

Answer: C. "Actualmente" in Spanish means "currently" or "at present." "Actually" in English means "in fact" or "really," contradicting expectation. These are not the same.

Q5: What is the total score range for TOEFL iBT?

A) 0-100
B) 0-120
C) 1-9
D) 0-90

Answer: B. TOEFL iBT total score ranges from 0-120 (four sections of 0-30 each). IELTS uses 1-9 band scale.

Q6: How long is the speaking section of TOEFL iBT?

A) 5 minutes
B) 10 minutes
C) 20 minutes
D) 30 minutes

Answer: C. 20 minutes total. Four tasks, with independent and integrated speaking. Each response is about 60 seconds recorded into a microphone.

Gamification and Progress Tracking

Watch Video Lesson: +50 XP
Master 10 Terms (Anki): +25 XP
Complete Knowledge Check: +30 XP
Community Discussion: +20 XP
Download Resources: +15 XP

Total Available This Lesson: 140 XP

Streak Bonus: Technical Fluency Badge

Complete all aerospace vocabulary cards in Anki by end of week and open up Technical Fluency badge. Earn 3x XP multiplier for 7 days. Aerospace vocabulary is your competitive edge.
