ENGLISH
← Previous: Lesson 2.2Course index

Lesson 2.3: Situation Scripts and Daily Speaking Practice

Module 2: English Rocket Fuel

Lesson Objectives

  • Master 10 real-world aerospace scenario scripts for professional situations
  • Build confidence in technical interviews, salary negotiations, and networking
  • Practice handling difficult questions and thinking on your feet in English
  • Create a 30-minute daily speaking practice routine you can sustain for life
  • Learn accent preservation principles from Franklin Chang-Díaz and other LATAM aerospace leaders

Video Lesson: 10 Scripts for Speaking Success in Aerospace

Aracely demonstrates each script, shares pronunciation tips, and reveals what hiring managers actually listen for (approximately 10 minutes).

Video Script (for Aracely)

Today I want to give you ten scripts. These are not memorized speeches. These are frameworks. You memorize the structure and the key phrases, and then you make them yours. You adapt them to your personality and your story. A script that sounds like you is a hundred times more powerful than a script that sounds like a robot.

Script One is your elevator pitch. Thirty seconds. You are in an elevator with someone who works at SpaceX or NASA or Blue Origin. They ask, "What do you do?" You have thirty seconds to make them interested. Not thirty minutes. Thirty seconds.

You say: "Hi, I am [your name]. I am an engineer with [your specialty, for example, structural analysis or avionics systems] with [number] years of experience in [your industry or projects]. I am particularly interested in [your specific area: propulsion systems, thermal analysis, mission control]. Right now, I am focused on [what you are working on or learning]. I think the biggest opportunity in aerospace right now is [your insight: increased LATAM participation, reusable rocket technology, miniaturized satellites]. What do you work on?" Notice the last sentence. You ask a question back. You are not giving a monologue. You are starting a conversation.

Script Two is the technical interview opener. An interviewer asks, "Tell us about a technical project you led." You have two to three minutes. Here is the structure: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Situation: describe the project context in one sentence. Task: what was the challenge or the goal. Action: what did you do. Result: what was the outcome. Quantify when you can. "Improved thermal efficiency by twelve percent." Not "made it better." Numbers are proof.

Script Three is handling the difficult question: "What is your biggest weakness?" Do not say you have no weaknesses. Do not say you work too hard or care too much. That is not true. What you do is name a real challenge you faced and show how you addressed it. For example: "Early in my career, I struggled with the American style of direct communication in meetings. In Peru, we are taught to be indirect, to soften criticism. Here, people expect you to say what you think directly. I realized this was limiting my impact. I took a communication course. I practiced speaking up in meetings. Now I am comfortable saying, 'I disagree, and here is why.' That is still growing."

Script Four is the salary negotiation script. This one is sensitive. You are offered a position. You want more money. You do not say, "I need more money." You say: "I am thrilled about this opportunity. The role and the team are exactly what I am looking for. The salary is slightly below market rate for this level of experience and this specialty. Based on [research: Glassdoor, PayScale, your network], engineers with my background and [X years] experience in [your specialty] in [this region] typically earn between [lower number] and [higher number]. I would like to propose [your number], which reflects my experience and the value I bring. Is there flexibility in the budget?" This is not demanding. This is data-driven and respectful.

Script Five is the networking conversation at a conference. You see someone interesting. You walk over. You do not say, "Can I talk to you?" You say: "Hi, I heard your talk on [topic] earlier. I found your point about [specific detail] really interesting because [your reason]. I work in [your area], and we face a similar challenge. Have you found solutions that work across different applications?" Notice you are offering something: you acknowledge their expertise. Then you create a connection. Then you ask a question. You give them something to talk about, not yourself.

Script Six is asking for mentorship. You identify someone you admire. You write an email or you ask in person: "I have been following your work in [their field]. Your approach to [specific achievement] inspired me. I am working on [your field] and I would value your perspective on [specific question]. Would you be open to a fifteen-minute call in the next month to discuss [topic]?" Notice you are specific. You are not asking for hours of their time. You are asking for fifteen minutes. You show you have done your homework.

Script Seven is the technical presentation introduction. You are presenting a project or a design at a meeting. You start: "Good morning. My name is [name]. I am here to present our analysis of [topic]. I will cover three things: [first thing], [second thing], [third thing]. At the end, we will have time for questions. This work was done with [teams]. The motivation for this work was [the problem we are solving]. Let us start with [first point]." You are telling them what you will tell them, then you tell them, then you tell them what you told them. It is repetition. It helps people follow.

Script Eight is handling the "I do not know" moment. You are in an interview or a technical meeting. Someone asks you a question and you have no idea. What do you do? You do not panic. You do not make up an answer. You say: "That is a great question. I do not have that specific data in front of me right now. But here is how I would approach finding that answer. First, I would [logical step one]. Second, I would [logical step two]. If you are interested, I can research this and come back to you with the answer by [date]." You have shown your thinking process. You have shown you are reliable. You are not perfect. You are competent.

Script Nine is the follow-up email after a meeting or interview. You write: "Thank you for taking the time to meet with me today. I especially appreciated your insights on [specific thing they said]. I was particularly interested in the question about [their concern]. After our conversation, I realized [something you thought of]. I think [your insight] could help address that. I would like to [specific next step: send more information, schedule a follow-up]. I am still very interested in [the position or collaboration]. Please let me know if there is anything else you need from me." You are not desperate. You are professional. You are proactive.

Script Ten is the daily standup or update at work. You keep it to one minute. You say: "Yesterday I completed [task one] and [task two]. Today I am working on [task three]. The blocker I am facing is [issue], and I am addressing it by [your approach]. I am on track for [deadline]." Concise. Clear. Honest. If you have a blocker, you name it. Your manager wants to know about blockers early, not surprises later.

Now, about accent. I want to tell you about Franklin Chang-Díaz. He is from Costa Rica. He arrived in the US at eighteen years old. He spoke only Spanish. He earned an MIT PhD. He flew seven Space Shuttle missions. He leads major programs at NASA. His accent is absolutely still Costa Rican. If you listen to him on videos, he has an accent. No one cares. He is clear. He is confident. He is articulate. He has ideas. That is what matters.

Your accent is your heritage. It is where you come from. It is your Lambayeque, your Argentina, your Colombia. Do not erase it. Just make sure people can understand you. Speak clearly. Speak confidently. Speak slowly when you are presenting. Especially in the first five minutes when people are adjusting to your accent. Then you can go faster.

Here is your daily 30-minute routine to maintain speaking fluency for life:

Minutes 0-10: Shadowing. Pick a five to ten-minute aerospace YouTube video. Listen once without pausing. Listen again and try to copy the speaker's intonation and accent. You are not trying to perform. You are trying to absorb.

Minutes 10-20: Speaking practice. Use your iTalki tutor or a Tandem partner. Or practice alone. Pick a scenario from these ten scripts and speak it out loud. Record yourself. Speak for five minutes straight on a topic from your field. Do not worry about perfection. Focus on fluency. Can you speak for five minutes without stopping? That is the goal.

Minutes 20-30: Technical reading and reflection. Read one aerospace article or technical blog post. Read it out loud. Then write a three to four-sentence summary in your own words. This helps you practice comprehension and written expression at the same time.

This thirty-minute routine can be done at five in the morning before work. It can be done at lunch. It can be done on the train home. It does not require a teacher or a class. It requires commitment. Do this every single day and in two months you will not recognize your English.

The scripts I gave you are your toolkit. You do not memorize them word-for-word. You memorize them enough that when you are nervous or under pressure, your brain can access the structure. The structure keeps you from fumbling. The structure gives you confidence. And confidence, in English or in life, is everything.

10 Professional Scripts for Aerospace Success

Each script below is a framework. Memorize the structure. Personalize with your details. Practice out loud until it feels natural, not rehearsed.

Script 1: The 30-Second Elevator Pitch

Context: You meet a hiring manager or colleague at a conference or event. They ask, "What do you do?"

Framework:

"Hi, I'm [name]. I'm a [job title] with [number] years of experience in [industry/field]. My specialty is [specific area: structural analysis, avionics, mission control]. Right now I'm focused on [current project or learning]. I think the biggest opportunity in aerospace is [your insight]. What brings you to [event/company]?"

Key principle: End with a question. Turn the monologue into a conversation.

Script 2: The Technical Interview Project Story (STAR Method)

Context: Interviewer says, "Tell me about a technical project you led."

Framework (2-3 minutes):

Situation: "I led a thermal analysis project for [spacecraft/system] in [year]."
Task: "The challenge was [specific technical problem]. We needed to [outcome goal]."
Action: "I [your role]. I used [tools/methods]. I coordinated with [teams/people]. We tested [what you tested]. I documented [what you documented]."
Result: "We achieved [specific results with numbers]. The system performed [better/as designed]. The project outcome was [impact]."

Key principle: Use numbers. "Improved efficiency by fifteen percent" is stronger than "improved efficiency."

Script 3: Answering "What is Your Biggest Weakness?"

Context: Classic interview question meant to see if you are self-aware.

Framework:

"Early in my career, [describe real challenge: I struggled with direct communication, I had weak skills in X, I avoided public speaking]. I realized this was limiting my impact. I [took a course, found a mentor, practiced deliberately]. Now I [show growth]. I still work on this because [it is a lifetime skill], but I have concrete evidence it is better: [example of improvement]."

Key principle: Show self-awareness, action, and growth. Not perfection.

Script 4: Salary Negotiation

Context: You have been offered a position. The salary is below market. You want to negotiate.

Framework:

"I'm excited about this role and the team. This opportunity aligns with my career goals. The salary offered is slightly below market rate for [my level and specialty] in [location]. According to [Glassdoor, PayScale, your research], engineers with my background typically earn between [lower number] and [higher number]. I propose [your target number], which reflects my experience and market value. Is there flexibility in the budget? I'm also open to discussing other compensation like signing bonus, relocation, or flexible schedule."

Key principle: Data, respect, and flexibility. Negotiation is normal. Companies expect it.

Script 5: Networking at Conferences

Context: You see someone interesting at an aerospace conference. You approach them.

Framework:

"Hi, I attended your presentation on [topic]. Your point about [specific detail] really resonated with me because [your reason]. I work in [your area], and we face a similar challenge with [related problem]. Have you found solutions that scale across different applications? I'd love to learn from your experience."

Key principle: Compliment their work specifically. Show a connection. Ask a question that invites them to share expertise.

Script 6: Requesting Mentorship

Context: You want a mentor. You write an email or approach them at an event.

Framework:

"I've followed your work in [their specialty]. Your approach to [specific achievement or project] really inspired me. I'm working in [your field], and I'd value your perspective on [specific question]. Would you have time for a fifteen-minute call in [timeframe] to discuss [topic]? I'm happy to work around your schedule. Thank you for considering this."

Key principle: Specific, respectful of their time, and appreciative of their expertise. Not demanding.

Script 7: Technical Presentation Introduction

Context: You are presenting technical work in a meeting or conference.

Framework (first 2 minutes):

"Good morning. I'm [name], [title/role]. Today I'm presenting our [project type] on [topic]. I'll cover three areas: first, [area one] and why it matters; second, [area two] and our approach; third, [area three] and our results. This work was done in collaboration with [teams]. The motivation for this work is [the problem we're solving]. Let's start with [first point]."

Key principle: Tell them what you will tell them. Tell them. Tell them what you told them. Repetition helps comprehension.

Script 8: Handling "I Don't Know"

Context: In an interview, meeting, or technical discussion, someone asks a question you cannot answer.

Framework:

"That's a great question. I don't have that specific information in front of me right now. But here's how I'd approach finding the answer. First, I'd [logical step one]. Second, I'd [logical step two]. If you're interested, I can research this and get back to you by [specific date]. Does that work?"

Key principle: Show your thinking. Show you're reliable. Admitting what you don't know is stronger than guessing.

Script 9: Follow-Up Email After Interview or Meeting

Context: You have just interviewed or met with someone important. You send a follow-up email within 24 hours.

Framework:

"Thank you for taking the time to meet with me today. I especially appreciated your insights on [specific thing they shared]. After our conversation, I was thinking about [their concern or question]. I believe [your insight] could help address that. I'd like to [specific action: send research, schedule a follow-up, provide more information]. I remain very interested in [the opportunity/collaboration]. Please let me know if you need anything else from me. Best, [your name]"

Key principle: Timely, thoughtful, and proactive. You show you were listening and you are adding value.

Script 10: Daily Standup or Status Update

Context: At work, you give a daily or weekly update in a team meeting. Keep it to one minute.

Framework:

"Yesterday I completed [task one and task two]. Today I'm working on [task three]. The blocker I'm facing is [specific issue]. I'm addressing it by [your approach, timeline]. I'm on track for [milestone/deadline]. Any questions?"

Key principle: Concise, honest, and proactive about blockers. Good communication builds trust.

The 30-Minute Daily Speaking Routine

Practice this routine every single day for life. It maintains fluency. It does not require a teacher. It requires commitment.

Time Block Activity Goal How It Works
0-10 min Shadowing Practice Maintain accent recognition and listening comprehension Find one 5-10 min aerospace video. Listen once without pausing. Listen again and copy speaker intonation. Do NOT pause. Do NOT translate.
10-20 min Speaking Practice Maintain fluency and confidence in spontaneous speaking Choose a scenario from the 10 scripts above OR pick an aerospace topic you know well. Speak for 5-10 minutes straight, no pauses. Record yourself. Listen back for clarity, not perfection.
20-30 min Technical Reading and Reflection Maintain reading comprehension and written expression Read one aerospace article, blog post, or technical abstract out loud (pronunciation practice). Write a 3-4 sentence summary in your own words. This combines reading, speaking, and writing in one activity.

Schedule flexibility: This routine can be done in the morning before work, during lunch, on a commute, or in the evening. The key is consistency, not timing. Missing one day is okay. Missing a week breaks the habit. Stay committed to the habit.

Franklin Chang-Díaz: Accent as Asset, Not Liability

Franklin Chang-Díaz arrived in the United States from Costa Rica at age 18, speaking only Spanish. He earned a PhD from MIT in applied plasma physics. He flew 7 Space Shuttle missions (tied for the most spaceflights by any person). He founded Ad Astra Rocket Company and leads major propulsion research. His Costa Rican accent is unchanged. Here is what you should learn from his example:

  • Accent preservation: Your accent is not a deficiency. It is evidence of your international background. It makes you memorable. Hiring managers hear it and think, "Here is someone who crossed the world to be here."
  • Clarity over Americanization: Chang-Díaz does not try to sound American. He speaks clearly. He speaks confidently. He speaks at a measured pace. That is what matters. You are not trying to hide where you come from. You are trying to communicate.
  • Expertise overcomes everything: Chang-Díaz is listened to because he is an expert in plasma propulsion. If you are an expert, your accent becomes irrelevant. The content is what matters.
  • Representation matters: Every time a Latino aerospace professional speaks with confidence and an accent, it opens the door for the next person. You are not just building your career. You are building a path for others.

Download: Situation Scripts and Daily Practice Templates

  • 10 Situation Scripts (PDF) - Printable versions of all scripts for reference and practice.
  • 30-Minute Daily Routine Tracker (XLSX) - Log your daily practice. Track consistency. Build accountability.
  • Interview Q&A Bank (PDF) - 30 common aerospace interview questions with framework responses.

Community Discussion Prompt

Share and practice:

Which of the 10 scripts scares you the most? Why? (Salary negotiation? Technical interview? Networking?) Pick one script and practice it out loud. Record yourself for 30 seconds. Share what you learned about your own English.

Knowledge Check Quiz

Q1: The STAR method for technical interviews stands for?

A) Smart, Technical, Analytical, Results
B) Situation, Task, Action, Result
C) Specific, Time-bound, Achievement, Reflection
D) Systems, Testing, Analysis, Reasoning

Answer: B. Situation (context), Task (goal), Action (what you did), Result (outcome with numbers when possible).

Q2: How long is the 30-minute daily speaking routine?

A) 15 minutes
B) 30 minutes
C) 1 hour
D) 2 hours

Answer: B. 30 minutes total: 10 min shadowing, 10 min speaking practice, 10 min technical reading. Daily consistency matters more than long sessions.

Q3: What is Franklin Chang-Díaz an example of?

A) How to lose your accent and become American
B) How accent does not prevent aerospace success if you are clear and expert
C) That you need an American accent to fly space shuttles
D) That Latinos cannot reach leadership in aerospace

Answer: B. Chang-Díaz arrived speaking only Spanish, earned MIT PhD, flew 7 space shuttle missions, founded rocket company, leads major research. His Costa Rican accent is unchanged. He proves expertise and clarity override accent.

Gamification: Speaking Streaks and Achievement Badges

Watch Video Lesson: +50 XP
Practice One Script (Recorded): +40 XP
Complete Knowledge Check: +30 XP
Daily 30-Min Practice (per day): +20 XP

Total Available This Lesson: 140 XP

Ultimate Streak: Speaking Master Badge

Complete 30 consecutive days of the 30-minute daily routine and earn Speaking Master. Your speaking confidence will transform. Open up 5x XP multiplier for next module. You are not just learning English. You are becoming a fluent aerospace professional.

Module 2 Complete. You are ready for Module 3: Code Your Future.

You have mastered the English foundations. Now you will learn the technical skills and coding languages that power modern aerospace engineering.

Module 3: Code Your Future
ENGLISH
← Previous: Lesson 2.3Course index

Lesson 3.1: Why Coding Matters + Python Fundamentals (Weeks 1-2)

Module 3: Code Your Future

Lesson Objectives

  • Understand why Python dominates aerospace engineering and mission control systems
  • Master Python fundamentals: variables, data types, control flow, functions, and collections
  • Build aerospace-themed exercises: orbital period calculator, rocket equation solver
  • Establish daily 90-minute study blocks and coding habits
  • Create your first GitHub repository and document your learning path

Why Python Powers Aerospace: The NASA and SpaceX Advantage

When I was working on the James Webb Space Telescope software operations team, I realized something fundamental: the telescope doesn't move without code. Every alignment correction, every thermal sensor reading, every command sequence that travels 1 million miles to JWST relies on Python tooling. At NASA Goddard, we use Python for telemetry analysis, mission control automation, and the data pipelines that process images from the deepest corners of the universe. SpaceX relies on Python for Starlink network management, launch sequence automation, and flight dynamics. Blue Origin uses Python for autonomous systems and propulsion modeling. AstroPy, the NASA community library, processes raw data from Hubble and JWST. Python isn't just popular in aerospace because it's trendy. It's the standard because it combines rapid prototyping, scientific computing, and the human ability to reason about code under pressure.

Tool/Project Organization Primary Use Case Why Python
AstroPy NASA / Astronomical Community Space telescope data analysis, calibration NumPy/SciPy integration, open-source standard
GMAT (General Mission Analysis Tool) NASA Goddard Space Flight Center Trajectory design, mission planning Python scripting for automation and custom workflows
OrbitPy Open-source aerospace community Orbital mechanics, satellite propagation Pure Python, lightweight, educational, maintainable
Starlink Network Stack SpaceX Satellite constellation optimization, routing Fast data processing, ML integration, rapid iteration
Flight Dynamics and Simulation Blue Origin Trajectory simulation, autonomous vehicle control Scientific computing ecosystem, NumPy/SciPy, reliability
Propellant and Structural Analysis Rocket Lab Material properties, tank stress analysis Pandas for data, Matplotlib for visualization, engineering workflows

Key insight: Learning Python is not a nice-to-have skill. It is the direct pathway into the tools and workflows you will use on your first day in any aerospace engineering role, whether at NASA, a commercial launch provider, or a satellite startup.

Weeks 1-2 Curriculum: From Zero to Functional Programmer

Week Core Topics Daily 90-Minute Study Block Aerospace Project
Week 1 Variables, data types, operators, input/output, conditionals (if/else), loops (for/while) 30 min video + 45 min coding exercises + 15 min reflection Rocket Equation Solver. Input thrust, mass, duration. Calculate acceleration using F=ma.
Week 2 Functions, lists, dictionaries, file I/O, error handling, standard library modules 30 min video + 45 min coding exercises + 15 min reflection Orbital Period Calculator. Input orbital altitude. Calculate period using Kepler's 3rd law.

Week 1 Deep Dive: Variables Through Loops

Day 1-2: Variables and Data Types Python treats everything as an object. Integers (whole numbers), floats (decimals), strings (text), booleans (True/False). Example:

thrust_newtons = 5_000_000  # Falcon 9 first stage thrust
mass_kg = 549_000  # First stage + fuel mass
time_seconds = 10  # Burn duration in seconds

# Calculate acceleration using F=ma
acceleration = thrust_newtons / mass_kg
distance = 0.5 * acceleration * (time_seconds ** 2)

print(f"Acceleration: {acceleration:.2f} m/s²")
print(f"Distance traveled: {distance:.0f} meters")

Day 3-4: Control Flow If statements branch logic. For loops iterate over sequences. While loops run until a condition is false.

# Check if altitude is safe for Stage 1 engine cutoff
altitude_meters = 68000

if altitude_meters > 60000:
    print("Max-Q passed. Stage separation safe.")
elif altitude_meters > 30000:
    print("Still in dense atmosphere. Reduce throttle.")
else:
    print("Too low. Continue ascent.")

Day 5-6: Data Structures Lists store ordered collections. Dictionaries store key-value pairs. Both are essential for organizing mission telemetry.

# Sensor readings from a satellite
sensor_data = [23.5, 24.1, 23.8, 24.3, 23.9]

# Calculate average temperature
avg_temp = sum(sensor_data) / len(sensor_data)
print(f"Average temperature: {avg_temp:.1f} C")

# Mission telemetry as a dictionary
spacecraft = {
    "name": "ISS Module A",
    "altitude_km": 408,
    "velocity_mps": 7660,
    "battery_pct": 87
}

print(spacecraft["name"])

Day 7-8: Functions Functions are reusable blocks of code. Write once, use anywhere. When I was building telemetry dashboards at Goddard, functions were how we avoided copying code 50 times.

def calculate_orbital_velocity(altitude_km):
    """
    Calculate orbital velocity for circular orbit.
    Uses: v = sqrt(GM / (R + h))
    where G = 6.674e-11, M = 5.972e24 (Earth mass), R = 6371 km
    """
    G = 6.674e-11  # Gravitational constant
    M = 5.972e24   # Earth mass in kg
    R = 6371000    # Earth radius in meters
    h = altitude_km * 1000  # Convert to meters

    v = (G * M / (R + h)) ** 0.5
    return v

# Test for ISS
iss_velocity = calculate_orbital_velocity(408)
print(f"ISS orbital velocity: {iss_velocity:.0f} m/s")

Week 2 Deep Dive: Building Toward Real Projects

Day 9-10: File I/O Aerospace engineers work with data files constantly. CSV logs from rocket tests, JSON configuration files, text telemetry dumps. Learn to read, parse, and write files cleanly.

import csv

# Read launch data
with open('launch_log.csv', 'r') as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:
        mission = row['mission_name']
        payload_kg = float(row['payload_mass_kg'])
        success = row['success']
        print(f"{mission}: {payload_kg} kg, Status: {success}")

Day 11-12: Error Handling Code fails. Networks drop. Sensors glitch. Real engineers write code that catches errors gracefully instead of crashing.

def get_altitude(sensor_reading):
    try:
        altitude = float(sensor_reading)
        if altitude < 0:
            raise ValueError("Altitude cannot be negative")
        return altitude
    except ValueError:
        print("Error: Invalid altitude reading. Using last known value.")
        return None

Day 13-14: Using Libraries Python's standard library (math, random, datetime) and scientific packages (NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib) solve 80% of aerospace problems. You do not need to reinvent algorithms.

import math

# Calculate escape velocity
def escape_velocity(body_mass_kg, body_radius_m):
    G = 6.674e-11
    return math.sqrt(2 * G * body_mass_kg / body_radius_m)

# Earth
earth_escape = escape_velocity(5.972e24, 6371000)
print(f"Earth escape velocity: {earth_escape:.0f} m/s")

# Moon (lighter, smaller)
moon_escape = escape_velocity(7.342e22, 1737400)
print(f"Moon escape velocity: {moon_escape:.0f} m/s")

Learning Resources for Weeks 1-2

Resource Format Cost Why It Works
Real Python Interactive tutorials, videos Free tier available Industry-standard tutorials written by engineers. Clear, practical examples.
CS50P (Harvard) Video lectures, problem sets Free (edX) Rigorous computer science fundamentals taught by David Malan. Aerospace engineers appreciate the depth.
NASA Open MOOC Online courses Free NASA instructors. Python for satellite operations, data analysis, automation.
Automate the Boring Stuff with Python Book + free online Free online Teaches practical automation. Every aerospace engineer automates repetitive tasks.

Video Lesson: Python in Aerospace Mission Control

Aracely's 900-word video script (10 minutes)

Hi, I'm Aracely. I work on the James Webb Space Telescope at NASA Goddard. Today, I want to tell you why learning Python changed everything about how I do my job and why it will change yours, too.

I grew up in a small town in northern Peru called Marripón. My grandmother raised me to work hard and ask questions. I trained in karate as a girl, and I learned that discipline compounds. Every day I showed up, I got a little stronger. Every technique I practiced, I understood a little deeper. That is how coding works. You do not need to be brilliant. You need to show up, write code every single day, and trust the compound effect.

When I first came to the United States, I taught myself engineering from textbooks and online courses. But Python was different. Python let me automate the tedious parts of my job. I could write a script in 30 minutes that would have taken me three hours to do by hand. As I advanced, I started writing tools that other engineers used. That is power. That is use. That is why Python matters.

Let me give you a concrete example. At Goddard, we have telemetry coming in from multiple satellites every second. Thousands of sensor readings. We need to check those readings in real time: Is the temperature in normal range? Is the battery charging? Are we seeing anomalies? A human cannot do this at speed. A spreadsheet crashes. But a Python script? A Python script can monitor 10,000 data points per second and alert you the instant something goes wrong. That script keeps multimillion-dollar satellites healthy.

Here is the thing that inspires me about your generation: You have access to tools that I had to hunt for 15 years ago. You can learn Python for free. You can access NASA's own data, SpaceX's API, real-time satellite tracking. You can build projects that matter while you are still learning. When I was your age, I could only read about these things. You can code them.

I want you to think about the engineers at Blue Origin working on New Shepard. The engineers at Rocket Lab launching daily. The engineers at Satellogic in Argentina building a constellation of nanosatellites. Every single one of them writes Python. Not because they love programming in the abstract. Because Python is the language that connects human intention to machine action. It is how you tell a rocket what to do. It is how you tell a satellite where to point. It is how you know if anything is going wrong.

Katya Echazarreta, who was born in Mexico and studied electrical engineering at Cal State Long Beach, worked at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory writing software for the Europa Clipper and Perseverance rover instruments. She built real code that will run on missions to find life beyond Earth. Then she became an astronaut. Her path started with Python.

The Tobacia brothers from Colombia, they both studied aerospace engineering and moved to the United States. They are engineers at Blue Origin right now. They wrote their first Python scripts as students, just like you are about to do. Now they are writing code that powers rockets.

I want you to understand something essential: This course is not about becoming a professional programmer. It is about becoming an engineer who thinks computationally. It is about seeing a problem, writing a tool, and testing it. It is about collaborating with other engineers using GitHub. It is about building things that matter.

Over the next two weeks, you will write a rocket equation solver. You will write an orbital period calculator using Kepler's third law. These are not toy problems. These are the actual calculations that mission planners use. When you finish, you will have code on your GitHub that a recruiter at SpaceX or Blue Origin or a satellite startup will look at and recognize immediately: "This person understands the fundamentals. This person can think in code."

My grandmother used to say in Spanish, "Nunca dejes que nadie te diga que no puedes" (Never let anyone tell you that you cannot). That applies to coding. Do not let anyone tell you that you are not a programmer. If you can articulate a problem, if you can break it down into steps, if you are willing to debug and iterate, you are a programmer. The only thing standing between you and the aerospace industry is 100 hours of dedicated practice.

So here is my challenge to you: Commit to these two weeks. Code every single day for 90 minutes. Do the exercises. Read the error messages. They are trying to help you. Build the rocket solver. Build the orbital calculator. Then push it to GitHub. Write a README that explains what you built and why. That is the beginning of your portfolio.

At the end of this module, you will be ready for intermediate Python, APIs, data analysis. You will be ready to work with real mission data. You will be ready to compete for roles at the organizations that are building the future of space exploration. But first, you have to show up every day and write code. That is where every engineer at NASA, SpaceX, Blue Origin started. That is where you start too.

Let us code your future together.

Download: Python Starter Kit and 30 Coding Challenges

aqn_python_fundamentals_checklist.pdf (4 pages)

Covers: variables, data types, operators, conditionals, loops, functions, lists, dictionaries, file I/O, error handling. Check off each concept as you master it. Track your 90-minute study blocks daily.

Community Discussion

Reflection Prompt for Your Learning Group: Share your first Python script with your cohort. What problem did you solve? What error did you encounter first, and how did you debug it? Why does that error message matter?

Quiz: Weeks 1-2 Fundamentals

Answer all 6 questions. Score 5 or higher to open up Lesson 3.2.

  1. Write a Python function that calculates the acceleration of a rocket given thrust (Newtons), total mass (kg), and returns the result in m/s². Your function should include a docstring explaining the formula.
  2. Create a list of five orbital altitudes (in km). Write a loop that converts each to meters and prints "Altitude X: Y meters" for each.
  3. Write an if/elif/else statement that takes a velocity value and prints: "Suborbit" if under 7,000 m/s, "Orbit" if 7,000-11,000 m/s, "Escape velocity" if 11,000+ m/s.
  4. Create a dictionary representing a spacecraft with keys: name, altitude_km, fuel_pct. Write a function that takes this dictionary and returns a status string like "ISS at 408 km, fuel 87 percent".
  5. Write a try/except block that reads a CSV file named "launch_data.csv" and handles two errors: FileNotFoundError and ValueError (if a row cannot be parsed). Print appropriate error messages.
  6. Using the math library, calculate the escape velocity of Mars (mass: 6.4171e23 kg, radius: 3.3895e6 m). Compare it to Earth's escape velocity. Print both values.

Answer Key and Explanations

Question 1 (Function with docstring): Correct answer includes a docstring, uses F=ma formula correctly, and returns a numeric result.

def calculate_acceleration(thrust_n, mass_kg):
    """Calculate rocket acceleration using F=ma."""
    return thrust_n / mass_kg

accel = calculate_acceleration(5_000_000, 549_000)
print(f"Acceleration: {accel:.2f} m/s²")

Question 2 (List and loop): Must create a list, iterate with a for loop, convert km to m (multiply by 1000), and use print with formatted string.

Question 3 (Conditional logic): Must use if/elif/else, not nested if statements. Boundary values 7,000 and 11,000 should be tested accurately. This mirrors real mission planning thresholds.

Question 4 (Dictionary and function): Function must accept a dictionary argument, access keys using bracket notation, and return a formatted string. Tests understanding of data structures and function parameters.

Question 5 (Error handling): Must include try/except, catch FileNotFoundError (file missing), and ValueError (parsing error). Demonstrate graceful failure.

Question 6 (Math library and calculation): Use math.sqrt(). Mars escape velocity approximately 5,027 m/s. Earth approximately 11,186 m/s. You should recognize Mars is lower gravity despite similar size because it is less dense.

Next: Lesson 3.2 (Intermediate Python + APIs)

In Lesson 3.2, you will master Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) by building a working Rocket class with real delta-v calculations. You will learn NumPy and Pandas to analyze real ISS telemetry data. You will connect to live NASA APIs and pull satellite tracking data. You will plot orbital trajectories using Matplotlib. By the end of Week 6, you will have built five mini-projects and are ready for the capstone: ISS Pass Predictor.

ENGLISH
← Previous: Lesson 3.1Course index

Lesson 3.2: Intermediate Python + APIs (Weeks 3-6)

Module 3: Code Your Future

Lesson Objectives

  • Master Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) with a working Rocket class and delta-v calculations
  • Learn NumPy for array operations and scientific computing
  • Master Pandas for tabular data, ISS telemetry analysis, satellite track data
  • Master Matplotlib for plotting orbital trajectories, satellite passes, launch windows
  • Connect to real NASA, SpaceX, and satellite APIs. Query live data
  • Build five mini-projects with real mission data and real APIs
  • Set up a Jupyter notebook workflow for reproducible aerospace data analysis

Week 3: Object-Oriented Programming for Aerospace

OOP means organizing code into reusable objects. Instead of writing scattered functions, you define classes that bundle data and behavior together. In aerospace, this is how we think about systems: A spacecraft is an object with properties (mass, altitude, velocity, fuel) and behaviors (burn engines, communicate home, adjust attitude). A rocket is an object with thrust, mass, fuel, and the ability to calculate acceleration and delta-v.

The Rocket Class: A Working Example

class Rocket:
    """Represents a launch vehicle with thrust, mass, fuel capacity, and delta-v calculations."""

    def __init__(self, name, mass_kg, thrust_n, isp_seconds):
        """Initialize a rocket.

        Args:
            name: Rocket designation (e.g., "Falcon 9", "New Shepard")
            mass_kg: Dry mass (structure, engines, avionics) in kg
            thrust_n: Sea-level thrust in Newtons
            isp_seconds: Specific impulse in seconds (ISP is exhaust efficiency)
        """
        self.name = name
        self.mass_kg = mass_kg
        self.thrust_n = thrust_n
        self.isp_seconds = isp_seconds
        self.fuel_kg = 0
        self.altitude_m = 0
        self.velocity_mps = 0

    def add_fuel(self, fuel_kg):
        """Load fuel into the rocket."""
        self.fuel_kg = fuel_kg
        print(f"{self.name}: Loaded {fuel_kg} kg of propellant")

    def total_mass(self):
        """Total mass including fuel."""
        return self.mass_kg + self.fuel_kg

    def calculate_acceleration(self):
        """Calculate current acceleration using F=ma."""
        G = 9.81  # Earth surface gravity in m/s^2
        thrust_eff = self.thrust_n - (self.total_mass() * G)  # Subtract weight
        return thrust_eff / self.total_mass()

    def calculate_delta_v(self):
        """Calculate total delta-v available (exhaust velocity times ln(mass ratio)).

        Delta-v is the total velocity change the rocket can achieve from all fuel burns.
        Formula: dv = isp * g * ln(initial_mass / final_mass)
        where g = 9.81 m/s^2 (standard gravity)
        """
        import math
        G = 9.81
        exhaust_velocity = self.isp_seconds * G
        initial_mass = self.total_mass()
        final_mass = self.mass_kg  # After all fuel is burned

        if final_mass <= 0:
            return 0

        mass_ratio = initial_mass / final_mass
        delta_v = exhaust_velocity * math.log(mass_ratio)
        return delta_v

    def status(self):
        """Print rocket status."""
        print(f"\n{self.name} Status:")
        print(f"  Dry mass: {self.mass_kg} kg")
        print(f"  Fuel: {self.fuel_kg} kg")
        print(f"  Total mass: {self.total_mass()} kg")
        print(f"  Thrust: {self.thrust_n:,} N")
        print(f"  ISP: {self.isp_seconds} seconds")
        print(f"  Delta-v available: {self.calculate_delta_v():.0f} m/s")
        print(f"  Current acceleration: {self.calculate_acceleration():.2f} m/s²")

# Create a Falcon 9 first stage
falcon9 = Rocket("Falcon 9 F1", mass_kg=25_000, thrust_n=5_100_000, isp_seconds=282)
falcon9.add_fuel(385_000)
falcon9.status()

# Create New Shepard (suborbital)
shepard = Rocket("New Shepard", mass_kg=6_000, thrust_n=110_000, isp_seconds=210)
shepard.add_fuel(24_000)
shepard.status()

Week 4: NumPy and Pandas for Aerospace Data

NumPy provides fast arrays and mathematical operations. Pandas provides tabular data (DataFrames) which is how real telemetry is organized. Every aerospace engineer uses these libraries to crunch mission data.

Example: ISS Altitude Data Analysis with Pandas

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Sample ISS altitude data over time (real format from space agencies)
data = {
    'timestamp': ['2026-04-22 08:00', '2026-04-22 08:05', '2026-04-22 08:10', '2026-04-22 08:15'],
    'altitude_km': [408.2, 408.1, 407.9, 407.8],
    'velocity_mps': [7659, 7658, 7660, 7659],
    'temperature_c': [22.1, 22.0, 22.3, 22.1]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print(df)
print(f"\nAverage altitude: {df['altitude_km'].mean():.2f} km")
print(f"Min altitude: {df['altitude_km'].min():.2f} km")
print(f"Max velocity: {df['velocity_mps'].max()} m/s")

# Detect anomalies (altitude drops more than 0.2 km)
df['altitude_change'] = df['altitude_km'].diff()
anomalies = df[df['altitude_change'] < -0.2]
if len(anomalies) > 0:
    print("\nAnomalies detected (rapid altitude loss):")
    print(anomalies)

Week 5: REST APIs and Live Data

APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) let your code ask servers for data. NASA provides free APIs. SpaceX provides an API. N2YO provides real-time satellite tracking. Use the requests library to fetch data in JSON format, then parse it with Python.

API Organization Data Provided Python Example
NASA API NASA APOD (Astronomy Photo of Day), Mars rovers, exoplanets requests.get("api.nasa.gov/...?api_key=...")
SpaceX API SpaceX (community maintained) Launch history, rocket specs, missions requests.get("api.spacexdata.com/v4/launches")
N2YO Satellite Tracker N2YO Real-time TLE, satellite passes, positions requests.get("api.n2yo.com/rest/v1/satellite/...")
Open-Meteo Open-Meteo (no key needed) Weather, atmospheric data requests.get("api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast")
USGS Earthquake USGS Seismic activity (used to study Earth from orbit) requests.get("earthquake.usgs.gov/earthquakes/feed/v1.0/...")

Example: Query SpaceX Launch History

import requests
import json

# Query SpaceX API for recent launches (no auth required)
url = "https://api.spacexdata.com/v4/launches?limit=5&sort=-date_unix"
response = requests.get(url)
launches = response.json()

for launch in launches:
    name = launch.get('name', 'Unknown')
    date = launch.get('date_utc', 'Unknown')
    success = launch.get('success', None)
    status = "Success" if success else "Failure" if success is False else "Pending"
    print(f"{name} - {date[:10]} - {status}")

Week 6: Data Visualization with Matplotlib

Raw data is hard to understand. Plots tell the story. Plot orbital trajectories, satellite passes, launch windows, telemetry trends.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Plot an orbital trajectory
time = np.linspace(0, 90, 360)  # 90 minutes, every 15 seconds
altitude = 408 + 2 * np.sin(time / 90 * np.pi)  # Simulated altitude variation
velocity = 7660 - 10 * np.cos(time / 90 * np.pi)  # Simulated velocity

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(10, 6))

ax1.plot(time, altitude, 'b-', linewidth=2)
ax1.set_ylabel('Altitude (km)', fontsize=11)
ax1.set_title('ISS Orbital Profile (1 Orbit)', fontsize=13)
ax1.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

ax2.plot(time, velocity, 'r-', linewidth=2)
ax2.set_xlabel('Time (minutes)', fontsize=11)
ax2.set_ylabel('Velocity (m/s)', fontsize=11)
ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Capstone Project: ISS Pass Predictor

Specification

Build a command-line tool that takes your latitude/longitude and outputs when the ISS will pass overhead. Use N2YO API or NASA's Spot the Station API. Output: Rise time, max altitude, set time, brightness.

Requirements

  • Accept user input: city name or lat/lon coordinates
  • Query ISS position from an API
  • Calculate pass times and max elevation angle
  • Output formatted predictions: "ISS visible from Lima, Peru on 2026-04-23 at 19:34 UTC, max elevation 72 degrees"
  • Store results in a CSV file for future reference
  • Use Matplotlib to plot the ISS orbit and your location

Video Lesson: OOP and APIs in Practice

Aracely's 950-word video script (12 minutes)

Hi. This is Aracely. In these four weeks, you have moved from writing simple Python scripts to designing real aerospace software systems. That is the transition from student to engineer.

When I first started at NASA Goddard, I worked in telemetry. We were receiving thousands of data points per second from the James Webb Space Telescope. If you try to process that with lists and loops, your code is too slow. Your alert system lags. Your analysis takes hours. That is when I learned to use objects, NumPy, Pandas, and APIs. These tools are not optional in aerospace. They are survival.

Let me tell you about Emiliano Kargieman, who founded Satellogic in Argentina. Satellogic builds nanosatellites for Earth observation. They have launched over 50 satellites in a constellation. Every single one of those satellites generates gigabytes of imagery. Emiliano's engineers use Python with NumPy and Pandas every single day to process that data, detect changes on Earth, and deliver insights to customers. He started as an engineer. He taught himself Python through online courses and projects. He built a company.

Or consider the work being done at Blue Origin by engineers like the Tobacia brothers. They are writing code that controls autonomous rocket systems. They use OOP to model the rocket, the engines, the attitude control system, each as an object with properties and behaviors. Then they integrate those objects into a flight control software stack that runs in real time during launch. That software has to be correct, readable, and maintainable by a team. That is why they use Python classes and object-oriented design.

The ISS Pass Predictor you are building is not a toy. It is a real engineering problem. You are querying live satellite position data from an API. You are calculating geometry in three-dimensional space. You are outputting information that an actual astronomer or a satellite enthusiast would find useful. This is the kind of tool that space agencies, satellite operators, and commercial space companies build all the time.

When you finish this module, you will have written code that uses real data from real missions. You will have practiced OOP, NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib, and APIs. You will have built tools that do actual work. That is what separates a person who knows how to write code from a person who understands how to think like a software engineer.

My challenge to you: Do not just complete the ISS Pass Predictor. Extend it. Add a feature to show the ISS orbit visually. Add a feature to predict multiple days ahead. Add a feature to estimate the ISS's position in real time using TLE data. Push it to GitHub with a clean README. That is how you build a portfolio that recruiters recognize.

The future of space exploration is being written by engineers who can code. You are becoming one of them.

Download: OOP Starter Code and API Documentation

aqn_oop_rocket_class.pdf (3 pages, contains full Rocket class template with instructions)

aqn_iss_pass_predictor_spec.pdf (5 pages, ISS Pass Predictor full specification and starter code template)

Community Discussion

Reflection Prompt: Share a screenshot of your ISS Pass Predictor output showing a pass for your city or a city you choose. What was the most challenging part of the project? What feature would you add next?

Quiz: Weeks 3-6 Object-Oriented and API Programming

Answer all 7 questions. Score 6 or higher to open up Lesson 3.3.

  1. Create a Satellite class with properties: name, altitude_km, inclination_degrees, orbital_period_minutes. Write a method that returns the satellite's mean motion (revolutions per day). Use the formula: mean_motion = 1440 / orbital_period_minutes.
  2. Write a function that uses requests library to query the SpaceX API for the latest Falcon 9 launch. Parse the response JSON and print: launch name, launch date, success status.
  3. Create a pandas DataFrame with three columns: mission_name (strings), launch_date (dates), success (boolean). Filter the DataFrame to show only successful missions. Calculate the success rate (percentage).
  4. Write code using Matplotlib to plot two lines on the same graph: altitude (y-axis) vs time (x-axis) and velocity (y-axis, secondary) vs time (x-axis). Include legends and axis labels.
  5. Write a function that accepts a satellite's altitude_km and orbital_period_minutes, then uses NumPy to calculate the satellite's orbital velocity using v = 2*pi*R / T, where R is orbital radius and T is period in seconds. Test with ISS data.
  6. Explain in 3-4 sentences why we use classes in aerospace engineering software. How does a Rocket class help us model a real rocket?
  7. Design a mini-project idea: What data would you query from an API, what would you analyze, and what visualization would you create? (Write 5-7 sentences.)

Answer Key and Explanations

Question 1 (Satellite class): Class must have __init__ with four properties. Method must calculate mean motion correctly. Test with realistic values (ISS: altitude 408 km, period 90 minutes, should yield ~16 revolutions/day).

Question 2 (SpaceX API): Must use requests.get() with correct SpaceX API v4 endpoint. Parse JSON response correctly. Extract launch name, date_utc, success fields. Handle missing fields gracefully.

Question 3 (Pandas filtering): DataFrame must have correct column types. Filter using df[df['success'] == True]. Success rate = (count of True) / (total rows) * 100.

Question 4 (Matplotlib): Must create two y-axes using twinx(). Plot lines on each. Include legends, axis labels, title. Both lines visible without overlap.

Question 5 (NumPy orbital velocity): Formula v = 2*pi*R / T. R = Earth radius + altitude. T = orbital_period_minutes * 60. ISS example: v ~ 7660 m/s.

Question 6 (OOP explanation): Classes bundle data and behavior. Rocket class encapsulates mass, thrust, fuel, and behaviors like calculate_delta_v(). Makes code reusable and maintainable. Mirrors physical reality.

Question 7 (Mini-project design): Must identify a specific data source (API), analysis question, and visualization type. Examples: "Query NASA APOD API, classify images by content using Python, create a gallery HTML page." or "Get earthquake data from USGS, analyze epicenter locations, plot on a map."

Next: Lesson 3.3 (Portfolio Projects and GitHub Mastery)

In Lesson 3.3, you will learn GitHub as a professional tool, not just a storage box. You will design and build five portfolio projects with full technical specifications. You will learn the README format that recruiters actually read. You will understand how GitHub profiles are screened by companies like SpaceX and Blue Origin. By the end, your GitHub will demonstrate mastery of Python, APIs, data analysis, and version control. You will be ready to apply for junior aerospace engineer and data engineer roles.

ENGLISH
← Previous: Lesson 3.2Course index

Lesson 3.3: Portfolio Projects and GitHub Mastery

Module 3: Code Your Future

Lesson Objectives

  • Understand why GitHub is the #1 hiring signal in aerospace engineering
  • Design five portfolio projects with full technical specifications
  • Master README writing: badges, demos, architecture, install instructions
  • Optimize your GitHub profile for recruiter searches
  • Build a portfolio that competes with aerospace internship and junior engineer roles
  • Learn the version control workflow that professional teams use

Why GitHub Is the Hiring Signal

At NASA Goddard, JPL, SpaceX, Blue Origin, Rocket Lab, and Satellogic, recruiters do the same thing: They search GitHub for engineers who can code. They look at commit history. They check if a developer writes clean code. They see if projects are documented. They want to know if you collaborate, if you handle feedback, if you iterate. A GitHub profile is a resume written in code. It is the most honest thing you can show an employer.

Katya Echazarreta, who worked at NASA JPL and flew on Blue Origin New Shepard, has a public GitHub. Emiliano Kargieman, who founded Satellogic and took it public, has public repositories showing his path as a founder-engineer. The Tobacia brothers at Blue Origin contribute to open-source projects. They understand that your GitHub is your professional brand.

What Recruiters Look For

Signal What It Shows Why It Matters for Aerospace
Commit consistency You code regularly, not in spurts Mission-critical code needs daily discipline, not deadline cramming
Project diversity You can learn and apply different languages, frameworks, tools Aerospace uses Python, C++, MATLAB, hardware scripting, all in one mission
Quality READMEs You communicate clearly with engineers who need to use your code In real missions, your README is how teammates understand your flight software
Clean code and comments Your code is readable and maintainable by others Flight-critical software must pass code review by senior engineers
Problem-solving in issues You debug systematically, document errors, iterate Debugging a telemetry anomaly at 2 AM requires systematic thinking
Collaboration (PRs, reviews) You work with teammates, accept feedback, improve code together Every rocket mission is a team effort. Code review is non-negotiable.

Five Portfolio Projects with Complete Specifications

Project 1: Satellite Tracker Web App (Real-Time TLE Data)

  • Tech stack: Python backend (Flask or FastAPI), HTML/CSS/JavaScript frontend, real-time TLE data from N2YO or NASA
  • Features: Search satellite by name, display current position on interactive map (Leaflet.js), show orbit prediction for next 24 hours, alert when satellite passes overhead
  • Data source: N2YO API or Space-Track.org TLE data, Open-Meteo for weather integration
  • Deliverables: GitHub repo, deployed web app (free tier on Heroku or Vercel), README with setup instructions and architecture diagram

Project 2: Rocket Equation Simulator with GUI (Real Physics)

  • Tech stack: Python with Tkinter GUI or PyQt, NumPy for calculations, Matplotlib for plots
  • Features: Input rocket parameters (mass, thrust, ISP, fuel), calculate delta-v, plot acceleration/altitude/velocity profiles over time, compare different rocket designs side-by-side
  • Real physics: Gravity losses, drag, actual rocket equation (not simplified)
  • Deliverables: GitHub repo, executable app, README with physics documentation and screenshots

Project 3: NASA Image of the Day CLI Tool (Learning APIs)

  • Tech stack: Python, requests library, argparse for command-line interface
  • Features: Fetch NASA Astronomy Photo of the Day, download image, display metadata (date, title, description), search by date range, save image locally or upload to cloud
  • Stretch goal: Create a web gallery of saved images with filtering and search
  • Deliverables: GitHub repo with detailed README, pip-installable package, documentation

Project 4: ISS Pass Predictor for Your City (End-to-End)

  • Tech stack: Python backend, web or CLI interface
  • Features: Enter your city name or coordinates, get ISS pass times for next 7 days, show rise time, max altitude, set time, brightness, direction of approach
  • Data: Real TLE data from NASA or N2YO, real ISS position calculations
  • Advanced features: Notifications (email/SMS when ISS is 2 hours away), historical database of past passes
  • Deliverables: GitHub repo, live web version, README, video demo

Project 5: Orbital Mechanics Visualizer (3D, Interactive)

  • Tech stack: Python with Matplotlib 3D or JavaScript with Three.js, NumPy for orbit propagation
  • Features: Visualize Earth, Moon, ISS, geostationary satellites, user-defined orbits in 3D. Animate orbital motion. Show gravity vectors. Allow interactive camera movement.
  • Real physics: Use SGP4 propagation model (pyorbital or skyfield library)
  • Deliverables: Interactive visualization (web version preferred), GitHub repo with documentation, video walkthrough

README Best Practices: What Gets You Hired

Essential Sections (In This Order)

  1. Project Title and One-Liner - What does it do in one sentence. Example: "Real-time ISS position tracker with orbital prediction and web interface."
  2. Badges - Status, license, build status, language. Example: ![Python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Python-3.9+-blue) ![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-green)
  3. Demo or Screenshot - Show, do not tell. Include a GIF or screenshot. If web app, include live link.
  4. Table of Contents - For longer READMEs, helps readers navigate.
  5. Features - Bullet list of what the project does. Focus on user value, not implementation.
  6. Getting Started (Install + Run) - Step-by-step: clone repo, create virtual environment, pip install requirements, run the app. Must work exactly as written.
  7. Usage Examples - Code snippets or command-line examples showing how to use the tool.
  8. Architecture / How It Works - Diagram or text explanation of the system design. Aerospace projects especially need this: what data flows where, what are the key algorithms.
  9. API Documentation - If you exposed functions or endpoints, document them (parameters, return values, errors).
  10. Testing - How to run tests. What tests exist. Coverage percentage if available.
  11. Contributing - How others can report bugs or submit code changes.
  12. License - MIT, Apache 2.0, or GPL. Aerospace companies prefer permissive licenses (MIT, Apache).
  13. Acknowledgments and References - Credits to APIs, libraries, papers, people who helped. Shows you collaborate.

Example README Structure for Satellite Tracker Project

See template file: /downloads/aqn_github_readme_template.md

Optimizing Your GitHub Profile for Aerospace Recruiters

Profile Element What Recruiters See How to Optimize
Profile Picture Your face, briefly Professional headshot. Clear, friendly, good lighting. Not required but helps with recognition.
Bio 50 characters max. Your headline. Example: "Aerospace engineer studying Python, orbital mechanics, and flight software. Learning with NASA data."
Website/URL Link to portfolio, blog, or resume Link to a personal website (free option: GitHub Pages) or LinkedIn. Not just your home city.
Pinned Repositories (6 max) Your best work. First thing recruiters see. Pin your five portfolio projects plus one open-source contribution. Each must have an excellent README.
Contribution Graph Do you code consistently? Aim for commits at least 3-4 days per week. Consistency matters more than volume.
Repository Languages What stack do you know? For aerospace: Python (essential), C++ (for control software), MATLAB (useful), JavaScript (if building web tools).
Stars and Forks How many people found your work useful? Do not worry early. Build good projects with documentation, stars follow.

Version Control Workflow: How Professional Teams Collaborate

You have written code. Now learn how teams work together. Every aerospace company uses this workflow:

  1. Create a branch for each feature: git checkout -b feature/iss-predictor-alerts
  2. Make commits with clear messages: "Add email notification for ISS passes" (not "fix stuff")
  3. Push to GitHub: git push origin feature/iss-predictor-alerts
  4. Open a Pull Request (PR): Write a description of what you changed and why. Link any related issues.
  5. Address feedback: Respond to code review comments, make improvements, commit again.
  6. Merge when approved: Branch gets merged into main. Feature is live.

This process ensures code quality, team communication, and a reviewable history. Every line of flight-critical software has a PR behind it.

Video Lesson: Building a Portfolio That Opens Doors

Aracely's 1000-word video script (11 minutes)

Hi. I'm Aracely. You have learned Python. You have built projects. Now comes the part that actually changes your life: showing the world what you have built.

When I was applying to NASA, I did not have a GitHub profile. It did not exist the way it does now. I had to print out code samples on paper. I had to explain my projects verbally. You are in a different generation. You have a permanent, searchable record of every line of code you have written. That is power. That is also responsibility.

I want you to understand something. Your GitHub profile is a portfolio that never sleeps. It is working for you 24/7. Right now, recruiters at SpaceX are searching GitHub for engineers who have built real projects with real APIs. Recruiters at Blue Origin are looking for people who write clean, commented code. Recruiters at Satellogic in Argentina, at Rocket Lab, at every space startup you can imagine, are doing the same searches. And your GitHub either shows up in those searches or it does not.

Let me tell you about Katya Echazarreta again, because her path is instructive. She grew up in a Mexican immigrant family. She went to a state university, Cal State Long Beach. She studied electrical engineering. Then she went to work at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory. She built real software for the Perseverance rover and the Europa Clipper mission. Those projects are on her resume, but here is the thing: any recruiter can verify her work by looking at the code and the documentation. She can point to the actual tools she built, the problems she solved, the data she worked with. That is credential you cannot fake.

When she applied to be an astronaut, she had a GitHub profile. She had projects that showed she could code under pressure, debug problems, and deliver working software. She had code that actual space missions depend on. That is the difference between a person who says "I can code" and a person whose code literally runs in space.

I want you to build five projects. Not because five is a magic number. But because five projects show a recruiter that you are serious. They show that you can see a problem, build a solution, document it, and ship it. Five projects show consistency. They show that you have depth in multiple areas.

The Satellite Tracker teaches you APIs and web development. The Rocket Simulator teaches you physics and visualization. The NASA Image tool teaches you command-line interfaces and working with real data. The ISS Pass Predictor teaches you end-to-end thinking, from problem to solution. The Orbital Visualizer teaches you advanced mathematics and interactive design.

Together, these five projects prove that you understand aerospace fundamentals, that you can code real solutions, and that you can ship work. That is what aerospace companies want to see.

Now, let me talk about GitHub profiles. I know engineers at SpaceX and Blue Origin who literally have their hiring filtered by a GitHub search. They enter keywords: "Python, orbital mechanics, satellite tracking". They look at the results. They check the top-starred projects first. Those engineers then manually review the code, read the READMEs, and decide whether to reach out.

Your README is your first impression in that review. A README that is clear, complete, and honest will make a recruiter think: "This person knows how to communicate. This person understands that code is written for humans, not computers. I want to meet this person."

A README that is sloppy or incomplete makes a recruiter think: "This person does not care about documentation. If this project breaks, I will not be able to understand it. Pass."

Your README must include: why you built this project, what it does, how to install it, how to use it, what the architecture is, what the open questions are, and how someone can contribute. That is professionalism. That is how you show up as an engineer, not as a student who coded for a grade.

And your commit history matters. If you have written all your code in one giant commit called "Initial Commit", that tells a recruiter that you do not think like a professional. Real engineers commit early and often. They write commit messages that tell the story of the project: "Add ISS pass calculator", "Fix orbital element parsing", "Improve performance with NumPy vectorization". Your commit history is a narrative of your thinking.

At the end of this lesson, you will have five projects, all with excellent READMEs, all with clean commit histories, all with documentation that explains the aerospace domain. You will have a GitHub profile that shows a recruiter: "This person has done the work. This person understands the domain. This person can ship code."

That is when doors open. That is when you get interviews at companies building the future. That is when you become not a student learning to code, but an engineer with a portfolio.

Your GitHub is your reputation. Build it like it matters. Because it does.

Downloads: GitHub and Portfolio Templates

aqn_github_readme_template.md (Template README.md with all best-practice sections)

aqn_portfolio_project_specs.pdf (Complete specs for all 5 projects with wireframes and code stubs)

aqn_github_profile_checklist.pdf (Checklist: bio, pinned repos, contribution graph targets, project quality standards)

Community Discussion

Reflection Prompt: Share the GitHub link to one of your five projects. Ask your cohort: What is one strength of your project? What is one feature you would add if you had more time? How would you improve your README based on feedback?

Quiz: Portfolio and GitHub Mastery

Answer all 7 questions. Score 6 or higher to complete Module 3.

  1. Write a complete README.md for one of your five portfolio projects. Include: project title, one-liner, badges, features, installation instructions, usage example, architecture explanation, and license. (Rubric: completeness, clarity, professionalism. Minimum 400 words.)
  2. Create a commit message log (5-7 commits) as if you were building your Satellite Tracker project. Write realistic, descriptive commit messages that tell the story of development. Example: "Add real-time TLE parsing and N2YO API integration".
  3. Describe your GitHub profile optimization strategy. What will your bio say? Which 6 projects will you pin? How will you demonstrate commit consistency over the next 3 months?
  4. Choose one of your five projects. Explain its architecture using text or ASCII diagram: What are the main components? How do they communicate? What data flows where? What are the dependencies? (Minimum 300 words or clear diagram.)
  5. Write a contribution guide for one of your projects. Tell future collaborators: How do they set up a development environment? How should they structure a pull request? What coding standards do you expect? How do you handle code review?
  6. Identify an aerospace company (SpaceX, Blue Origin, Rocket Lab, etc.) and one of their hiring requirements (e.g., "Python, C++, understanding of flight software"). Describe how your five-project portfolio demonstrates that you meet this requirement.
  7. Reflect: In 5-7 sentences, describe how building a portfolio of five projects has changed how you think about engineering. What did you learn about collaboration, documentation, and shipping code?

Answer Key and Rubric

Question 1 (Complete README): Must include all sections. Title and one-liner clear and concise. Badges present (language, license, etc.). Features bullet list covers core functionality. Installation instructions are step-by-step and tested. Usage examples include code snippets or commands. Architecture explanation is clear to someone unfamiliar with the project. License is specified (MIT or Apache recommended for aerospace). Minimum 400 words. Professional tone throughout.

Question 2 (Commit log): Each commit message starts with an action verb ("Add", "Fix", "Improve", etc.). Messages are specific (not "update code"). Log tells a coherent development story. Commits seem to represent logical, reviewable units of work. Shows understanding of professional development workflow.

Question 3 (Profile optimization): Bio is specific to aerospace/coding, not generic. Shows understanding of what recruiters search for. Six pinned projects are your best work with strong READMEs. Commit consistency strategy is realistic and measurable (e.g., "Code 4 days/week, 10+ commits/month"). Shows awareness of GitHub as a professional tool.

Question 4 (Architecture explanation): Identifies main components (backend, frontend, APIs, databases, etc.). Shows data flow clearly (input sources, processing, outputs). Lists dependencies explicitly (libraries, APIs, datasets). Explains why components are organized this way, not just what they are. Diagram (if provided) is clear and labeled. Minimum 300 words or clear, professional diagram.

Question 5 (Contribution guide): Development setup instructions are complete (Python version, virtual env, pip install, required API keys). Pull request template is defined (title format, description requirements, testing checklist). Code standards are explicit (style guide reference like PEP 8, commenting requirements, test coverage). Code review process is defined (who reviews, timeline, feedback expectations). Shows maturity and collaboration mindset.

Question 6 (Company/requirement mapping): Student correctly identifies a real aerospace company and a real job requirement. Describes specifically which project(s) demonstrate that skill. Examples are concrete, not vague. Shows understanding of how a portfolio builds credibility with real employers.

Question 7 (Reflection): Genuine reflection on learning. Mentions specific skills or mindsets developed (collaboration, documentation, iterating on feedback, shipping code). Shows growth from "I can code" to "I can engineer". Connects portfolio work to real aerospace engineering practices.

Module 3 Complete

You have earned mastery of Python fundamentals, intermediate Python with OOP and APIs, and portfolio development with professional GitHub practices. You are now ready for Module 4, where you will explore aerospace research methodologies, technical writing, and the engineering behind real missions. You have the coding skills. Now it is time to understand the science.

Module 4: Research Innovation Lab
← Previous: Lesson 3.3Course index

Lesson 4.1: NASA Research Programs & Why Research Matters

Module 4: Research & Innovation Lab

When I was studying in the U.S., I didn't have a roadmap. My mother and grandmother in Lambayeque believed I could be anything, but they had no idea what a NASA fellowship meant. What I learned, slowly, is this: every NASA mission that reaches the stars started as a research question. Someone asked, "Can we measure rainfall better?" or "How do we see the moon's ancient craters?" Those questions became my life's work. Right now, you're asking the same questions. And NASA has formal pathways to turn your curiosity into professional reality. They exist specifically so that young people like us, first generation, from places where nobody knows NASA, can belong here.

Video Script, Aracely / Irma reads on camera

VIDEO SCRIPT:

"Hello. I'm Dr. Aracely Quispe Neira, and I want to talk to you about something that changed my life: NASA research programs.

When I first came to the U.S., I was studying engineering and working three jobs. I didn't have time to look for opportunities, I was just trying to survive. But my advisor told me about the NASA Pathways Internship Program. It was free, it had a stipend, and it gave me real work experience in spacecraft operations. That one internship led to my career at NASA Goddard.

Today, NASA has multiple entry points. If you're in high school or college, there's the Pathways program. If you're in graduate school, there are NASA Fellowships in every major research area: aeronautics, earth sciences, space technology. If you're already working, there's the Undergraduate Student Research Program (USRP) or the internships at JPL, Ames, Glenn. Some of you might be thinking, 'But I'm not from the U.S.' or 'I don't have a fancy degree.' Let me stop you right there. NASA hires talent. They invest in people who ask good questions and do the work.

What I want you to understand is this: NASA doesn't just fund missions. NASA funds people. They fund your education, your research, your growth. And they specifically want young people from underrepresented communities, that includes us, people from Latin America, first-generation students, people from rural backgrounds like me. The programs exist. The money exists. What you need is a plan.

In this lesson, I'm going to show you exactly which programs exist, who qualifies, how to apply, and what happens after you're accepted. Your research career doesn't start 'someday.' It starts with knowing what's available right now."

NASA Research Pathways: Your Entry Points

1. NASA Pathways Internship Program

What it is: A federal internship designed to bring students into NASA centers for hands-on experience. You work on real projects, not busywork.

Who qualifies: U.S. citizens (including permanent residents) enrolled full-time in an accredited college/university, GPA 2.9+. Pathways comes in three tiers: Intern (during school), Recent Graduate (within 2 years of graduation), or Veteran.

The offer: Paid internship (typically 10-40 hours/week during school, full-time in summers), mentorship from NASA engineers/scientists, housing stipends at some centers. Upon completion, conversion to permanent federal employment is possible.

Where to apply: USAJobs.gov, search "NASA Pathways". Apply to specific centers: Goddard, Jet Propulsion Lab (JPL), Johnson Space Center (Houston), Glenn Research Center (Ohio), Ames (California), Marshall (Alabama), Kennedy (Florida).

Timeline: Hiring cycles usually open November-December (summer positions) and April-May (fall positions). Apply 6-8 weeks before start date.

Aracely's note: I worked Pathways at Goddard for 18 months while finishing my degree. The people you meet become your career network for life. The work is real, I was on the TRMM mission ops team, actually supporting the spacecraft, not filing papers.

2. NASA Graduate Student Researchers Program (GSRP) & Fellowships

What it is: Fellowships for master's and PhD students conducting research aligned with NASA's strategic priorities (earth science, planetary exploration, aeronautics, space technology, human spaceflight).

Who qualifies: Enrolled in an accredited U.S. graduate program, U.S. citizen (some fellowships include foreign nationals under visa sponsorship).

The award: Typically $28K-$38K per year for 3 years (varies by program). Covers tuition, stipend, and sometimes requires a NASA internship summer placement.

Real programs to target:

  • NASA Space Technology Research Fellowships (NSTRF): For innovative tech research (materials, robotics, propulsion). $35K/year.
  • NASA Earth and Space Science Fellowship (NESSF): For earth science, planetary, or astrophysics research. $31K/year.
  • NASA Aeronautics Scholarship and Advanced STEM Training and Research (ASTAR): For aeronautics and propulsion work. Up to $180K over 5 years.

Application timeline: Most open March-April for fall start. Requires letters from your advisor, research proposal, and GPA 3.0+.

Why it matters: Fellowship funding means your research advisor isn't paying your stipend from their grant, you bring external money to the lab, which makes you attractive to top PIs. You're not a cost; you're an investment.

3. Undergraduate Student Research Program (USRP) & Community College Pathways

What it is: Summer research internships for undergraduates, often paired with course credit.

Who qualifies: Sophomore or junior standing, GPA 2.5+. Community colleges and 4-year schools both have programs. U.S. citizenship not always required (check each center).

The offer: 10-12 week summer placement, paid (~$500-$800/week), assigned to a NASA research team, mentorship.

Where to look: Each NASA center posts through their university partnerships office. JPL Summer Undergraduate Research Fellowships (SURF) is highly competitive but famous, apply there if you're on the West Coast or interested in planetary science.

Difference from Pathways: USRP is pure research (you're on a PI's team). Pathways is broader operational/engineering exposure. Both are valuable; choose based on your interest (Do you want to do research or see the full mission operation?).

4. Beyond Internships: University Research Partnerships

NASA also funds research directly through universities. Your institution may have a NASA Research Agreement (NRA) or a Cooperative Agreement with NASA. This means tenured professors at your university have grants to do NASA-aligned research, and they hire students.

How to find your PI: Ask your department chair or check your university's research office website (it's usually called "Office of Research" or "Graduate Studies Office"). Search their database for professors with "NASA" in their project titles.

Example: Arizona State University has dozens of faculty running NASA-funded earth science and planetary projects. University of Colorado has orbital mechanics research. If your institution doesn't have direct NASA funding, nearby universities often do, and some take visiting researchers.

5. The Strategic Importance of Research to Your NASA Career

Why does NASA fund research so heavily? Because every mission requires new knowledge. Before we built the James Webb Space Telescope, we had to develop novel materials for mirrors, innovative deployment mechanisms, new cryogenic systems. Graduate students and researchers invented those. When I transitioned from Pathways Internships to NASA full-time, I was hired as an engineer because I had done hands-on research. My resume showed problem-solving, not just enthusiasm.

Research also builds your professional network. The PIs you work with, the conference presentations you give, the papers you write, they create visibility.
