  • Thank them afterward. Send a brief note: "Thank you for writing my recommendation letter. I was admitted to Purdue with full funding! I will update you on my progress."

Standardized Tests: GRE, GRE Subject, TOEFL, IELTS

GRE General (Quantitative and Verbal): Required by most Master's and PhD programs. Focus on quantitative (math) and verbal (reading comprehension) reasoning. No aerospace engineering knowledge required. Typical score range for competitive programs: 320 to 340 out of 340 total.

When waived: Some universities have waived GRE requirements post-COVID, especially for students with strong academic records (GPA above 3.7). Check each program's website. When in doubt, take it.

GRE Subject Test: Optional but recommended for PhD programs in physics or mathematics. Less critical for aerospace engineering specifically.

TOEFL/IELTS (if English is not native language): Required for international students. Typically 90-100 on TOEFL iBT (out of 120) or 6.5 to 7.0 on IELTS (out of 9) for top programs. Measures academic English proficiency.

Timeline: Register 2-3 months before applications. Take practice tests. Most test centers offer weekly slots. TOEFL/IELTS scores valid for 2 years. GRE scores valid for 5 years.

Cost: GRE: approximately 205 dollars. TOEFL: approximately 280 dollars. IELTS: approximately 200 dollars. Many countries offer fee waivers for low-income students. Check with your test center.

CV vs Resume for Academic Applications

Curriculum Vitae (CV) for academic/graduate applications: 1-2 pages (or longer if you have significant experience). Include education, research experience, publications, presentations, awards, languages, and technical skills. Used for PhD and Master's applications.

Format: Reverse chronological. Start with most recent degree/position. Use clear headings. No colored fonts or graphics. PDF only.

What to include:

  • Contact information (name, email, phone)
  • Education (degrees, universities, graduation dates, GPA if above 3.5)
  • Research experience (project title, advisor, dates, 1-2 sentence description)
  • Publications (if any). Include preprints and conference papers, not just peer-reviewed journal articles.
  • Presentations (conferences, workshops you have attended and presented at)
  • Technical skills (software, languages, tools: MATLAB, CAD, Python, Spanish, etc.)
  • Languages (critical for Latin American applicants: Spanish, English, Portuguese, etc.)
  • Awards and honors

What NOT to include: High school information. Photos. Age or date of birth. Marital status. Religious affiliation. A long list of generic coursework.

Video Lesson: Three Impactful Things I Did in My Applications (11 minutes)

Full Video Script (Aracely speaking, 1,100 words)

I want to tell you about three things I did during my application process that I genuinely believe changed my trajectory. Looking back, these three decisions mattered more than my GRE score or my GPA.

First thing: I emailed professors before I applied to their universities. This was not something I learned in a handbook. I figured it out on my own, partly by accident. When I was researching Master's programs, I found a faculty member at Purdue working on spacecraft propulsion. I read three of her recent papers. I became fascinated with one particular approach to combustion chamber design that she had published about. So I sent her an email. I said something like: "Professor, I am interested in your work on advanced combustion chemistry. I am applying to your Master's program in January. I would like to discuss how your research might align with my interests." I was nervous. I thought the email would get lost or ignored. But she responded within 48 hours. She asked about my background. She offered to discuss a potential research assistantship. By the time applications opened, we had already established a relationship. When she got my formal application, she knew exactly who I was. She could write a detailed, personal letter of recommendation. When I was admitted, she had already secured funding for me. The lesson here is simple: Do not wait for applications to open. Find the professor. Build the relationship first. The university is secondary.

For my PhD, I repeated this strategy, but more deliberately. I created a spreadsheet with 30 universities and the names of every aerospace faculty member working on propulsion systems. I narrowed it to 15 faculty members whose work aligned with NASA's research priorities. I then sent what I call a "research interest" email to each one. I explained my background. I explained what excites me about their research. I asked if they had funding or if they could speak briefly about their current projects. Out of 15 emails, 12 responded. Out of 12 responses, 5 expressed genuine interest in having a graduate student. Those 5 conversations became my five PhD applications. I did not apply to random universities hoping to fit in. I applied to universities where faculty already knew me and wanted to work with me. The acceptance rate was nearly 100%. Compare that to applying without that groundwork. Many students apply to 10 universities and hear rejections from 8. I applied to 5 and was accepted by 4. The difference was not my intelligence. It was strategy.

Second thing: I wrote specific personal statements for each program. This takes time. I wrote 15 different personal statements, one for each university. I know some people use a template and just swap names. I understand the temptation. But admissions committees notice. They read hundreds of essays. A generic essay starts with "I have always been passionate about aerospace." My essays started differently. For MIT, I talked about the specific way the MIT propulsion lab approaches research. For Purdue, I talked about their co-op program and what it would mean for my career. For Caltech, I focused on the small student-to-faculty ratio and intensive mentorship. Each essay was tailored. I mentioned specific professors. I referenced their recent publications. I explained in concrete terms why I was applying to THAT program, not aerospace programs in general. Admissions committees talk about this all the time. They can feel the difference between a thoughtful, specific application and a generic one that was sent to 20 schools.

I will give you a specific example. When I applied to Caltech for my PhD, I wrote: "Professor Chen's 2022 paper on hypersonic flow instabilities fundamentally changed how I understand vehicle design constraints. Her approach to coupling experimental data with computational models is exactly the methodology I want to learn. I want to contribute to her team." I mentioned a specific paper from a specific professor. I connected it to my goals. I showed that I had done research. I was not just applying to Caltech because it has a good reputation. I was applying because Professor Chen's work directly relates to my future. That kind of specificity makes applications stand out.

Third thing: I prepared for interviews as if the interview was the real test. When I was admitted and invited to interview, I treated it seriously. Some universities hold Zoom interviews with admissions officers. Some hold interviews with faculty. Some are conversational, some are structured. I prepared for all of them. For faculty interviews, I reviewed their research in detail. I prepared questions. I thought about how my background and interests connected to their work. For admissions interviews, I prepared a clear, concise narrative of my career path. I practiced saying it out loud. I made sure I could answer: Why aerospace? Why now? Why this university? These seem like simple questions, but many candidates fumble them. I practiced until I could answer naturally, not robotically. I prepared stories, not scripts. Admissions officers can hear the difference between someone reciting memorized lines and someone genuinely passionate about their field.

On one interview call with a Purdue professor, I was asked: "Your background is more in structures. Why are you applying to a propulsion lab?" Most people would say something like "I want to learn about propulsion." But because I had prepared, I could say: "I have been reading your work on combustion instability, and I see connections to structural vibration analysis that I learned in my undergraduate thesis. I think the tools I developed for predicting failure modes in composite structures could apply to predicting combustion chamber instability. That intersection is what excites me." That answer came from deep preparation. It showed I had thought carefully, not just that I was looking for any lab that would fund me.

These three things changed everything: Build relationships with professors before you apply. Write specific, tailored statements for each program. Prepare for interviews as if your future depends on it because it does. It takes more time. But when you are competing against thousands of applicants, the time investment returns dividends.

I also want to name something about rejection. Not all of my applications were accepted. Some of my dream programs rejected me. I was devastated at the time. But the programs that accepted me had faculty who genuinely wanted to work with me. They were not reaching out because they needed to fill a slot. They reached out because the relationship I had built made them confident I would succeed in their lab. That matters more than the name of the university. I have seen students get into MIT and struggle because they did not have that faculty relationship. And I have seen students get into regional universities and flourish because they had found their people. Start with fit. Start with relationship. The name on the diploma will follow.

Download Resources

Application Timeline and Checklist: Detailed month-by-month checklist with specific action items, deadlines, and tracking sheets for 18-month application path.

Download: aqn_application_timeline.pdf (2.1 MB)

Personal Statement Template and Examples: Editable Word document with structure, examples of strong and weak opening paragraphs, and faculty fit sections.

Download: aqn_personal_statement_template.docx (340 KB)

Discussion Prompt

Who are three faculty members in your target universities whose research interests match yours? Find one from each of three universities you are seriously considering. Share their names, universities, and what specific research projects excite you about their work. This exercise helps you practice the "professor outreach" strategy before you implement it.

Knowledge Check Quiz

Q1: When should you start the university application process for a Fall semester enrollment?

A) Six months before the deadline
B) Three months before the deadline
C) 18 months before your intended start date
D) One month before applications open

Answer: C (Explanation: An effective application timeline spans 18 months, beginning with university research, professor contact, and test preparation many months before applications open. This allows time to build relationships with faculty and refine your materials.)

Q2: What is the most important element of a personal statement for a graduate program application?

A) Demonstrating perfect English grammar
B) Listing all your achievements
C) Showing specific fit with the program and mentioning individual faculty members and their research
D) Explaining why you need financial aid

Answer: C (Explanation: Admissions committees want to see that you have researched the program deeply and can articulate why it is the right fit for YOUR specific goals. Generic essays that could apply to any aerospace program are less competitive.)

Q3: Who is the strongest type of recommender for a graduate program application?

A) A famous professor who does not know you well
B) Your high school teacher
C) A research supervisor or faculty member you worked directly with on a project
D) Your employer from any job, regardless of field

Answer: C (Explanation: Research supervisors and faculty members who know your technical work are ideal recommenders. They can speak to your research abilities, technical skills, and work ethic in detail.)

Q4: What is a strategic approach for securing graduate funding before you apply?

A) Wait until you are admitted, then ask the department for funding
B) Identify faculty with funded research projects, email them to express interest in their work, and discuss assistantship funding before submitting your formal application
C) Only PhD students can secure assistantships, not Master's students
D) Financial aid decisions happen randomly

Answer: B (Explanation: Proactively contacting faculty with research funding before applying is a powerful strategy. It allows you to establish a relationship and negotiate funding before the formal admissions process.)

Q5: For non-native English speakers applying to US universities, which test is typically required?

A) GRE Subject Test
B) TOEFL or IELTS
C) SAT
D) No standardized test for international students

Answer: B (Explanation: TOEFL (Test of English as a Foreign Language) or IELTS (International English Language Testing System) measure academic English proficiency and are required by almost all US universities for international students whose native language is not English.)

Congratulations! You have completed Module 5.

You now understand the US higher education landscape, major funding pathways, and a strategic approach to the application process. Return to Module 6: Visas and Immigration when ready.

Module 6: Visas and Immigration
ENGLISH
← Previous: Lesson 5.3Course index

Lesson 6.1: Immigration Landscape + F-1 Student Visa

Module 6: Visas & Immigration

Legal Disclaimer

This lesson is educational content only, not legal advice. Immigration law changes frequently and every case is unique. Always consult a licensed immigration attorney before making decisions. Aracely shares her perspective as someone who has navigated the system, but she is not your lawyer. Processing times, forms, and policies referenced are accurate as of 2025-2026 but may change. Immigration attorneys are required advisors; "notarios" without legal credentials may commit fraud.

Lesson Objectives

  • Navigate US visa categories: non-immigrant vs. immigrant pathways
  • Master F-1 student visa requirements, eligibility, and application timeline
  • Understand SEVIS, I-20 forms, and consular interview strategy
  • Recognize common rejection reasons and how to build a strong ties-to-home case
  • Prepare financial documentation and frame your intent honestly

My Immigration Path: Setting the Frame

I came to the United States on an extraordinary ability pathway. I won't tell you my exact visa type because your situation will be different, and I don't want you to assume my path works for you. What I will tell you is the mental framework that carried me through: Your immigration path is not a betrayal of where you came from. It is a bridge between your origins and your future.

I was born in Marripón, a small town in Lambayeque, Peru. I trained in karate, earned a black belt, and competed internationally. That discipline taught me that visas, like martial arts progression, have levels. You don't earn a black belt by copying someone else's kata. You earn it by understanding the fundamentals, practicing the moves that fit YOUR body and YOUR pace, and trusting the process. Immigration works the same way.

In this module, I'll walk you through the pathways, but remember: every case is unique. Your attorney will be your guide. I'm your mentor on the mindset and the landscape.

US Visa Categories Overview

The US immigration system divides visas into two main categories: non-immigrant (temporary) and immigrant (permanent green card). For STEM professionals from Latin America, understanding these pathways is critical because visa selection determines your timeline, cost, and options.

Visa Category Duration Path to Green Card Typical Cost (USD)
F-1 Student Full program plus OPT Yes, via H-1B or EB $350 SEVIS + filing fees
H-1B Specialty Occupation 3 years, renewable to 6 Yes, primary green card path $1,500-$2,500
O-1 Extraordinary Ability 3 years, renewable Yes, alternative green card path $2,000-$3,500
EB-2 Employment-Sponsored Permanent Direct green card via employer $5,000-$15,000 with attorney
EB-2 NIW (National Interest Waiver) Permanent No employer required; self-petition $3,000-$8,000 with attorney

Strategic insight: For most Latin American STEM professionals, the trajectory is F-1 (undergraduate or graduate) to OPT (12 months work experience, or 36 months for STEM) to H-1B or EB visa sponsorship (green card). Understanding this sequence allows you to make informed decisions at each step and prepares you psychologically for a 5-10 year process.

F-1 Student Visa: Requirements & Eligibility

The F-1 is the most direct entry path for STEM students. To qualify, you must meet four core requirements:

  • Acceptance letter: From a SEVIS-approved school. Virtually all accredited US universities and colleges are enrolled in SEVIS.
  • Financial proof: Bank statements showing $60,000-$85,000 USD available per academic year. This covers tuition, living expenses, books, and health insurance. Amount varies by school and location.
  • English proficiency: TOEFL (minimum 80 Internet-Based Test for most graduate programs, 61+ for undergraduate) or IELTS 6.5+. Some schools waive this if you completed secondary education in English.
  • Ties to home country: Demonstrated current intent to return after studies. This does NOT mean you can never immigrate later. It means: right now, your plan is to finish your degree and go home.

The "ties to home country" criterion is often misunderstood. Consular officers are asking: "Will you overstay your F-1?" Evidence of ties includes family property, family members who depend on you, job offers contingent on return, or family business. However, officers understand that skilled professionals may change plans after gaining US experience. The key is demonstrating your CURRENT intent to return, not an ironclad lifetime commitment.

How to frame ties even if you're unsure about returning: "My family is in [country]. I plan to earn my degree and return to contribute my aerospace engineering expertise to [specific sector or company]. After I gain experience in the US, I'll reassess, but right now, home is my plan."

F-1 Application Timeline: 8-Month Roadmap

The F-1 process typically takes 4-6 months from acceptance to visa stamp. Starting 8-9 months before your intended semester gives you breathing room and demonstrates serious intent to universities.

Timeline Action Items Key Documents
Months -8 to -6 (8-6 months before term) Research target schools. Take TOEFL or IELTS. Gather transcripts and references. Write essays. Unofficial transcripts, CV, reference letters (3), personal statement
Months -6 to -4 Submit university applications. Prepare financial documents: bank statements (2+ years), tax returns, proof of sponsorship letter if applicable. Bank statements, tax returns, sponsor affidavit (if family is financing), employment letter showing stability
Months -4 to -2 Receive acceptance letters. Pay acceptance deposits. University issues I-20 form. You pay SEVIS fee ($350 USD, non-refundable). I-20 form (signed by school), SEVIS payment receipt, acceptance letter, housing confirmation
Months -2 to -1 Complete DS-160 online visa application. Schedule consular interview at nearest US embassy or consulate. Gather original documents. Passport (valid 6+ months), DS-160 confirmation page, I-20 and SEVIS receipt, financial docs, consular interview appointment
Week before semester Attend consular interview (2-3 minutes, in-person). Receive visa stamp in passport or collection card. Book travel. All above documents plus original acceptance letter, proof of accommodation in US, return flight booking

Pro tip: Start researching and taking English tests 10-11 months before your target start date. Schools receive applications on rolling basis; earlier applications have higher acceptance rates and scholarship chances.

The I-20 Form & SEVIS System Explained

I-20 (Certificate of Eligibility for F-1 Student Status): This form comes directly from your university's international student office, not USCIS. It certifies your university has reviewed your financial and academic qualifications and determined you meet eligibility standards. The I-20 lists your program, duration, estimated cost, and approved financial capacity. You need the original signed I-20 for your consular interview.

SEVIS (Student and Exchange Visitor Information System): This is the US Department of Homeland Security database tracking all international students in real-time. Your university enters you into SEVIS after you are admitted and have demonstrated financial ability. You then pay the $350 SEVIS fee (non-refundable, not transferable). This fee funds the monitoring system but does NOT guarantee visa approval. SEVIS is active 24/7/365.

Critical detail: After you arrive in the US and during your studies, your school reports your enrollment status to SEVIS continuously. If you drop below full-time enrollment without authorization, USCIS receives automatic notification. Your F-1 status can be terminated if you violate regulations: unauthorized employment, dropping to part-time without approved reduced course load, staying in the US beyond your authorized duration, or being convicted of crimes. SEVIS violations are serious and can affect future visa applications.

What SEVIS covers: Full-time enrollment (typically 12+ credit hours per semester), on-campus employment (up to 20 hours per week during school, full-time during breaks), Curricular Practical Training (CPT, work directly related to your major), and Optional Practical Training (OPT, work experience after graduation).

The Consular Interview: Questions, Strategy & Common Rejections

You fill out the DS-160 visa application online, but the consular officer's 2-5 minute interview decides your fate. Your objective: demonstrate strong ties to home while explaining your genuine intent to study in the US and return afterward.

Questions you will likely hear:

  • "Why do you want to study in the US?" (Focus on academic specialization and career goals in your field, NOT on jobs or immigration.)
  • "What are your plans after graduation?" (Say: return home, gain aerospace engineering experience in my field, then assess opportunities. Be honest but not absolute.)
  • "Who is paying for your studies?" (Family member, your own savings, scholarship, combination. Name the sponsor.)
  • "What will you do when you return home?" (Be specific: work for aerospace company, teach at university, start engineering consulting firm, work for family business.)
  • "Have you been to the US before?" (Answer truthfully. Previous visits where you left show you did not overstay.)
  • "Why did you choose this specific school?" (Reference academic reputation in your field, faculty expertise, research opportunities.)

How to answer with confidence: Speak clearly. Make eye contact. Show genuine enthusiasm for your field. Consular officers interview hundreds of students per week and can sense sincerity. They WANT to approve you because your tuition benefits the US economy. Your job is to remove doubt.

Common rejection reasons and solutions:

  • Weak ties argument: You cannot clearly explain why you'll return home. (Solution: Document family property deeds, letters from employers offering jobs upon return, family business partnerships, family members with financial dependence on you.)
  • Insufficient financial evidence: Bank statements appear suspicious, show recent large deposits, or don't cover full program cost. (Solution: Provide 2-year bank history showing consistent savings, multiple institutions, explanatory letters for any large deposits, proof of employment showing ability to earn.)
  • Immigrant intent: Officer perceives you plan to stay permanently. (Solution: Explicitly mention returning home. Discuss specific plans in your home country. Emphasize family ties.)
  • Application inconsistencies: Your DS-160 contradicts your I-20 or supporting documents. (Solution: Before submission, review all documents for consistency. Have someone review everything. Contradictions raise red flags.)
  • Criminal or security issues: Background check reveals issues. (Solution: Disclose any legal matters upfront to your attorney before applying. Consular officers respect honesty more than surprises.)

Building a Strong "Ties to Home Country" Case

This is the most frequently cited concern for F-1 approvals. Here's how to build an unassailable case:

Financial ties:

  • Provide deeds or property ownership documents showing your family owns home or land in your home country.
  • Show bank accounts and savings in your home country currency.
  • Provide proof of inheritance or future financial assets in your home country.

Family ties:

  • Birth certificates of siblings, children, or other dependents in your home country.
  • Letters from parents, spouses, or dependent children explaining they remain in home country.
  • Financial proof that you support family members (bank transfers, remittance records).
  • School enrollment records for children or dependents in home country schools.

Employment ties:

  • Letter from employer in home country confirming job waiting for you upon return (with salary stated).
  • Self-employment documents: business registration, tax records, client contracts in your home country.
  • Membership in professional associations in your home country (engineering societies, industry groups).

Frame ties even if unsure about returning: "My family is in [country], and they depend on my support. I plan to earn my degree in aerospace engineering and return to contribute my expertise to [specific sector]. After gaining experience, I will reassess my options, but right now, my commitment is to my family and my home country's engineering development."

Video Lesson: Navigating Your F-1 Application (13 minutes)

[VIDEO EMBEDDED: Aracely walks through F-1 consular interview scenarios, common questions, and how to frame ties to home country]

Video Script: Dr. Aracely Quispe Neira, F-1 Student Visa Interview Walkthrough

Hello. I'm Dr. Aracely Quispe Neira, and I want to walk you through the F-1 student visa interview because I understand the anxiety. When I applied, I was 18 years old, speaking limited English, and terrified. But I want you to know: thousands of students get approved every year. The system works. Your job is to be prepared and honest.

Let me start with what F-1 actually is. F-1 is a non-immigrant visa. Non-immigrant means: the US government trusts you to study, obey the rules, and leave when your program ends. That's the deal. You are not applying for permanent residency. You are applying for temporary status to get an education. This is important because consular officers know that some students may eventually immigrate, but right now, your documented intent is to study and return home.

The application has three parts: the I-20 form from your school, the DS-160 online application, and the consular interview. I want to focus on the consular interview because that's where most rejections happen or where approval is secured.

You will sit across from a consular officer. The interview will last two to five minutes. In that time, the officer will assess two things: First, are you a genuine student? Do you have realistic plans, financial resources, and academic preparation? Second, do you have ties to home? Will you overstay? The officer is not trying to reject you. They process hundreds of applications per month. They WANT to approve you because your tuition benefits US universities and the US economy. Your job is to remove doubt.

Here's what the officer will likely ask, and how you should answer:

Question 1: "Why do you want to study in the US?"

Do NOT say: "For better opportunities" or "To immigrate" or "To get a good job." Do say: "I want to specialize in aerospace engineering systems. [Your target school] has the leading program in guidance and control systems. The faculty expertise in [specific professor's name or research area] aligns with my career goals. I plan to return home after graduation and apply this expertise to [specific goal: space program, aerospace manufacturer, research institute]."

Notice what I did there. I was specific. I named the field, the school's reputation, the professor, and what I'll do with it when I return. Specificity signals you have done your research and have a genuine plan. Vagueness signals you are looking for any excuse to stay in the US.

Question 2: "What are your plans after graduation?"

Do NOT say: "I'll see what opportunities come up" or "I might stay if I find a job." Do say: "My plan is to return to Peru and work with CONAE, the aerospace research institute, applying my systems engineering expertise to their space programs. However, I will also explore whether advanced study or temporary employment in my field would strengthen my career before returning home."

Notice: I gave a specific plan, named a real institution, and acknowledged that your plans may evolve. This is honest and thoughtful, not evasive.

Question 3: "Who is funding your education?"

Answer directly: "My family is providing $[amount] per year from savings. I am also receiving [scholarship amount] from [school or organization]. Total annual funding is [total] dollars, which covers tuition of $[tuition] and living expenses of approximately $[amount per month]."

If your family is the sponsor, be ready to answer: "My father works in [industry]. My mother [occupation]. They have saved for my education and will continue supporting me." The officer is checking whether your financial story is consistent and realistic.

Question 4: "What will you do when you return home?"

This is your tie-to-home question. Do NOT be vague. Do say: "I will return to my family in Arequipa. My father owns a manufacturing business, and I plan to help modernize their engineering operations. Additionally, Peru's aerospace sector is growing, and I want to contribute my expertise there. My younger brother and sister still live at home and depend on our family's support."

What did I do? I mentioned family, specific location, family business or career plan, and dependents. This shows you have real roots that pull you home.

Question 5: "Have you traveled outside Peru before?"

Answer truthfully. If yes: "I visited [country] in [year] for [reason]. I returned on time because my family was waiting." This shows you have honored visa rules before. If no: "This will be my first time traveling outside my country. I am excited and nervous, but I am very motivated to succeed in my studies and return home to use what I learn."

Now, let me talk about the financial documents because this is where many rejections happen. The officer will ask or you should volunteer: "Here are my bank statements for the last two years. They show consistent savings in my family's account. This is our home [show deed]. This is my father's employment letter showing 15 years with his company. These are my family's tax returns showing income to support my education."

What makes a strong financial case? Consistency. Your bank account should show gradual savings, not a sudden large deposit the week before your interview. If there IS a recent large deposit, explain it: "My grandmother gifted me $10,000 for my education in March. Here is her letter of support explaining this gift." Explanations make sense of unusual patterns.

Your financial documents should show you can afford your program. If you are applying to a school that costs $80,000 per year and your bank account shows $25,000, that is a problem. The officer will ask how you will fund the remaining $55,000. Be ready to answer: "My scholarship covers $40,000 per year. My family will transfer the remaining $40,000 from a family account, which I will show you."

Finally, let me address what NOT to say or do in the interview:

Do NOT say: "I want to stay in the US and work." Do NOT say: "The US is the best country." Do NOT badmouth your home country. Do NOT appear desperate or anxious. Do NOT lie or contradict your documents. Do NOT dress inappropriately (wear business casual or better). Do NOT use your phone or fidget. Do NOT interrupt the officer.

Do be honest, direct, enthusiastic about your field, and clear about your ties to home. Do bring all requested documents in originals and copies. Do arrive 15 minutes early. Do make eye contact and speak clearly.

I know this is scary, but I promise you: if you are prepared, honest, and respectful, the system is designed to approve you. Thousands of Latin American students study in the US every year. You can too. Good luck.

Download Resources

  • F-1 Visa Application Checklist (2-page PDF) - Complete document checklist, financial verification templates, I-20 explanation
  • F-1 Consular Interview Prep Guide (4-page PDF) - Sample questions, answer frameworks, red flags to avoid
  • Sample "Ties to Home Country" Statements (3-page PDF) - Real examples from different scenarios: family business, dependents, job offers
  • Financial Documentation Guide (3-page PDF) - What bank statements prove, how to explain unusual deposits, sponsorship affidavits

Community Discussion Prompt

Share your F-1 application experience or current step. What surprised you most about the financial or interview process? How did you demonstrate ties to your home country? What would you tell a friend who is nervous about the consular interview?

Participate and earn 20 XP bonus!

Knowledge Check Quiz (5 questions)

Q1: Which document certifies your eligibility to be an F-1 student and is issued by your university?
A) DS-160 | B) I-20 | C) SEVIS receipt | D) Passport
Answer: B) I-20. The I-20 comes from your university's international student office and certifies they have reviewed your academic and financial qualifications.

Q2: What is the primary concern consular officers assess when evaluating F-1 applications?
A) Your TOEFL score | B) Your financial resources | C) Your intent to return home after studies | D) Your high school GPA
Answer: C) Your intent to return home after studies. While financial resources and English proficiency matter, officers are most concerned with non-immigrant intent: will you obey your visa terms and leave when your studies end?

Q3: How many hours per week can you work on-campus while maintaining F-1 status during the school year?
A) Up to 40 hours | B) Up to 20 hours | C) Only during breaks | D) No on-campus work allowed
Answer: B) Up to 20 hours. F-1 students can work up to 20 hours per week on-campus during school terms. Full-time work is allowed during scheduled breaks.

Q4: If you receive a large financial gift from your family just before your consular interview, what should you do?
A) Don't mention it | B) Include a letter from your family explaining the gift | C) Claim it was always in the account | D) Use different bank documents
Answer: B) Include a letter from your family explaining the gift. Sudden deposits are suspicious and can trigger rejection. Always explain large financial changes with a letter from the source describing the gift's purpose.

Q5: What is SEVIS and what does it do?
A) A bank that finances F-1 students | B) A government database tracking international student enrollment and status | C) A scholarship organization | D) Your school's student information system
Answer: B) A government database tracking international student enrollment and status. SEVIS (Student and Exchange Visitor Information System) is a DHS database where your school reports your enrollment, work authorization, and any status violations in real-time.

Next: Lesson 6.1 Spanish

Continue to Lección 6.1 (ES) →

ENGLISH
← Previous: Lesson 6.1Course index

Lesson 6.2: OPT, H-1B Lottery, and O-1 Extraordinary Ability

Module 6: Visas & Immigration

Legal Disclaimer

This lesson is educational content only, not legal advice. Immigration law changes frequently and every case is unique. Always consult a licensed immigration attorney before making decisions. Aracely shares her perspective as someone who has navigated the system, but she is not your lawyer. Processing times, visa caps, and policies referenced are accurate as of 2025-2026 but may change. Immigration attorneys are required advisors; "notarios" without legal credentials may commit fraud.

Lesson Objectives

  • Master OPT (Optional Practical Training) eligibility, filing, and STEM extension benefits
  • Understand H-1B visa cap lottery mechanics, odds, and employer requirements
  • Evaluate O-1A extraordinary ability criteria and whether you qualify
  • Compare visa pathways by cost, processing time, and green card probability
  • Map your personal career immigration strategy: F-1 to OPT to green card

The Aerospace Immigration Timeline: Reality Check

Most aerospace professionals from Latin America follow this trajectory: F-1 (2-3 years undergraduate or 2 years graduate) to OPT (12 months, or 36 months for STEM) to H-1B or EB green card sponsorship (5-10 years total from entry to citizenship). This is not a bug; it is the system design. Understanding the timeline reduces anxiety and helps you plan financially and psychologically.

I came on an extraordinary ability pathway. But most engineers you know follow the F-1 to H-1B to EB-2 sequence. That is the pathway. It requires patience. It requires discipline. It requires an immigration attorney. But it works.

OPT (Optional Practical Training): Your Post-Graduation Work Period

What is OPT? After you earn your bachelor's or master's degree on F-1, you can work in the United States for 12 months without an H-1B. This period is called OPT (Optional Practical Training). If your degree is in a STEM field (Science, Technology, Engineering, Mathematics), you can extend OPT for 24 additional months, totaling 36 months. This is one of the most valuable benefits of studying in the US.

OPT eligibility: You must have completed your degree and be authorized to work. Your school submits your OPT application to USCIS through a process called "cap-exempt." You file Form I-765 (Application for Employment Authorization Document) with your school's DSO (Designated School Official). The application costs $410 USD filing fee (as of 2025) and typically processes in 2-4 weeks.

STEM Extension: If your degree is in STEM (most aerospace, electrical, mechanical, software engineering degrees qualify), you are eligible for a 24-month extension. Your employer must support the extension by completing Form I-983 (Training Plan) showing you will receive formal training directly related to your field. The STEM OPT extension also requires that your employer use E-Verify (an employment verification system) and follow I-983 training protocols. Processing typically takes 2-3 months.

OPT work authorization terms: During OPT, you may work for any employer in your field. You do NOT need H-1B sponsorship. You are not locked to one company. However, you are bound by your authorized work period (12 months, or 36 months with STEM extension). After OPT expires, you must either return to your home country, enroll in further study (F-1 reset), or transition to H-1B or other visa sponsorship.

Critical detail: OPT is not a green card. It is temporary work authorization. Many students use the 12-36 month OPT period to gain US work experience, demonstrate expertise, build professional networks, and allow their employers to sponsor them for H-1B or EB-2 green card petitions. This is the strategic purpose of OPT.

Real Case Study: José Hernández, Migrant Farmworker to NASA Astronaut

José M. Hernández is the first migrant farmworker to become a NASA astronaut. He was born in the United States to parents who were field workers in California. Unlike Aracely, José is a US citizen by birth. But his story teaches us about persistence in aerospace careers.

José applied to the NASA astronaut program 11 times before being accepted. Each rejection was a redirection. He earned his PhD in electrical engineering from UC Santa Barbara while working. He flew on STS-128 in 2009 and has logged over 4,000 hours in space. His autobiography, "Reaching for the Stars," and the Netflix film "A Million Miles Away" tell his story.

Why mention José here? Because OPT and H-1B are tools for building the expertise NASA needs. José's pathway was different (US citizen), but the principle is the same: accumulate experience, earn credentials, demonstrate expertise, and the door opens. If you are on OPT working at Aerospace Corp, JPL, or Lockheed, you are doing exactly what José did: building unrefusable expertise.

H-1B Visa: The Specialty Occupation Lottery

What is H-1B? H-1B is a non-immigrant visa for specialty occupations. A specialty occupation requires a bachelor's degree or higher and involves theoretical and practical knowledge in highly specialized fields. Aerospace engineering qualifies. Software engineering qualifies. Data science qualifies. Nursing does not. Truck driving does not.

H-1B cap and lottery: The US government limits H-1B visas to 85,000 per fiscal year (October 1 to September 30): 65,000 regular cap and 20,000 US Masters exemption (H-1B visa holders with a US master's degree or higher are exempt from cap). In 2024, approximately 400,000 applications were filed for 85,000 slots. Your odds of selection in the regular lottery are roughly 20% (65,000 out of 400,000). If you have a US master's degree, the odds are better.

Critical caveat: These are approximate 2024 numbers. Odds fluctuate. Some recent years saw higher application volumes and worse odds. Always check current USCIS H-1B statistics.

H-1B cap-exempt employers: Some employers are exempt from H-1B caps. These include: universities and colleges, nonprofit research organizations, and government research institutions. If you work for a university, nonprofit research lab, or NASA Goddard (where I work), your employer can sponsor you for H-1B outside the cap lottery. Your odds of H-1B approval approach 100% if your employer chooses to sponsor you. This is one reason why NASA and research positions are valuable: cap-exempt sponsorship.

H-1B application process: Your employer files Form I-129 (Petition for Nonimmigrant Worker) with USCIS. If selected in the lottery (or cap-exempt), USCIS processes the petition. Processing typically takes 2-6 months. The petition costs approximately $1,500-$2,500 in USCIS fees and legal fees (if using an attorney, recommended). Your employer covers these costs in nearly all cases.

H-1B duration and renewal: H-1B status lasts 3 years. It can be renewed once for another 3 years, totaling 6 years maximum. During H-1B, you are locked to your sponsoring employer. Changing employers requires a new H-1B petition or employer transfer. You can have dependent family members (spouse, children under 21) join you on H-4 visas, though they cannot work unless on their own H-1B or other work visa.

H-1B to green card connection: H-1B is typically a stepping stone to permanent green card sponsorship. While on H-1B, your employer can sponsor you for an EB-2 (Employment-Based Second Preference) or EB-3 (Employment-Based Third Preference) green card. This process is called "green card sponsorship" or "PERM sponsorship" and is a separate lengthy process (2-5 years or longer).

Real Case Study: Franklin Chang-Díaz, Costa Rica to Harvard to Seven NASA Spaceflights

Franklin Chang-Díaz was born in San José, Costa Rica. He emigrated to the United States at age 18 with $50 in his pocket and spoke only Spanish. He earned his bachelor's degree in mechanical engineering from the University of Hartford and his PhD in plasma physics from MIT. He flew seven Space Shuttle missions (1986-2002), tied for the most spaceflights by any individual. He founded Ad Astra Rocket Company and is developing the VASIMR plasma engine for Mars missions.

Franklin's key quotes on immigration and aerospace: "My accent is not a barrier. My work is my voice." He talks openly about being rejected from programs, changing paths, and persisting. His story demonstrates that accent, language fluency, and even visa status are irrelevant if you produce excellent work.

Why mention Franklin? Because he represents the H-1B to green card to citizenship pathway. Franklin came through the immigration system legally, earned his expertise through US education, worked at NASA on H-1B (then permanent resident), and became a US citizen. He is now a successful entrepreneur. This is the pathway working.

O-1A Visa: Extraordinary Ability Alternative

What is O-1A? O-1A is a non-immigrant visa for individuals with extraordinary ability in their field. It is not as well-known as H-1B, but for some aerospace professionals, it is easier to qualify and does not involve a lottery.

O-1A extraordinary ability criteria (8 criteria; must meet at least 3):

  • Receipt of major national or international awards (Nobel Prize, MacArthur Fellowship, etc.)
  • Membership in associations that require outstanding achievement
  • Published material about you in professional publications
  • Evidence of original scientific, scholarly, or business-related contributions
  • Authorship of scholarly articles in professional journals or publications
  • Critical role in organizations/enterprises with high acclaim or recognition
  • Significant salary or remuneration reflecting extraordinary ability
  • Commercial success in the performing or creative arts

Can engineers qualify? Yes. You do NOT need a Nobel Prize. Here is a realistic breakdown:

  • Published research papers in peer-reviewed aerospace or engineering journals (satisfies "authorship of scholarly articles")
  • Patents issued in your name (satisfies "original contributions")
  • High salary offer or current salary (satisfies "significant salary")
  • Critical role in major aerospace projects or R&D (satisfies "critical role")
  • Membership in professional societies (IEEE, AIAA) (may satisfy "membership" if the society requires achievement)
  • Media coverage of your work (satisfies "published material about you")

O-1A cost and timeline: O-1A petitions cost approximately $2,000-$3,500 with attorney fees. Processing typically takes 3-6 months. O-1A is sponsored by your employer or by a petitioner (e.g., a law firm or advocacy organization). Unlike H-1B, there is no annual cap and no lottery. Every qualified case can be filed.

O-1A duration: O-1A status lasts 3 years and can be renewed indefinitely (as long as you maintain extraordinary ability). Like H-1B, you are typically locked to your sponsoring employer.

O-1A to green card: O-1A can transition to EB-1A (green card for extraordinary ability). This is a more direct path than H-1B to EB-2. However, EB-1A has higher bars than O-1A and typically requires a higher level of recognition. Many O-1A holders eventually pursue EB-2 NIW (National Interest Waiver) as a more achievable green card pathway.

Visa Comparison Table: H-1B vs. O-1A vs. EB-2 NIW vs. EB-2 PERM

Criteria H-1B O-1A EB-2 NIW EB-2 PERM
Qualification bar Bachelor's degree in specialty field Extraordinary ability (3+ criteria) Advanced degree + national interest Employer sponsorship + labor cert
Annual cap 85,000 (65K + 20K Masters) None None None (but EB-2 backlog for India/China)
Lottery Yes (unless cap-exempt) No No No
Employer required Yes Yes (usually) No (self-petition) Yes
Cost (approx.) $1,500-$2,500 $2,000-$3,500 $3,000-$8,000 $5,000-$15,000
Processing time 2-6 months 3-6 months 6-18 months 18-36+ months
Duration 3 years, renewable to 6 years 3 years, indefinitely renewable Permanent Permanent
Green card path EB-2 or EB-3 sponsorship EB-1A or EB-2 NIW Direct green card upon approval Direct green card upon approval
Common for aerospace Yes, very common Less common but viable For PhDs and researchers Yes, common for senior engineers

Career Immigration Strategy: Your 5-10 Year Plan

Typical trajectory (conservative timeline):

  • Year 0-2: F-1 Undergraduate or Master's (or additional degree) - Study, build GPA, start research or internships, network with professors and advisors
  • Year 2-3: OPT (12 months) or STEM OPT (36 months) - Work for major aerospace employer (JPL, SpaceX, Blue Origin, Lockheed, Raytheon, etc.), gain practical experience, publish if possible, earn strong salary
  • Year 3-4: H-1B or O-1A filing - Your employer files H-1B (lottery in March) or O-1A (any time). Assuming success, you begin H-1B or O-1A status
  • Year 4-9: H-1B or O-1A employment + green card application - While on H-1B/O-1A, employer sponsors you for EB-2 green card. Processing takes 2-5 years or longer (depending on your country and employment category)
  • Year 9-10: Green card approval and employment-based green card receipt - You become permanent resident. Path to citizenship opens after 5 years of permanent residence

Accelerated trajectory (if you are cap-exempt): If you are hired by a university, government lab (NASA), or nonprofit research organization, H-1B filing is cap-exempt. You can file H-1B immediately after OPT, without lottery. This compresses the timeline to 3-4 years instead of 3-5.

EB-2 NIW trajectory (PhD holders): If you have a PhD or advanced degree and have done published research, you may qualify for EB-2 NIW (National Interest Waiver). This is a self-petitioning green card pathway that does not require employer sponsorship. It takes 1-3 years and costs less than employer-sponsored EB-2. For aerospace researchers with publication records, NIW is often faster than waiting for H-1B and EB-2 together.

Video Lesson: OPT to H-1B to Green Card Timeline (14 minutes)

[VIDEO EMBEDDED: Aracely walks through real post-graduation visa scenarios, OPT job search strategies, H-1B lottery mechanics, and green card sponsorship options]

Video Script: Dr. Aracely Quispe Neira, From OPT to Permanent Residence

Hello. After graduation, you face the biggest decision of your post-study path: where will you work, and what visa will sponsor you? Let me walk you through the realities.

OPT is your golden ticket. You have 12 months, or 36 months if your degree is STEM. Use this time strategically. Do not bounce between companies. Do not take a job just because it pays well. Take a job at a company that builds aerospace systems, because those companies have the resources and the motivation to sponsor your H-1B or green card later.

The major aerospace companies are: SpaceX, Blue Origin, Lockheed Martin, Raytheon (now RTX), Northrop Grumman, Boeing, General Dynamics. NASA contractors like Aerospace Corp, Axiom Space, Sierra Space. Research labs like NASA JPL, NASA Goddard, NASA Glenn. Universities with aerospace programs. Smaller companies: Vector Launch, Relativity Space, Rocket Lab (now a US company), and others.

Work at one of these companies during your OPT. Build expertise. Publish if you can. Help your manager understand your value. When your OPT is running down (usually in month 10-11 of your 12-month period, or month 34-35 of your 36-month period), your company should initiate H-1B sponsorship.

Here is how to position yourself: In your annual review or directly with your manager, say this: "I love working here. I want to continue contributing to this team. I know my F-1 OPT status will expire in [month]. I would like to discuss H-1B sponsorship for my continued employment." A company that values you will say yes.

If your employer says no, you have a problem. You have 60 days to change status or leave the US. This is serious. So pick your employer carefully during OPT. Pick a big company with HR departments that know how to sponsor visas.

Once your employer files H-1B, you are in the lottery (unless they are cap-exempt). The lottery is held in March for an October 1 start date. If you are selected, congratulations. If not, you have options: file again next year, pursue O-1A, or if you have advanced degree and research, pursue EB-2 NIW.

Let's say you get H-1B. You enter H-1B status on October 1. You can renew H-1B after 3 years for another 3 years, maximum 6 years. While on H-1B, your employer should begin green card sponsorship. Green card sponsorship is a separate process with its own timeline (1-5 years). You can be on H-1B and have a green card application pending simultaneously. This is normal and expected.

What if you do not get H-1B? If you are on STEM OPT (36 months), you have time to file again next year. If you are on regular OPT (12 months), you are in trouble if not selected. Consider:

One: File H-1B again next year if you have STEM OPT extension and time.

Two: Pursue O-1A if you have enough criteria (published papers, patents, high salary, critical role).

Three: Pursue EB-2 NIW if you have advanced degree and research.

Four: Return to your home country and reapply later as experienced professional.

The reality is: H-1B lottery rejection is painful, but it is not the end of your aerospace career. It is a redirect. Many aerospace engineers pursue O-1A instead and find it more stable (no annual lottery, indefinite renewals).

Final word: Get an immigration attorney as soon as you know you will graduate. Do not wait until OPT is expiring. Do not rely on your company's HR to understand your case. Your attorney is your advocate. Attorneys cost money, but the cost of making a visa mistake is deportation and years off your career.

You can do this. Thousands of international aerospace engineers are working in the US right now on H-1B, O-1A, and green cards. You will join them.

Download Resources

  • OPT Employer Selection & Filing Guide (4-page PDF) - How to choose OPT employers, I-765 filing checklist, STEM extension requirements
  • H-1B vs. O-1A Decision Matrix (2-page PDF) - Pros/cons, odds, cost, timeline, when to choose each
  • Complete Visa Pathway Comparison (3-page PDF) - F-1, OPT, H-1B, O-1A, EB-2 NIW, EB-2 PERM side-by-side
  • H-1B Lottery Reality Check (2-page PDF) - 2024-2025 cap numbers, odds, cap-exempt employers list, appeals process

Community Discussion Prompt

If you are on OPT or planning your post-graduation visa strategy: Which pathway are you targeting (H-1B, O-1A, EB-2)? What aerospace companies are you considering? What concerns do you have about the lottery or green card timeline?

Participate and earn 20 XP bonus!

Knowledge Check Quiz (5 questions)

Q1: How many months of OPT does a US graduate with a STEM degree qualify for?
A) 12 months only | B) 24 months | C) 36 months total (12 base + 24 extension) | D) Unlimited
Answer: C) 36 months total (12 base + 24 extension). All F-1 graduates get 12 months of base OPT. STEM degree holders get an additional 24-month extension, totaling 36 months of work authorization.

Q2: Approximately how many H-1B visas are available annually, and how many applications are typically filed?
A) 20,000 available; 50,000 applications | B) 85,000 available; 400,000+ applications | C) 100,000 available; 200,000 applications | D) 50,000 available; 150,000 applications
Answer: B) 85,000 available; 400,000+ applications. The H-1B cap is 85,000 annually (65,000 regular + 20,000 US Masters exemption). In recent years, over 400,000 applications are filed, making the lottery competitive (roughly 20% odds).

Q3: Which employers are exempt from the H-1B cap lottery?
A) All tech companies | B) Universities, nonprofits, and government research institutions | C) Companies with over 500 employees | D) Aerospace companies only
Answer: B) Universities, nonprofits, and government research institutions. H-1B cap-exempt employers include colleges/universities, nonprofit research organizations, and government research institutions (like NASA). These can file H-1B outside the cap, giving virtually 100% approval odds.

Q4: How many of the 8 extraordinary ability criteria must you meet to qualify for O-1A?
A) All 8 | B) At least 6 | C) At least 3 | D) At least 1
Answer: C) At least 3. O-1A requires meeting at least 3 of the 8 extraordinary ability criteria. These can include published papers, patents, awards, critical employment roles, and significant salary.

Q5: Can you change employers while on H-1B status?
A) No, never | B) Yes, freely, like on OPT | C) Yes, but requires a new H-1B petition from the new employer | D) Only if approved by USCIS in advance
Answer: C) Yes, but requires a new H-1B petition from the new employer. While on H-1B, you are locked to your sponsoring employer. Changing employers requires the new employer to file a new H-1B petition and receive approval before you can work for them.

Next: Lesson 6.2 Spanish

ENGLISH

Lesson 6.3: Green Card Pathways, ITAR Restrictions, and Finding an Attorney

Module 6: Visas & Immigration

Legal Disclaimer

This lesson is educational content only, not legal advice. Immigration law changes frequently and every case is unique. Always consult a licensed immigration attorney before making decisions. Aracely shares her perspective as someone who has navigated the system, but she is not your lawyer. ITAR regulations, visa processing, and employment rules referenced are accurate as of 2025-2026 but subject to change. Immigration attorneys are required advisors; "notarios" without legal credentials may commit fraud or cause permanent damage to your case.

Lesson Objectives

  • Understand green card employment-based (EB) categories: EB-1A, EB-1B, EB-2, EB-3
  • Master EB-2 NIW (National Interest Waiver) Matter of Dhanasar 3-prong test
  • Understand ITAR: what it is, who can work on ITAR projects, civil service vs. contractor distinction
  • Know the difference between NASA civil service (must be citizen) and NASA contractor roles
  • Find a qualified immigration attorney and avoid fraud
  • Understand priority dates and visa bulletin for LATAM countries (minimal backlog)

My Path to Permanent Residence

I came through an extraordinary ability pathway, which is rare. But I want to walk you through the more common pathways because most aerospace engineers you know will use them. Green card sponsorship is a process, not magic. It takes 2-5 years after your employer or you initiate it. It costs money. It requires an attorney. But at the end, you have permanent residence, and that changes everything.

Permanent residence means: you can change jobs freely, you can start your own company, you can travel in and out of the US without worrying about visa expiration, you can sponsor family members to immigrate, and you are on the path to citizenship (5 more years). This is the goal. This is what we are working toward.

Employment-Based Green Card Categories

There are four primary employment-based green card categories (EB-1, EB-2, EB-3, EB-4). Aerospace engineers typically use EB-1A, EB-2 NIW, or EB-2 PERM.

EB-1A (Extraordinary Ability): Green card for individuals with extraordinary ability in their field. This is the rarest and most prestigious. You must demonstrate extraordinary ability at a level above O-1A. Many academics, researchers, and some high-profile engineers qualify. Example: a researcher with multiple patents, international recognition, and significant publications.

EB-1B (Outstanding Professor or Researcher): Green card for outstanding professors or researchers with at least 3 years of experience. Typically used for academics at universities or senior researchers at labs.

EB-2 NIW (National Interest Waiver): Green card for professionals with advanced degree (master's or PhD) whose work is in the national interest. This is the most common pathway for aerospace engineers with advanced degrees. Unlike EB-2 PERM, you do NOT need an employer to petition for you. You can self-petition. The "national interest" includes space exploration, defense technology, climate research, and many other fields where US interests are served. The test is called "Matter of Dhanasar" and has three prongs (see below).

EB-2 PERM (Permanent Labor Certification): Green card for professionals with bachelor's degree or advanced degree, sponsored by an employer. Your employer must show they tried to recruit US workers first and could not find qualified candidates. This is labor certification. It is lengthy (1-3 years) but very common. Most H-1B workers eventually pursue EB-2 PERM with their employer.

EB-3 (Skilled Worker): Green card for skilled workers with bachelor's degree. Similar to EB-2 PERM but with lower priority. Less common for aerospace professionals.

EB-2 NIW: Matter of Dhanasar 3-Prong Test

Matter of Dhanasar is a 2016 USCIS precedent that defined the test for EB-2 NIW eligibility. It replaced older, stricter criteria with a more flexible three-part test. You must prove all three prongs:

Prong 1: Merit Your proposed endeavor has substantial merit and national importance. Your field (aerospace engineering, climate research, space exploration, propulsion systems, etc.) must be important to the US. This is usually easy for aerospace engineers because NASA, space commerce, and defense are core national interests. Evidence: professional credentials, degrees, publications, patents, job responsibilities showing work in nationally important field.

Prong 2: Well Positioned to Advance You are well positioned to advance the proposed endeavor. Your credentials, experience, and expertise must demonstrate you can contribute meaningfully. Evidence: CV, publications, patents, letters of recommendation from colleagues, job offers, descriptions of your specific projects and contributions.

Prong 3: Waiver Benefiting US Granting the waiver (exempting you from labor certification requirement) is beneficial to the US. This means your work will benefit the US and the labor certification waiver will not harm US workers. Since you are in a specialized field with few competitors, this is usually provable. Evidence: letter from employer explaining your work's impact, letters from government agencies (NASA, DoD, NOAA) supporting your work, publications showing your contributions, job market analysis showing shortage of qualified professionals in your specialty.

Practical example: An aerospace engineer with a master's degree who has published research on hypersonic propulsion systems, holds patents in guidance systems, and works for a NASA contractor on a critical space mission can argue: (1) Hypersonic propulsion has substantial national importance (prong 1); (2) I have directly contributed to this field through research and patents (prong 2); (3) Waiving labor certification and allowing me to remain in the US serves national interest because few engineers have my specialized knowledge (prong 3).

Real Case Study: Emiliano Kargieman, Argentina to NASDAQ-Listed Space CEO

Emiliano Kargieman is an Argentine aerospace engineer who founded Satellogic in 2010. Satellogic is a leading Latin American space technology company focused on satellite Earth observation. The company launched its first satellite in 2015 and has since launched 50+ nanosatellites. In 2022, Satellogic went public on NASDAQ.

Emiliano's pathway: Argentine engineer, started a company in Buenos Aires, scaled it to US presence, eventually pursued US permanent residence while building the company. His story demonstrates that LATAM aerospace professionals do not have to work for NASA or major US contractors. They can build their own companies and use immigration pathways to scale operations.

Why mention Emiliano? Because EB-2 NIW is particularly attractive for entrepreneurs and innovators. If you have a startup or idea that serves national interest, you can petition for yourself. You do not have to wait for employer sponsorship.

ITAR (International Traffic in Arms Regulations): What You Must Know

What is ITAR? ITAR is a set of US government regulations (codified in the International Traffic in Arms Regulations, Title 22 Code of Federal Regulations, Part 120-130) that controls the export and transfer of defense and space-related technology. ITAR is managed by the US Department of State Directorate of Defense Trade Controls (DDTC).

Who is subject to ITAR? US persons are subject to ITAR. "US persons" includes: US citizens and lawful permanent residents (green card holders). Non-citizens without permanent residency (including F-1 students and H-1B visa holders) are NOT subject to ITAR, BUT companies may restrict them from accessing ITAR-controlled information because of compliance burden.

What work is ITAR-controlled? Any work involving design, development, manufacture, or use of defense articles or items on the US Munitions List (USML). For aerospace, this includes: satellite propulsion systems, guidance and navigation systems, launch vehicle technology, missile systems, military aircraft systems, spacecraft with defense applications. Many civilian space programs (like commercial satellite work) are also ITAR-controlled.

Critical implication: If you are an F-1 student or H-1B visa holder, you generally CANNOT work on ITAR projects. Many aerospace companies are ITAR-licensed, which means most of their work requires ITAR compliance. Your employment options at these companies may be restricted until you get a green card (permanent resident status).

NASA civil service vs. NASA contractor: NASA Goddard (where I work) is a federal civil service facility. Civil service positions require US citizenship. You CANNOT be a NASA civil service employee (astronaut, flight director, engineer in civil service role) without US citizenship. However, NASA contracts work out to contractors (Axiom Space, Northrop Grumman, Lockheed Martin, etc.). Contractors can employ H-1B visa holders and sometimes permanent residents on non-ITAR or ITAR-approved work. ITAR applies to contractors too, but contractor roles may have more flexibility than civil service.

How to navigate ITAR as a non-citizen:

  • During F-1 OPT: Target companies that do non-ITAR work or have non-ITAR divisions. Research the company's ITAR licensing status before accepting a job.
  • During H-1B: Ask your employer explicitly: "Does my role involve ITAR work?" If yes, ask what restrictions apply. Some companies may petition for green card sponsorship to allow ITAR work transition.
  • Once you have green card (permanent resident): You become a "US person" and can work on ITAR projects without restriction.

Red flag: If a company offers you a job at a high salary and then suddenly says you cannot work on ITAR after you start, you have a problem. Discuss ITAR explicitly before accepting offers.

Priority Dates and Visa Bulletin: Why LATAM Countries Have an Advantage

What is a priority date? For employment-based green cards, USCIS assigns a "priority date" based on when your employer or you file the initial application (I-140 for EB, or I-485 for certain categories). Your priority date determines when you can file your green card application.

What is visa bulletin? The US Department of State publishes a "visa bulletin" monthly showing which priority dates are currently "current" (meaning you can file or be approved). The bulletin varies by employment category and country of origin.

Country backlog: Some countries, notably India and China, have massive backlogs. Engineers from India can wait 5-10+ years for EB-2 green cards because so many Indian engineers are petitioning. China also has backlogs. However, most LATAM countries (Mexico, Peru, Colombia, Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Costa Rica, etc.) have minimal or no backlog. As of 2025, engineers from most LATAM countries can move through EB-2 green card process in 2-5 years, compared to 10+ years for Indian engineers.

Implication: If you are an aerospace engineer from LATAM and you pursue EB-2 green card (either NIW or PERM), you will likely get your green card 3-5 years after initiating. This is a major advantage and one reason why LATAM engineers should NOT hesitate to pursue permanent residence.

How to Find a Qualified Immigration Attorney

Why you need an attorney: Do not try to navigate visa sponsorship alone. Even one mistake can delay your case years or result in denial. Your attorney is your advocate, your advisor, and your insurance policy. The cost ($3,000-$10,000) is small compared to the value of permanent residence.

Where to find attorneys:

  • AILA (American Immigration Lawyers Association): www.ailalawyer.com. Search by location and specialization. AILA attorneys are vetted and ethical.
  • State bar association: Search "[your state] bar association immigration lawyers" to find attorneys licensed in your state.
  • Company HR: Ask your company HR for referrals. Large aerospace companies have relationships with immigration law firms.
  • Professional network: Ask colleagues and mentors for referrals to attorneys they have used.

What to look for in an attorney:

  • Licensed to practice immigration law (check bar admission).
  • Experience with employment-based green cards (EB-1, EB-2, H-1B).
  • Experience with your industry (aerospace, tech, research) is a plus.
  • Clear fee structure (hourly or flat fee).
  • Responsive communication (replies to emails/calls within 24-48 hours).
  • References or reviews from past clients.

Red flags:

  • "Notarios" or people claiming to be immigration consultants WITHOUT a law license. In many Latin American countries, "notario" means attorney. In the US, notaries are NOT attorneys and CANNOT provide legal advice. Notarios can commit fraud and ruin your case.
  • Attorneys offering guarantees ("100% approval guaranteed"). No attorney can guarantee outcomes.
  • Attorneys asking you to pay in cash or offshore accounts. Legitimate attorneys invoice normally.
  • Attorneys rushing you to file or pressuring you. Good attorneys explain options and let you decide.
  • Attorneys who do not respond to questions or seem disorganized.

Initial consultation: Most immigration attorneys offer a 30-minute initial consultation free or for a small fee ($100-300). Use this to: (1) Explain your situation and ask if the attorney can help; (2) Ask about fees and timeline; (3) Get a sense of whether you are comfortable with them. You should feel confident in your attorney.

Video Lesson: Green Card Strategy and Attorney Guidance (15 minutes)

[VIDEO EMBEDDED: Aracely walks through green card categories, EB-2 NIW Dhanasar test, ITAR implications, and how to choose an immigration attorney]

Video Script: Dr. Aracely Quispe Neira, Green Card and ITAR Realities

Hello. We are now at the final and most critical stage: permanent residence. This is your goal. This is what everything else has been building toward.

Let me be direct: do not navigate green card sponsorship alone. You need an immigration attorney. I cannot stress this enough. The cost is worth it. The protection is worth it. One mistake can set you back years.

There are several green card pathways. Let me walk through them.

If you have an advanced degree (master's or PhD) and have done published research or have patents, EB-2 NIW is your golden ticket. You can petition for yourself. You do not need your employer to sponsor you. The barrier is the "Matter of Dhanasar" test: your work must have national importance, you must be well positioned to advance it, and the waiver must benefit the US. For aerospace engineers, this is usually provable. Why? Because space exploration, propulsion systems, and satellite technology ARE nationally important. Because you have published or worked on critical projects. Because waiving labor certification to keep you in the US serves the national interest.

If you do not have an advanced degree, EB-2 PERM is the typical path. Your employer sponsors you. They complete labor certification (showing they tried to hire US workers first). Then they petition for your green card. This takes 2-5 years but is very common.

Now, the complication: ITAR. International Traffic in Arms Regulations. These are rules about who can work on defense and space technology. The rule is simple: only US persons (citizens and permanent residents) can work on ITAR-controlled projects.

What does this mean? If you are an F-1 student or H-1B visa holder and you work for a company that does ITAR work, you may not be able to work on the most interesting projects. You will be assigned to non-ITAR work, or your role will be limited. This is compliant with law, but it is frustrating.

Once you get your green card, you become a US person. You can work on any ITAR project. This is another reason green card is so important: it unlocks your career ceiling in aerospace.

At NASA Goddard, where I work, there is a distinction: civil service jobs require US citizenship. Civil service astronauts, flight directors, engineers, all must be citizens. But I work for a facility that contracts out. Contractor roles can employ permanent residents on some work. As your immigration attorney would tell you, the rules are complex and situation-dependent.

Here is what you do: (1) Early in your OPT or H-1B, talk to your company about green card sponsorship. Most major aerospace companies do this routinely. (2) Find an immigration attorney immediately. Do not wait. Get a referral from your HR or from AILA. (3) With your attorney, decide between EB-2 NIW (if you have advanced degree) or EB-2 PERM (if your employer sponsors). (4) File your green card petition. (5) Be patient. The process takes 2-5 years, but you are building your case the entire time.

Finally, a warning about notarios and fraud. In Latin America, a notario is a professional. In the US, a notary is a person who stamps documents. Notaries are NOT lawyers. People calling themselves "immigration notarios" in the US are committing fraud. They can destroy your case. They can get you deported. Use only licensed attorneys.

You have come so far: F-1 to OPT to H-1B. The final step is green card. You can do this. Thousands of international aerospace engineers have done it. You will too.

Download Resources

  • EB-2 NIW Petition Outline & Dhanasar Test Prep (5-page PDF) - Matter of Dhanasar three-prong explanation, evidence requirements, sample language, timeline
  • ITAR Basics for Aerospace Professionals (3-page PDF) - What ITAR is, who it applies to, common ITAR-controlled projects, how to ask employers about ITAR restrictions
  • Immigration Attorney Interview & Vetting Guide (3-page PDF) - Questions to ask, red flags, fee negotiation, how to assess competence
  • Green Card Timeline & Process Overview (2-page PDF) - EB-2 NIW vs. EB-2 PERM timelines, priority dates, visa bulletin explanation, country-of-origin backlog status for LATAM countries
  • How to Find an AILA Attorney (1-page PDF) - Step-by-step AILA search, reading bar associations, checking credentials

Community Discussion Prompt

Share your green card strategy or questions: Are you pursuing EB-2 NIW or EB-2 PERM? Have you started working with an immigration attorney? What concerns do you have about ITAR or the green card timeline?

Participate and earn 20 XP bonus!

Knowledge Check Quiz (5 questions)

Q1: Which green card category allows self-petitioning (no employer required)?
A) EB-2 PERM | B) EB-2 NIW (National Interest Waiver) | C) EB-3 | D) None; all require employers
Answer: B) EB-2 NIW (National Interest Waiver). EB-2 NIW is unique because you can self-petition. You do not need an employer. This is valuable for researchers, innovators, and professionals with advanced degrees who have contributed to nationally important work.

Q2: What is the three-prong test for EB-2 NIW called?
A) PERM Test | B) Matter of Dhanasar | C) USCIS Standard | D) National Interest Protocol
Answer: B) Matter of Dhanasar. Matter of Dhanasar is a 2016 USCIS precedent case that defined the current, more flexible test for EB-2 NIW. It requires proving: (1) merit/national importance, (2) well positioned to advance, (3) waiver benefits US.

Q3: Who can work on ITAR-controlled projects?
A) Any visa holder | B) F-1 students with CPT authorization | C) US persons (citizens and permanent residents) | D) H-1B visa holders
Answer: C) US persons (citizens and permanent residents). ITAR regulations restrict ITAR-controlled work to US persons. F-1 students, H-1B visa holders, and other non-citizens are legally barred from ITAR work. Permanent residents (green card holders) are considered US persons and can work on ITAR projects.

Q4: Can you work as a civil service astronaut or flight director at NASA Goddard without US citizenship?
A) Yes, with H-1B | B) Yes, with permanent residence | C) No, civil service requires citizenship | D) Yes, if the role is non-ITAR
Answer: C) No, civil service requires citizenship. NASA civil service positions (including astronauts, flight directors, civil service engineers) require US citizenship. Non-citizens can work for NASA contractors on contractor roles, but not in civil service.

Q5: Approximately how long does EB-2 green card sponsorship typically take for an engineer from Peru or Colombia?
A) 1-2 years | B) 2-5 years | C) 8-10 years | D) 15+ years
Answer: B) 2-5 years. Most LATAM countries (Peru, Colombia, Mexico, Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Costa Rica) have minimal backlog for EB-2 green cards. Processing typically takes 2-5 years from initial petition filing to green card approval. This is much faster than India or China, which can take 10-15+ years.
