Lesson 7.3: Networking, Interviews, and Salary Negotiation

Module 7: Build Your NASA Resume

Lesson Objectives

  • Master cold LinkedIn outreach: five templates with high response rates
  • Understand the power of referrals in aerospace hiring
  • Conduct informational interviews to build network and learn insider knowledge
  • Navigate interview stages: recruiter screen, hiring manager, technical panel, behavioral
  • Use STAR method to answer behavioral questions with aerospace examples
  • Master salary negotiation: know your market, always counter, understand leveling systems

The Referral Advantage: 70% of Aerospace Hires Come Through Networks

Cold applying to a job posting is a numbers game. You compete with hundreds of candidates. Your resume waits in an online portal. A recruiter screens it in 6 seconds. Your chance of getting an interview: maybe 2%.

Getting referred is different. When someone inside the company says, "I know someone for this role," the hiring manager looks at your resume first, before the stack. Your chance of getting an interview: 40-50%. This is why referrals are game-changers.

The challenge: you may not know anyone in aerospace yet. Here's how to build those connections from zero.

Five Cold LinkedIn Outreach Templates (High Response Rate)

These templates have a 25-40% response rate when personalized. The key is specificity: mention something about them or their work, not a form letter.

Scenario Template
Referral Ask (Warm) "Hi [Name], I'm [Your Name], engineer at [Your Company]. I saw your role in propulsion at SpaceX and your AIAA paper on thermodynamic cycles. I'm interested in moving into newspace propulsion work. Would you have 15 minutes for a call about what it's like building rocket engines? I'm not asking for a job, just insights. [Your LinkedIn]"
Informational Interview Ask "Hi [Name], I'm [Your Name], working toward a career in guidance and control systems. I followed your work on the Artemis GNC architecture. Would you be open to a 20-minute informational interview? I'd love to understand your path into NASA and what skills are critical for GNC engineering. Happy to work around your schedule. Thanks, [Your Name]."
Direct Job Ask (If opening exists) "Hi [Name], I'm [Your Name]. I saw the structures engineer opening on USAJOBS for Goddard. My background is FEA analysis in NASTRAN, thermal stress on composite materials, and 3 years propulsion component design. Your team's work on JWST structures interests me deeply. Would you consider recommending my application? [Link] Happy to discuss. [Your Name]."
Conference Follow-up "Hi [Name], great meeting you at the AIAA SciTech conference last week. I enjoyed hearing about Blue Origin's reusability strategy, particularly your point about thermal protection systems. I'd like to stay in touch as I build my career in aerospace. Let's connect on LinkedIn? [Your Name]."
Re-engagement After 6 Months "Hi [Name], it's [Your Name] from [Company]. We connected 6 months ago about GNC systems. I've completed a CFD certification and led a new thermal analysis project. I'm still interested in exploring opportunities at [Company Name]. Are you open to a quick call? [Your Name]."

Keys to success: Keep it short (3-4 sentences max). Show you know their work (cite a paper, project, or accomplishment). Ask for 15-20 minutes, not a job. Be specific about why you're interested (not "aerospace is cool"). Don't be salesy. Follow up after 1 week if no response.

Informational Interviews: 15 Minutes to Build Trust

An informational interview is a conversation with someone working in a role or company you're interested in. You're not asking for a job. You're learning. But if you're smart, smart and genuine, they'll often refer you when an opening comes up.

  • Set expectations upfront: "I'm hoping to spend 15 minutes learning about your path into aerospace and what skills matter most in your role."
  • Ask genuine questions: "What was your first aerospace job, and how did you get it?" "What surprised you about working at NASA vs. private?" "What skills did you wish you had learned in school?"
  • Listen more than you talk. They're the expert. You're learning. Take notes.
  • Don't ask for a job in the interview. It breaks the trust. If they want to refer you, they will volunteer it at the end.
  • End with gratitude and ask for one connection: "Thank you for the insights. Would you mind introducing me to [someone else at your company/in your network] who does [specific role]?"
  • Send a follow-up email within 24 hours. Thank them, reference something specific from your conversation, and remind them you're open to referrals if opportunities arise.

Interview Stages: What Happens at Each Step

Aerospace hiring typically involves 3-5 interview rounds. Understand what each one is testing so you can prepare strategically.

  • Round 1: Recruiter Screen (30-45 min, phone/video)
    Goal: Verify your resume is accurate and you're genuinely interested. Recruiter is checking GPA, degree, work history, and interest in the role. This is easy if you're prepared. Questions: "Walk me through your resume." "Why are you interested in this role?" "What's your salary expectation?" Be honest, be enthusiastic, be concise.
  • Round 2: Hiring Manager (45-60 min, usually video)
    Goal: Does your background match what they need? This manager will be your boss. They're assessing technical knowledge and fit. Questions focus on your projects: "Tell me about a design you led." "How did you handle conflict on a team?" Come with 2-3 project stories prepared. Use STAR method (see below).
  • Round 3: Technical Panel (60-90 min, usually in-person or video with 2-3 engineers)
    Goal: Can you actually do the work? They'll ask technical questions specific to the role. Propulsion roles get thermodynamics questions. Structures roles get FEA questions. GNC roles get control systems math. You can't fake this. Know your domain cold. Use a whiteboard or paper if they ask you to sketch or solve a problem.
  • Round 4: Behavioral/Systems Design (60 min, usually with senior engineer or manager)
    Goal: How do you think about complex problems? They may give you a simplified engineering problem and ask you to work through it. Example: "How would you optimize a satellite solar panel for thermal constraints?" There's no perfect answer. They're listening to your problem-solving approach, assumptions, and communication.
  • Round 5: Final Round or Committee (30-45 min, executive/director level)
    Goal: Culture fit and long-term potential. At this stage, technical fit is confirmed. They want to know if you align with company values and if you can grow into bigger roles. Questions are about your career vision, leadership philosophy, and what success looks like to you.

Behavioral Interviews: STAR Method with Aerospace Examples

STAR stands for Situation, Task, Action, Result. It's a framework for answering behavioral questions. Here are three real aerospace-style examples you should adapt to your own experience.

Example 1: Technical Conflict

Question: "Tell me about a time you disagreed with a teammate on a technical decision."

STAR Answer:
Situation: "I was on the propulsion subsystem team designing a rocket engine nozzle. Our manufacturing engineer wanted to use a simpler throat geometry to reduce cost. Our thermal analyst said it would exceed temperature limits."
Task: "I needed to find a solution that satisfied both constraints, manufacturability and thermal safety."
Action: "I proposed parametric FEA analysis in ANSYS. We tested 8 geometry configurations to find the boundary where thermal performance met manufacturing tolerance. I owned the analysis and presented findings to both stakeholders."
Result: "We found a solution that was 92% as thermally efficient as the ideal design but 40% cheaper to manufacture. The team built confidence because the decision was data-driven, not opinion-driven."

Example 2: Missed Deadline Under Pressure

Question: "Describe a time you missed a deadline or failed at something. How did you handle it?"

STAR Answer:
Situation: "I was assigned to write a critical design review (CDR) supporting document on guidance and control algorithms. The deadline was 4 weeks away. I underestimated the complexity."
Task: "At week 2, I realized I was behind and would miss the deadline. The CDR was scheduled with stakeholders from 5 organizations."
Action: "I escalated immediately to my manager. We brought in a senior engineer to review my draft and identify gaps. I worked weekends to incorporate feedback. I also communicated weekly to stakeholders about status, so there were no surprises. The document shipped 3 days late, but all stakeholders approved it on first review."
Result: "The delay cost 3 days, but preventing a failed review would have cost 3 weeks. I learned to break complex documents into smaller checkpoints earlier. My manager appreciated the transparency and now uses my approach as a team standard."

Example 3: Difficult Stakeholder

Question: "Tell me about a time you had to work with someone difficult or address a conflict."

STAR Answer:
Situation: "Our team was integrating a guidance algorithm into flight software. The software lead wanted to implement it his way. I, as the guidance engineer, knew it wouldn't pass our thermal margin requirements."
Task: "I had to convince him to use a different approach without stepping on his authority. We were both junior engineers, and neither of us wanted to escalate."
Action: "I asked for a 1-on-1 conversation, not a meeting. I acknowledged his expertise in software architecture. I explained the thermal constraint clearly with analysis data. I asked, 'What if we explore an implementation that meets your architectural goals and our thermal requirements?' We brainstormed together. He came up with a solution I hadn't considered."
Result: "We implemented the solution together. It became a standard approach for future integrations. We became a stronger team because we solved it collaboratively instead of letting ego block progress."

Pattern: Every STAR story shows problem-solving, accountability, and collaboration. It shows you handle pressure without blame. It shows data drives decisions. Write down 3 of your own stories now, before interviews. Memorize them so you can adapt them to different questions.

Salary Negotiation: Know Your Market, Always Counter, Understand Levels

Most people accept the first offer. Don't. A counteroffer of 10-15% is standard and expected in aerospace. Companies budget for it.

Salary Band Research: Before interviews, know the market:

  • Glassdoor: Search company + role, filter by location. Look at 50-75th percentile range (not the average).
  • Levels.fyi: Aerospace company salary data with stock, bonus, and signing bonus. Updated by employees.
  • PayScale: Another source for median and range comparisons by company and role.
  • USAJOBS equivalent: For federal positions, know GS grade salary ranges. GS-7 to GS-9 is $47K to $63K depending on location. Non-negotiable.

Leveling Systems (Know These):

Company Entry Level Mid Level Senior Level Typical Salary Range
SpaceX L3 (entry) L4 (mid) L5+ (senior) L3: $110K-$140K | L4: $140K-$180K | L5: $180K-$250K+
Blue Origin I-II III-IV V-VI I: $105K-$135K | III: $145K-$175K | V: $200K-$280K+
Rocket Lab IC2 IC3 IC4+ IC2: $100K-$135K | IC3: $135K-$170K | IC4: $170K-$220K+
Lockheed Martin L1-L2 L3-L4 L5-L6 L2: $90K-$120K | L4: $140K-$170K | L6: $200K-$280K+
NASA (Federal) GS-7 GS-9/11 GS-12/13 GS-7: $52K | GS-9: $63K | GS-11: $78K | GS-13: $115K (2026 estimates)

Negotiation Script:

"Thank you for the offer. I'm excited about the role and the team. Before I accept, I want to make sure the compensation reflects market rates for this level and location. Based on my research on Levels.fyi and Glassdoor, the range for [Level/GS-grade] in [Location] is [Your Research Range]. Could we discuss moving the base salary to [15% higher number] and also explore the bonus/equity terms?" Always be respectful, data-driven, and collaborative. Framing it as "making sure we're fair to both sides" works better than "I want more money."

Aracely's Perspective: Most immigrants and Latinas significantly undersell themselves. We're taught humility. In the U.S., negotiation is expected. It's not impolite. It's how markets work. Get your number in writing before negotiating. A verbal offer can change. A written offer is your baseline. If they won't budge on salary, negotiate signing bonus, stock vesting schedule, flexible work, or extra vacation days. Always trade something.

Video Lesson: From Rejection to Referral

Aracely speaks (first person, 1150 words):

"I was rejected from NASA 11 times before I got my first job there. Not 11 times from different centers. 11 times from Goddard alone. Each time, a cold application through USAJOBS. Each time, I waited 3 weeks for a form letter. 'Thank you for your interest. You were not selected.'

"Then I realized: I was playing the game wrong. I was treating NASA like a meritocracy where the best technical skills win. It's not. It's a network. The best candidates don't come through USAJOBS. They come through a person inside who says, 'I know someone.'

"So I changed my strategy completely. I stopped cold applying. I started reaching out to people.

"My first connection was at a conference. I attended an AIAA SciTech conference, and there was a panel on TRMM mission operations. One panelist was a flight director at Goddard. I went to the booth after the session and said, 'Your work on tropical rainfall measurement is exactly what I want to do.' We talked for 5 minutes. I asked, 'Would you mind if I stayed in touch?' She said yes.

"Six weeks later, I sent her an email: 'I still remember your talk on TRMM. I'm very interested in mission operations. Do you have 15 minutes for a call?' She said yes. We talked 20 minutes. She asked about my background. I told her. At the end, she said, 'Have you applied to Goddard?' I said, 'Yes, three times. Rejected each time.' She said, 'Apply again. And when you do, send me your resume. I'll pass it to the hiring manager.'

"I applied. She referred me. The hiring manager knew my name before my resume came through the system. That changed everything.

"That's the power of referral. The system is broken if you don't use it. You can be brilliant, but if you rely only on online forms, you're at a disadvantage.

"Here's my advice:

"First, go to conferences. AIAA SciTech, ASCE, IEEE, whatever is relevant to your field. You don't have to be fancy. You don't have to talk much. Just show up, listen, and after a talk, find the speaker. Say what you learned. Ask if they'll stay in touch. Most will say yes.

"Second, reach out to 2-3 people per week on LinkedIn. Keep it short. Make it personal. Reference their work. Ask for 15 minutes. Expect a 20-30% response rate. That's normal. Don't get discouraged if someone doesn't reply.

"Third, when they agree to talk, prepare. Know their background. Know their company. Know what you want to learn. Go in with 3-4 genuine questions. Not 'what's it like working at NASA?' More like, 'I saw you work on the GNC architecture for Artemis. What was the biggest constraint you had to design around?' Specific. Curious. Respectful.

"Fourth, listen. Don't pitch. Don't ask for a job in the first call. Just listen. Take notes. Ask follow-up questions. At the end, say, 'This was incredibly helpful. Is there anyone else in your network I should talk to?' Most people will introduce you.

"That's how you build a network: one genuine conversation at a time.

"Then when there's an opening, and there will be, your contact refers you. The hiring manager gets your resume with a note from inside: 'This person knows what they're doing. I talked to them. They're serious.' That changes your odds from 2% to 40%.

"Now, when you get an offer, here's the second lesson: negotiate.

"My first NASA offer was GS-9. They said, 'This is the standard entry level for someone with your degree and experience.' I said, 'Thank you. Can we discuss GS-11?' I had researched. I knew GS-11 was standard for someone with 3 years aerospace work. I had 5 years. They said, 'Let me check with HR.' Two days later, they said, 'Approved. GS-11.'

"That single negotiation moved me from $63K to $78K. Over a 30-year federal career, that's a difference of nearly $1 million.

"Why did they say yes? Because it was data-driven. I wasn't being emotional. I wasn't demanding. I was saying, 'This is what the GS scale shows for my qualifications.' They respected that.

"Most women I know, and especially Latinas, don't negotiate. We're taught it's impolite. It's not. Negotiation is how markets work. Companies expect it. They budget for it. If you don't negotiate, you're leaving money on the table.

"Here's what I tell young engineers: Get your offer in writing. Read it carefully. Look up the role on Levels.fyi or Glassdoor. Know what market rate is. Then counter with a number 10-15% higher. That's normal. Say, 'I'm excited about this opportunity. My research shows the market range for this level is X to Y. Could we move to X plus 10%?' Polite. Professional. Data-driven.

"If they say no to salary, ask for other things. Higher signing bonus. Flexible work location. Extra vacation. Stock vesting accelerated. They have levers besides salary.

"If they push back hard, be prepared to walk. If a company won't budge on fair market compensation, that tells you something about how they treat people. You don't want to work there.

"But here's the thing most negotiating advice doesn't tell you: walk away feeling good. If you negotiated fairly and they came up, thank them. If they didn't, and you decided to accept anyway, that's your choice. But own it. Don't build resentment.

"I negotiated my way from $63K to $78K on my first job. Ten years later, I'm a flight director making six figures. That $15K difference in the first job mattered. But the relationships I built, through genuine outreach and referrals, mattered more. That's what got me the opportunities.

"So: reach out to people. Have real conversations. Let them refer you. Then negotiate fairly. You'll start strong. And if you keep growing, keep learning, keep collaborating, you'll keep moving up."

Download: Interview Prep Packet and Salary Negotiation Scripts

STAR method templates, behavioral interview questions by role, and salary negotiation scripts with talking points.

Download Interview Prep Packet (PDF, 1.2 MB)

Download Salary Negotiation Scripts (PDF, 640 KB)

Community Discussion Prompt

Share your own STAR story (Situation, Task, Action, Result) about a technical challenge you overcame or a conflict you resolved. Practice answering interview questions in front of the community. Give peers feedback on clarity and impact. Earn 30 XP for detailed sharing and peer feedback.

Knowledge Check Quiz

Q1: What percentage of aerospace hires come through referrals vs. cold applications?

A) 30% | B) 50% | C) 70% | D) 90%

Answer: C) 70%
Research shows roughly 70% of aerospace hires come through employee referrals or internal networks. This means cold applications, while still viable, are lower-odds plays. Building your network is ROI.

Q2: In an informational interview, when should you ask for a job referral?

A) At the beginning to set expectations | B) In the middle to test interest | C) At the end if they volunteer it | D) In a follow-up email

Answer: C) At the end if they volunteer it
An informational interview is built on trust, not transaction. If you ask for a referral upfront, it breaks trust. If the conversation is genuine and they see potential, they'll offer to refer you. If they don't, respect that and ask if they'll introduce you to someone else.

Q3: Which interview stage tests whether you can actually do the technical work?

A) Recruiter screen | B) Hiring manager round | C) Technical panel | D) Final leadership round

Answer: C) Technical panel
The technical panel (usually 2-3 senior engineers) is where they verify you have the domain expertise. Recruiter screen checks background. Hiring manager checks fit. Technical panel checks competence. You cannot fake this. Study your field.

Q4: What does STAR stand for in behavioral interview preparation?

A) Skills, Training, Aptitude, Reasoning | B) Situation, Task, Action, Result | C) Strategy, Teamwork, Attitude, Resilience | D) Salary, Time, Achievement, Recognition

Answer: B) Situation, Task, Action, Result
STAR is a framework for answering behavioral questions. It ensures your story is complete, relevant, and shows problem-solving and accountability. Practice writing 3 STAR stories from your own experience before interviews.

Q5: In salary negotiation, what is a standard counteroffer percentage if the initial offer is below market?

A) 3-5% | B) 10-15% | C) 25-30% | D) 40-50%

Answer: B) 10-15%
A counteroffer of 10-15% is standard and expected in professional aerospace roles. Companies budget for negotiation. Always counter with data (Levels.fyi, Glassdoor) to justify your ask. If they won't move on salary, negotiate other terms.

Complete quiz: +50 XP | Perfect score: +25 XP bonus

Watch Video: +50 XP | Complete Quiz: +50 XP | Discussion: +30 XP | Total: 130 XP

Module 7 Complete: You Have a Hiring-Ready Resume and Strategy

You now understand federal and private resume formats, LinkedIn optimization, networking, interview rounds, and salary negotiation. Your next step: pick one aerospace employer, research one open role, build your network there, and apply. Then apply to five more. The math of job search: apply to 20, get 2-3 interviews, get 1 offer. You're statistically likely to be hired if you follow this process.

Module 8: Mission Launched
ENGLISH
← Previous: Lesson 7.3Course index

Lesson 8.1: Career Paths in Space Industry

Module 8: Mission Launch

Lesson Objectives

  • Map 10 core career tracks and understand realistic timelines, salaries, and progression
  • Learn Dr. Aracely's track-switching strategy from engineering to flight operations
  • Identify 6 emerging roles in space 2026-2030
  • Compare environments: government, contractor, newspace, startup
  • Understand visa sponsorship and recruitment pathways for international talent

10 Core Career Paths

PathEntryYears to Senior2025 Salary
AstronautEngineer/Pilot, 1000+ flight hours15-20$200,000+
Flight ControllerEngineering degree, Mission Control training8-12$155,000
Mission SpecialistTechnical background, 2-3 years hands-on7-10$148,000
Propulsion EngineerMechanical engineering, test-stand experience8-12$155,000
Systems EngineerTechnical degree, cross-functional projects8-12$158,000
Aerospace ResearcherPhD (5 years), postdoc (2-3 years)12-18$180,000
NASA Program ManagerTechnical background, management certs10-15$165,000
Space Policy AnalystTechnical or policy background6-10$125,000
Satellite OperationsEE or CS, orbit mechanics6-8$138,000
Spacecraft DesignerAerospace engineering, CAD, subsystems8-11$150,000

All these roles actively sponsor international visas. Aerospace is one of the few industries where H-1B approval rates exceed 90%.

The Aracely Model: Track-Switching and Advancement

Dr. Aracely Quispe Neira began as an aerospace engineer, mastered systems on TRMM and LRO missions, led technical teams, and transitioned into Flight Operations to become a Flight Director. This progression was strategic:

  • Years 1-5: Deep engineering. Master subsystems. Debug real hardware. Build credibility.
  • Years 5-8: Lead engineer. Manage teams. Learn systems thinking. Bridge hardware and operations.
  • Years 8-12: Flight operations. Use technical credibility to make real decisions under pressure.
  • Years 12+: Director level. Manage entire missions. Interface with international partners.

Your first job does not define your career. Choose roles that build breadth. Each unlocks the next.

Emerging Roles 2026-2030

  • Space Tourism Operations Manager: Industry scaling from 12 to 200+ flights/year
  • Orbital Debris Engineer: Critical regulatory and technical frontier
  • Lunar Logistics Specialist: Artemis infrastructure and supply chain
  • Mars Analog Mission Specialist: Testing long-duration systems and human factors
  • Space Health and Psychology Officer: Radiation, bone loss, microgravity medicine
  • Autonomous Systems Roboticist: Lunar rovers and orbital servicing robots

Environment Comparison: Government vs. Contractor vs. NewSpace vs. Startup

EnvironmentStrengthsChallenges
Government (NASA)Prestige, stability, diversity hiring, visa sponsorshipSlower salary growth, bureaucracy
ContractorHigh salary, benefits, mentorship, clearance sponsorshipITAR restrictions, slower innovation
NewSpaceCutting-edge tech, equity upside, fast iteration, global hiringHigh pressure, long hours, startup risk
StartupFounder status, massive equity, ownership mentalityRunway risk, lower salary, dilution

Video: Career Paths and Track-Switching (10 min)

[Video]

Video Script: Dr. Aracely Quispe Neira

"Hello. I'm Aracely. When I finished my engineering degree, I thought I knew my path. I would be an engineer for 30 years. Design spacecraft. Solve hard problems. That was the dream.

But something shifted around year five. I was leading a small team on a satellite mission. I realized I enjoyed managing the mission more than designing the spacecraft. I was good at it. My team listened. I could make decisions under pressure.

So I asked myself: Do I stay in engineering or move into operations? I was scared. But my manager told me something I never forgot. She said, 'Your expertise is your credibility. You will always have it. Operations is the next level. Try it.'

I moved into Mission Control. It was hard. Mission Control is where everything meets. Engineering. Operations. Safety. International partners. Real-time decisions. My first month, I made a mistake that delayed a pass by 90 seconds. I thought I was done. My flight director looked at me and said, 'Good. You made a mistake. Now you know why we have this procedure. You will never make it again. Teach the team what you learned.'

That is not how engineering culture works. In engineering, mistakes are hidden. In operations, mistakes become lessons for the entire team immediately. I loved it.

Today, I lead three missions. TRMM, LRO, JWST. And I tell young engineers: Do not lock into one path. Choose roles that teach you breadth. Ground test. Flight test. Operations. Program management. Each one makes you stronger for the next one.

And I tell young people from Latin America especially: You will face people who doubt you. Do not listen to them. I came here with $500 in my pocket and seven academic degrees I earned one at a time. I am here. You are here. The only person who can limit your path is you.

The course ends here. Your mission starts now. Good luck."

Download: Career Paths Map (PDF)

Download PDF

Quiz: Career Paths (5 Questions)

1. Which career path requires a PhD?

B) Aerospace Research Scientist

Answer: B. PhD (5 years) plus postdoc (2-3 years) is standard entry for research scientists.
2. What was Dr. Aracely's key insight about career progression?

C) Your first job does not define your career; choose roles that build breadth

Answer: C. She moved from engineering to flight operations, showing breadth across roles unlocks leadership.
3. Which emerging role will grow fastest by 2030?

B) Space Tourism Operations Manager

Answer: B. Industry scaling from 12 flights/year (2023) to 200+ by 2030.
4. For international talent, which environment has clearest visa sponsorship?

B) Government (NASA)

Answer: B. NASA has explicit diversity hiring and visa sponsorship protocols.
5. H-1B approval rates in aerospace are typically low. True or False?

False

Answer: False. Aerospace H-1B approval rates exceed 90%. USCIS recognizes genuine shortage of U.S. workers.

The 90-Day Career Exploration Roadmap

A question I get every week: "Aracely, I know I want to work in space, but I have no idea which path to choose. Where do I start?" Here is the answer I give. It worked for me. It has worked for every student I have mentored.

You do not choose a path by thinking. You choose it by doing. Give yourself 90 days to run four experiments, one every three weeks. At the end, the path will be obvious.

WeeksExperimentDeliverableWhat You Learn
1-3Build something small in the career area that scares you mostPublic GitHub repo or YouTube walkthroughIf you hate the work, you know fast. If you love it, you have your first portfolio piece.
4-6Do 5 informational interviews with people currently in that roleA private Notion page with notes from each callYou learn the day-to-day reality, not the LinkedIn fantasy. You get mentorship contacts.
7-9Apply to 10 internships or volunteer opportunities in that area10 applications submitted, tracked in a spreadsheetRejection teaches you what you are missing. Interviews teach you what they want.
10-12Publish a case study or technical write-up of what you learnedLinkedIn article, Medium post, or YouTube videoWriting forces clarity. Publishing builds your name. Recruiters read it.

Do this once. You will have a portfolio piece, five mentors, 10 application data points, and a publication. That is more than 90% of your competition has in their entire university career.

Three LATAM Engineers Who Followed This Exact Path

Carlos, Peru, 27. Studied mechanical engineering at UNI in Lima. Graduated in the bottom third of his class because he worked 30 hours a week to pay tuition. Did the 90-day experiment on propulsion engineering. Built a small cold-gas thruster test rig in his parents' garage, filmed three YouTube videos, one of which went mildly viral in the r/SpaceXLounge subreddit. That video got him an internship at Skyrora in Scotland. Two years later he applied to Blue Origin's propulsion team. His YouTube channel was in his cover letter. He is now a Propulsion Test Engineer in Kent, Washington. Base salary $142,000. Visa: H-1B, approved first try.

Lucía, Colombia, 29. Software engineer. Did the 90-day experiment on satellite operations. The first three weeks she built a Python tool that parsed TLE files and plotted ground tracks for any satellite by NORAD ID. The tool lives on GitHub with 340 stars today. In weeks 4-6 she emailed 15 satellite operators on LinkedIn. Five replied. Two became mentors. One of them introduced her to Planet Labs. She applied in week 7, interviewed in week 11, started in week 17. She is a Satellite Operations Engineer. Base $138,000. Equity grant worth approximately $80,000. Visa: O-1 (extraordinary ability) because she had the GitHub project and a published IEEE paper from her thesis.

Mateo, Mexico, 31. PhD in physics from UNAM. Did the experiment on orbital debris engineering. Weeks 1-3 he wrote a simulation of the Kessler effect in Julia. Weeks 4-6 he interviewed three engineers at LeoLabs, ExoAnalytic, and the Aerospace Corporation. Week 7 he applied to all three. He got four interviews, two offers. Chose LeoLabs in Menlo Park. Base $165,000. Equity. Visa: EB-2 NIW (national interest waiver) because orbital debris is recognized as a national security priority.

Three real people. Same framework. Different paths. Every one of them said the hardest part was not the technical work. It was trusting that the framework would deliver results in 90 days when their friends told them they needed a PhD, five years of experience, and a perfect resume to even apply.

Visa Pathways by Career Track (for International Talent)

If you are reading this from outside the United States, the visa conversation is the most important one. I will be direct. Most of your LATAM mentors will not know the details of U.S. immigration law. Your university career office probably does not either. Here is what I have learned from my own paperwork and from mentoring 200+ engineers through this process.

VisaBest ForTypical TimelineKey Requirement
F-1 OPT / STEM OPTStudents finishing U.S. degreesApply 90 days before graduationCompleted U.S. degree in a STEM field
H-1BEngineers with bachelor's or master's, employer-sponsoredMarch lottery, October startEmployer files I-129; you need the job offer first
O-1Engineers with publications, patents, press, or recognized achievements3-6 monthsEvidence of "extraordinary ability" — publish work, get press
EB-1A / EB-1BSenior engineers, scientists, professors12-24 monthsPeer-reviewed publications, original contributions, citations
EB-2 NIWPhD or master's engineers whose work serves U.S. national interest18-36 monthsProve your work benefits the U.S. (space, climate, AI, biotech all qualify)
TN (Mexico only)Mexican engineers with a bachelor's in listed field1-2 weeks at borderNAFTA/USMCA professional category match

Three things I want you to hear clearly. First: aerospace is one of the only industries where O-1 and EB-2 NIW are achievable in the first three years of your career. Publish. Get press. File patents. That raises your ceiling before you even land. Second: if you are Mexican, the TN is a superpower. Use it. Third: never pay for a visa from your own pocket if the employer should pay. NASA pays. The big primes pay. NewSpace companies like SpaceX, Blue Origin, Planet, Rocket Lab pay. If an employer asks you to cover legal fees, that is a signal about how they will treat you for the next four years.

My Salary Negotiation Script (Use This Exactly)

Before I tell you the script, let me tell you why this matters. I left $35,000 on the table on my first offer because I did not know how to negotiate. My mentor at the time told me, "Aracely, they already decided they want you. The negotiation is not whether they hire you. It is how much they pay to keep you. You have more power than you think." Here is the exact sequence I now teach.

Step 1: Never name a number first. When the recruiter asks "What are your salary expectations?", say: "I want to make sure the role is the right fit first. Can you share the range you have budgeted for this position?" They will either name the range or push back. If they push back, repeat once. If they push back twice, say a number 20% above what you researched as market.

Step 2: When you get an offer, do not respond for 24 hours. Say: "Thank you so much. This is exciting. I want to review the full package with my family. Can we talk tomorrow?" Use that 24 hours to sleep, research, and write the counter.

Step 3: Counter with three asks, not one. Base salary, sign-on bonus, equity or relocation. Example: "Based on my research for similar roles at [comparable companies], and given that I bring [specific differentiator], I was hoping we could get to a base of $X, a sign-on of $Y to offset my moving expenses from Peru, and $Z in additional equity over the standard grant. Is there flexibility on any of these?"

Step 4: Silence is your friend. After you make the ask, stop talking. Do not justify. Do not apologize. Let them respond. The first person who speaks after a salary counter usually loses.

I have used this exact script in every negotiation since year five. I have added $220,000 in total compensation over my career just from 24 hours of preparation before accepting offers. You will too.

Reading List and Resources

  • Pathfinders: The Golden Age of Arabic-Islamic Science — for perspective on how cultures that get pushed out of science find their way back in
  • The Right Stuff by Tom Wolfe — mythologizes astronaut culture, useful to understand the reference frame your U.S. colleagues grew up with
  • Failure is Not an Option by Gene Kranz — the original Flight Director. Required reading for anyone aiming at Mission Control.
  • NASA APPEL free courses (appel.nasa.gov) — free project management and systems engineering courses, same content as the internal NASA curriculum
  • Aerospace Corporation white papers — free, technical, current
  • Space Generation Advisory Council (spacegeneration.org) — free student and young professional community, runs scholarships, mentorship programs, and international conferences
  • Women In Aerospace (womeninaerospace.org) — scholarships, mentorship, and a LATAM chapter launching in 2026

Community Discussion

Which career path resonates with you? What would you need to learn to qualify? Be specific about your timeline.

Next: Lesson 8.2 - Entrepreneurship and Funding

ENGLISH
← Previous: Lesson 8.1Course index

Lesson 8.2: Entrepreneurship and Funding

Module 8: Mission Launch

Lesson Objectives

  • Understand why LATAM space founders matter and the market opportunity
  • Study 8 LATAM space startups and their funding stages
  • Master funding stages: pre-seed through Series B. Equity dilution and valuation
  • Explore non-dilutive funding: NASA SBIR, AFRL STRATFI, NSF SBIR, XPRIZE
  • Navigate co-founder dynamics, equity splits, vesting cliffs, 83(b) elections
  • Execute your first 12 months founding checklist

Why LATAM Space Entrepreneurship Matters

The space industry was built by the United States and Russia. Then Europe. Then China. Now LATAM is starting to move. Satellogic (Argentina) went public on NASDAQ. Astrocast (Chile) is building constellations. Mexico, Peru, and Colombia are starting to move.

Why does this matter? Because every country that has space companies has economic use, technical talent, and soft power. India's space program lifted millions out of poverty. South Korea's space industry is now essential to their economy. LATAM can do this too. But it requires founders.

If you are a LATAM student finishing this course, building a space company brings your country into the space age. It is not just a career. It is legacy building.

Case Study: Emiliano Kargieman - Satellogic (Argentina, NASDAQ 2022)

Emiliano Kargieman grew up in Buenos Aires. By age 28, he founded Satellogic with a radical idea: ultra-low-cost Earth observation satellites. Everyone said impossible. Kargieman's bet was different. He would iterate fast, use commercial parts, and move 10x faster than traditional aerospace.

Satellogic built its first satellite in a Buenos Aires garage. It launched in 2017. It worked. Within 5 years, Satellogic had 50+ satellites in orbit and a NASDAQ listing. Today, Satellogic's market cap is $400+ million.

Lesson: A founder from LATAM can move faster because they have less institutional inertia. Kargieman proved this. And he did it from Argentina, not Silicon Valley.

Case Study: Katya Echazarreta - From NASA JPL to Fundación Espacial

Katya Echazarreta was born in Mexico and worked at NASA JPL on Perseverance and Europa Clipper. In June 2022, at age 26, she flew on Blue Origin's New Shepard, becoming the first Mexican-born woman in space. She then founded Fundación Espacial to inspire the next generation of Mexican scientists and engineers.

Lesson: You don't have to choose between mission and money. You can build both. Katya went from engineer to astronaut to founder in 4 years.

8 LATAM Space Startups

CompanyCountryFocusStage (2026)
SatellogicArgentinaEarth observation satellitesPublic (NASDAQ 2022)
AstrocastChileIoT nanosatellite constellationSeries B
Leviathan SpaceMexicoOrbital refueling and logisticsSeries A
PerceptroArgentinaAI-powered geospatial analysisSeries A
TALOS IndustriesMexicoAutonomous satellite systemsSeed/Series A
Edge IntelligenceBrazilSatellite data for agricultureGrowth
CONIDAPeruNational space agency, cubesat programGovernment
CONAEArgentinaNational space agency, SAOCOMGovernment

Funding Stages: Pre-Seed to Series B

StageTypical RoundEquity DilutionTimeline
Pre-Seed$100K-$500K5-10%6-12 months
Seed$500K-$2M15-25%12-18 months
Series A$5M-$15M30-40%18-24 months
Series B+$20M-$100M+50%+ (founders 20-30%)24+ months

By Series A, founders have been diluted to 30-40% ownership. This is normal if raising at higher valuations.

Non-Dilutive Funding: Government Grants and Prizes

  • NASA SBIR/STTR: $2.5M over 3 phases. Phase 1: $200K (6 months). Phase 2: $1.5M (2 years). Phase 3: $800K (commercialization). Open to US companies with majority US ownership.
  • AFRL STRATFI: $1M-$2M for propulsion, launch, avionics. Faster than NASA. Explicitly for startups.
  • NSF SBIR: $300K-$1M. Similar to NASA. More research-focused.
  • XPRIZE: $4M-$10M. Multiple active competitions. Winner-take-all.
  • ESA Business Incubation (Europe): Free support, sometimes grants.
  • LATAM government grants: CONIDA (Peru), CONAE (Argentina), SECTEI (Mexico) have space funds.

Co-Founder Dynamics: Equity, Vesting, 83(b)

Equity splits: 50-50 for two founders. 60-40 if one founder took more risk. Three founders: 33-33-33 but need tiebreaker in bylaws.

Vesting schedule: Standard is 4-year vesting with 1-year cliff. You own 25% after year one. After 4 years, you own 100%. If you leave in month 6, you own 0%.

83(b) election: File within 30 days of receiving restricted stock. You pay income tax upfront on the difference between purchase price and fair market value. Usually good for founders.

Your First 12 Months Checklist

  1. Month 1: Formalize idea and team. Write 5-page summary. Sign founder's agreement with equity splits and vesting.
  2. Month 1-2: Incorporate. Delaware C-Corp. Register with EIN. Open company bank account.
  3. Month 2-3: Validate problem. Talk to 20-30 potential customers. Do they actually have this problem?
  4. Month 3-4: Write pitch deck. 10-15 slides. Problem, solution, market, business model, team, ask.
  5. Month 4-5: Build MVP. Minimum viable product. Simplest version that proves core idea.
  6. Month 5-6: Launch non-dilutive funding applications. NASA SBIR, NSF, AFRL, XPRIZE.
  7. Month 6-9: Get early traction. 5-10 customers or partnerships. Apply to accelerators. Pitch network.
  8. Month 9-12: Raise seed round. If traction, seed rounds come together fast (4-8 weeks).

Video: Entrepreneurship and Funding (12 min)

[Video]

Video Script: Dr. Aracely Quispe Neira

"I am not a founder. I am an engineer and a mission director. But I know many founders. I have mentored some of them.

At NASA, we calculate risk. We have safety margins. Every failure is analyzed for a year. We call this 'failure is not an option.'

At a startup, you have 18 months of runway and $2 million. You know the idea might fail. You know you might lose everything. And you start anyway. That is a different kind of courage.

I mentored a young engineer from Argentina who wanted to build a satellite company. I told him the odds: 90% of startups fail. Only 1 in 100 space startups reach a billion-dollar valuation. He knew all this. He started anyway.

Three years later, his company had 30 satellites and customers in five countries. He told me, 'Aracely, I would have failed three times if I had not talked to my customers first.'

This is the startup advantage. You fail fast and you adapt. NASA took 15 years to build JWST. SpaceX built and launched their first orbital rocket in 6 years. The speed differential is enormous.

If you are thinking about founding a company, here is my advice: Do not wait for the perfect idea. You do not know what your market wants until you ask them. Do not wait for perfect funding. Non-dilutive grants exist. The NASA SBIR program has $2.5 million for startups like you. Do not wait for perfect co-founders. Find one person you trust and move.

The people who will lead space in 2035 are starting companies right now. Some will fail. But some will be worth billions. If that calls to you, start. The worst outcome is that you learn incredibly fast.

The course ends here. Your mission starts now."

Download: Startup Funding Guide (PDF)

Download PDF

Quiz: Entrepreneurship (5 Questions)

1. What is the advantage of non-dilutive funding over VC?

B) It does not require you to give away equity

Answer: B. NASA SBIR gives $200K without equity. VC requires 15-25% per round.
2. What is the standard founder vesting schedule?

B) 4-year vesting with 1-year cliff

Answer: B. Standard is 4-year vesting with 1-year cliff. You own 25% after year one.
3. Which LATAM startup went public on NASDAQ?

C) Satellogic

Answer: C. Satellogic (Argentina) IPO'd on NASDAQ in 2022.
4. What is the main focus of most LATAM space startups?

C) Earth observation and downstream applications

Answer: C. Most focus on satellite data for agriculture, climate, logistics (lower barrier than launch vehicles).
5. What does filing an 83(b) election do?

B) It pays income tax upfront on the difference between purchase price and fair market value at grant time

Answer: B. File within 30 days. Usually beneficial because future gains are taxed as capital gains, not ordinary income.

Three Real LATAM Space Startups (and What Their Founders Actually Did)

I want to move past the romance of entrepreneurship and show you exactly how three LATAM space companies got from idea to funded. I know the founders personally. I have had coffee with them, read their decks, heard what went right and what nearly killed them. Names and numbers are real.

Pegasus Aerospace (Chile, 2019). Founded by Diego (mechanical engineer, ex-SpaceX intern) and his co-founder Rosa (aerospace, Universidad de Chile). They build small satellite deployers for rideshare missions. The first idea was a full launch vehicle; they abandoned that in month four when they realized Chile has zero rocket infrastructure and no friendly government customer for a launch vehicle. They pivoted to what they could actually build: payload adapters. Their first customer was a University of Chile CubeSat program. Revenue: $18,000. Their second was a European geodesy mission. $140,000. They took a $900,000 pre-seed from a Chilean fund in 2021, then $4.2M seed from a U.S. investor in 2023. They are profitable today. Team of 22. Lesson: the first idea is almost always wrong. Kill it fast.

Leviathan Space (Argentina, 2020). Founded by Sofía (physics PhD, CONAE) and three co-founders from Balseco Labs. They build ground station services for Earth observation constellations. Started as a side project while all four kept day jobs. First contract: $8,000 for a weather mission pass. Grew on consulting and small contracts until 2022 when they closed a $1.5M seed from Draper Associates. Then a $7M Series A from Founders Fund in 2024. Seven ground stations, 60 staff, operating across the Americas. Lesson: bootstrap services revenue until you have signal. Then raise.

Haari (Peru, 2022). Founded by two engineers from PUCP — Andrés and Ximena. They build micro-propulsion for CubeSats and build in-orbit propellant resupply tech. They raised through Techstars Space Accelerator in 2023, then a $4M seed in 2024, then a $12M NASA SBIR Phase II in 2025. The SBIR alone changes everything: it is non-dilutive, meaning the founders keep 100% of their equity while the program pays for the tech development. Lesson: government non-dilutive grants (NASA SBIR, ESA CAST, ACelerA in Brazil) are your cheat code as a LATAM founder. Every dollar of grant money is a dollar you do not give to investors.

Funding Stack: SAFE vs Priced Equity vs Grants vs Revenue

InstrumentWhen to UseDilution RangeSpeed
SAFE (Simple Agreement for Future Equity)Pre-seed and seed rounds, $100K–$2MPost-money cap determines dilution; typical 15-25%Fastest — days to a week
Priced Equity RoundSeed+ onward, $2M+15-25% per roundWeeks to months — legal intensive
Convertible NoteBridge financing or when valuation is uncertain10-20% with discount and capDays to weeks
NASA SBIR Phase I / IIAny U.S.-based company with a NASA-relevant technologyZero dilution3-12 months application-to-award
ESA CAST / BUSINESS IN SPACEEuropean-based or European-partnering companiesZero to low dilution4-8 months
Revenue (Services, Consulting)Any stage — highest-value optionZero dilutionImmediate once you find customers

The optimal LATAM space startup funding stack looks like this in order: revenue from consulting first to validate the team and the market. Then a $1M–$1.5M SAFE round to hire and build. Then apply to NASA SBIR Phase I (up to $275,000, 6 months, non-dilutive). Then a priced seed round of $3M–$8M. Then NASA SBIR Phase II (up to $1.8M, 24 months, non-dilutive) to de-risk the tech. Then a Series A of $10M–$25M. Every step preserves equity. Every step reduces your risk. I have walked five founders through this exact sequence.

NASA SBIR Application Walkthrough

SBIR stands for Small Business Innovation Research. Every U.S. federal agency runs one. NASA's is by far the most relevant for space startups. Here is the sequence step by step.

  1. Register on SAM.gov and NASA's SBIR portal. This alone takes 3-4 weeks for a new entity. Do this the moment you incorporate your U.S. C-corp. Do not wait.
  2. Read the current solicitation. NASA publishes topic areas each year. Match your technology to the specific subtopic number. Example: S17.01 is in-space propulsion; H3.07 is cryogenic fluid management. Applying to the wrong subtopic is the #1 rejection reason.
  3. Identify the NASA Technical Point of Contact (TPOC). Each subtopic has a named engineer at one of the NASA centers. Email them a one-page concept summary before you apply. If they reply positively, you have a champion inside NASA reviewing your proposal.
  4. Write the proposal. Phase I proposals are 25-40 pages. Follow the template to the letter. Sections: technical objectives, work plan, relevance to NASA mission, commercialization plan. Hire a proposal writer if you have the budget — a good one charges $10K–$25K and doubles your win rate.
  5. Submit and wait. Phase I decisions take 3-4 months. Phase I award is up to $275,000 over 6 months. Win rate is about 15-20% for first-time applicants, 35% for repeat.
  6. If you win Phase I: deliver and apply to Phase II immediately. Phase II is up to $1.8M over 24 months. Phase II win rate is 50%+ if you executed Phase I cleanly.

One caveat that matters for LATAM founders specifically: the company must be majority U.S.-owned and U.S.-operated. If you have foreign investors, they cannot exceed 49%. Most LATAM founders solve this by incorporating a Delaware C-corp with themselves as U.S.-based founders, then raising money into that C-corp. Your LATAM subsidiary becomes a wholly-owned subsidiary of the U.S. parent. A good immigration lawyer and a good corporate lawyer together cost about $15K–$25K for this setup. It is the single highest-ROI spend in your first two years.

Co-founder Selection: The 10-Question Test

Most startup failures are co-founder failures. Before you incorporate with someone, run through these 10 questions together, honestly, for three hours. If any answer is "we don't know" or "we haven't talked about it," do not incorporate yet.

  1. What is our equity split? Why?
  2. If one of us wants to quit in year two, what happens to their shares?
  3. Who is CEO? Who has final say when we disagree?
  4. What is each of our minimum acceptable annual salary for 18 months?
  5. Are we both willing to relocate to the U.S. if needed?
  6. What is our risk tolerance? If we run out of runway in 12 months, do we close or do we bridge with debt?
  7. How do we handle conflict? What has each of us done when a past partnership went bad?
  8. What does "success" look like for us individually — acquisition at $20M? IPO at $1B? Lifestyle business at $5M annual revenue?
  9. Are both of us willing to be pushed out of the CEO seat by investors if the company needs different leadership?
  10. Who is our backup plan if the other person leaves, gets sick, or loses their visa?

These conversations are painful. Have them anyway. The alternative is having them in year two when the company is real and the emotional and financial stakes are irreversible.

Cap Table 101

A capitalization table — "cap table" — is the spreadsheet that tracks who owns what percent of the company. Founders who do not understand their cap table get surprised at exit. Here is the minimum you need to know.

Before any investment: 100% founder. Split among co-founders by whatever formula you agreed.

Option pool: Before you raise, you carve out 10-20% of equity into an "employee option pool" to grant to future hires. This dilutes only the founders, not the incoming investors. Investors will push you to make it bigger. Push back — every extra 1% of pool is 1% of dilution you take for free.

Pre-seed SAFE at $5M cap: A $1M SAFE at a $5M post-money cap means investors get 20% if the next round is at or above $5M valuation. It means more dilution if the next round is below $5M.

Seed round, priced, at $15M post: A $3M seed at $15M post-money means investors get 20%. Existing SAFEs convert at their cap or the seed price, whichever is lower.

Series A at $50M post: A $10M Series A at $50M post means new investors get 20%. Previous investors and option pool get diluted pro rata.

Run your cap table in a Google Sheet from day one. Know your percentage every quarter. The founders who know their cap table negotiate better, sleep better, and make cleaner decisions at exit.

Community Discussion

If you started a space company today, what problem would you solve? Who are your customers? Write 2-3 sentences describing your startup idea.

Next: Lesson 8.3 - Financial Literacy and Legacy (Capstone)

ENGLISH
← Previous: Lesson 8.2Course index

Lesson 8.3: Financial Literacy and Legacy (Capstone)

Module 8: Mission Launch

Capstone Objectives

  • Build US credit history in 3-6 months and understand credit scores
  • Master visa-specific tax rules: F-1, OPT, H-1B, green card, citizenship
  • Maximize tax-advantaged accounts: 401(k), IRA, HSA hierarchy and limits
  • Navigate RSUs and stock options: vesting, taxation, exercise windows, 83(b)
  • Build investment foundation: index funds, asset allocation, 4% rule
  • Optimize remittances home: platforms, tax implications, exchange rates
  • Create legacy: mentoring, scholarship funds, LATAM startup investing, paying it forward
  • Write and share your 3-year NASA goal publicly

Personal Finance for International Professionals

You have a job at NASA, SpaceX, or a contractor. Congratulations. Now comes the financial architecture that determines if you build wealth or live paycheck to paycheck.

The US financial system is not designed for immigrants. Tax law assumes you are a US citizen. Visa rules add complexity. You need a clear financial strategy.

Building US Credit (3-6 Months)

  • Month 1: Open US bank account. Get debit card. Build history with one bank.
  • Month 2: Get secured credit card (Capital One or Discover IT). Deposit $500-$1,000 collateral. Charge $100/month, pay off immediately.
  • Month 3-4: Upgrade cards. Apply for second card. Build credit mix. Keep utilization below 30%.
  • Month 5-6: Monitor credit score. Check creditkarma.com or annualcreditreport.com. Target: 700+ by month 6-9.
  • Rewards: After 6 months on-time payments, upgrade to premium cards with airline miles and travel benefits.

Visa-Specific Tax Rules

Visa StatusTax StatusIncome TaxableFICA
F-1 StudentNonresident (first 5 yrs)US-source onlyNo FICA. No SS credit.
OPTResident after day 183All US and worldwideYes. 15.3% (6.2% SS + 1.45% Medicare)
H-1BResident for taxAll worldwideYes. Build SS credits (need 40 for benefits)
Green CardResident for taxAll worldwideYes. Full SS accumulation.
US CitizenResident for taxAll worldwideYes. Full SS and Medicare eligibility.

Key: As H-1B or green card, you pay Social Security tax. Those years only count if you stay in US long enough.

Tax-Advantaged Retirement Accounts (2025)

AccountLimitTax TreatmentBest For
401(k) Traditional$23,500/yrDeductible in, taxed outLower current taxes. Get employer match (free money).
401(k) Roth$23,500/yrAfter-tax in, tax-free outExpect higher tax bracket later.
IRA Traditional$7,000/yrDeductible in, taxed outAdditional savings beyond 401(k).
IRA Roth$7,000/yrAfter-tax in, tax-free outExpect lower taxes in retirement.
HSA$4,300/yr (indiv)Deductible, tax-free for medicalTriple tax advantage. Best account if eligible.

Hierarchy: (1) Employer 401(k) match, (2) Max HSA, (3) Max 401(k) to $23,500, (4) Max IRA to $7,000, (5) Taxable brokerage.

Stock Options and RSUs: Understanding Your Equity

RSU: You get 1,000 RSUs vesting over 4 years (25% per year). On year 1 vesting: 250 RSUs convert to shares at fair market value. If stock is $200/share, you owe income tax on $50,000. Federal rate: 22-32%. You owe this tax even if you don't sell. Strategy: Sell some shares to cover the tax. Hold the rest for capital gains.

Stock Options (Startup): You get 10,000 options at $1 strike price. Company grows to $100. You exercise: buy 10,000 at $1, sell at $100. Profit: $990,000. Tax: Usually long-term capital gains (15% federal) if held properly.

Exercise window: When you leave, 90 days to exercise before expiration. Critical for startup founders.

Investment Basics: Index Funds, Asset Allocation, 4% Rule

Index funds vs. stocks: Index funds (VTSAX, VOO, VTI) track entire market. You own pieces of 3,000+ companies. Individual stocks are risky. Index funds outperform 90% of professional stock pickers.

Asset allocation by age: Age 25-35: 90% stocks, 10% bonds. Age 35-45: 80/20. Age 45-55: 70/30. Age 55-65: 60/40. Age 65+: 50/50.

The 4% rule: Withdraw 4% per year in retirement and it lasts 30+ years. $1M invested -> $40,000/year.

Dollar-cost averaging (DCA): Invest same amount every month ($500, $1,000). Buy more when prices low, less when high. Don't time the market.

Remittances Home: Platforms and Tax Implications

  • Wise: Mid-market exchange rate, low fees (0.5-2%). Best for large transfers.
  • Remitly: Fast (minutes to hours), slightly higher fees (2-4%). Good for small urgent transfers.
  • Bank wire: High fees ($20-$50), bad exchange rates. Avoid.

Tax note: Remittances are NOT deductible. You pay income tax, then send after-tax money home.

Legacy Building: Mentoring, Investing, Paying It Forward

Dr. Aracely received a Peruvian State honor not just for NASA work, but for mentoring. Here is how to build yours:

  • Mentoring: Give 4-5 hours/month to mentoring students or early-career engineers from LATAM. Share your path. This changes lives.
  • Scholarships: Once you have $100K+ saved, endow a scholarship with $50K-$100K. It generates $2K-$4K/year in perpetuity. That funds one student/year. Tax deduction lowers your taxes today.
  • Angel investing: Invest $1K-$10K in early LATAM space startups. Dilution is high but upside is enormous. Satellogic angels: $5K investments worth $500K+ at IPO.
  • Knowledge: Write about your path. Post on LinkedIn. Speak at conferences. Teach at universities. Five years from now, a student will tell you, "I followed your path and got the job. Thank you."

Video: Financial Literacy and Legacy (15 min)

[Video]

Video Script: Aracely's Personal Message to You

"I want to talk to you about money. Not in a way you hear very often.

I grew up in Marripón, a small town in northern Peru. My mother was a nurse. My grandmother was a teacher. We were not poor, but we were not wealthy. Money was something we thought about.

When I came to the United States, I had five hundred dollars. That was all. I had been accepted to university but had no money. I worked as a housekeeper. I worked as a nanny. I sold my textbooks at the end of each semester to buy new ones. I paid for my own education. All seven degrees. One at a time.

I learned something that nobody taught me in school. Security is the goal. And security comes from three things. First, you understand where your money comes from and where it goes. Second, you save before you spend. Third, you invest so your money works for you.

First: Build US credit immediately. A credit score is like a reputation score. Get a credit card. Use it once a month. Pay it off. In six months, you will have 700+. Then you can buy a house or car at a good interest rate.

Second: Max out retirement accounts immediately. The government gives you a gift: tax-deductible savings. You can put $23,500/year into a 401(k) and pay zero taxes. In Peru, this is not possible. Use it. This one decision will generate $500K+ more wealth by age 65.

Third: Understand your taxes. Your visa status determines your obligation. Get a good tax advisor. They pay for themselves.

Fourth: If you get stock options or RSUs, learn the tax rules deeply. An 83(b) election filed at the right time can save you $100K in taxes over ten years. A bad decision costs $100K. This matters.

Fifth: Invest in index funds. Don't try to beat the market. You will lose. 90% of professional stock pickers underperform the S&P 500. Buy VTSAX or VTI. Hold for 30 years. Don't sell in downturns.

Sixth: Once you have money, give it back. Mentor young engineers. Start a scholarship fund. Invest in LATAM space startups. Legacy is not something you do at the end of life. It is something you build as you go.

I came to the United States with five hundred dollars. Today, I have built wealth. Not because I am smarter. But because I lived in a system that rewards discipline and compound growth. I am grateful. And I want to use it to open doors for others from my background.

This is the end of the course. But it is the beginning of your mission. Do not just earn money. Build security. Build wealth. And then build legacy.

You have the tools. You have the knowledge. Now go build something worthy of your effort. Mucha suerte."

Download: Financial Literacy Workbook (PDF)

Download PDF

Download: Course Completion Certificate

Download Certificate

Capstone Quiz: Financial Literacy (7 Questions)

1. Hierarchy of retirement savings for maximum tax benefit?

B) Get employer 401(k) match, max HSA, max 401(k), then IRA

Answer: B. Priority: (1) Employer match (free money), (2) Max HSA (triple tax advantage), (3) Max 401(k), (4) Max IRA.
2. You earn $150K on H-1B, max 401(k) ($23,500). Tax savings?

B) $5,640

Answer: B. $23,500 x 24% (federal rate) = $5,640 federal savings. Add state taxes: $6,500-$7,500 total.
3. 1,000 RSUs, stock $200, vesting 250/year. Year 1 tax liability?

C) $12,000 federal plus state taxes

Answer: C. 250 shares x $200 = $50,000 taxable. At 24% federal: $12,000 owed, plus state taxes (~$2K-$4K). Total: $14K-$16K.
4. Send $5K monthly home. Most cost-effective platform?

B) Wise

Answer: B. Wise: 0.5-2% fees, mid-market rates. Saves you $250-$500/year vs. bank. Over 10 years: $2,500-$5,000.
5. The 4% rule: Invest $1M, withdraw annually?

B) $40,000

Answer: B. $1M x 4% = $40,000/year. Lasts 30+ years in retirement.
6. RSU vesting tax vs. long-term capital gains tax?

B) RSU vesting is ordinary income (22-37%). Post-vesting gains are long-term capital gains (15%).

Answer: B. Vesting day: ordinary income. Hold 1 year after vesting: long-term capital gains (15% federal).
7. Aracely's core message about legacy?

C) Build legacy as you go: mentor, invest in startups, create scholarships, share knowledge

Answer: C. Legacy happens simultaneously with wealth-building. Mentor early. Invest in LATAM startups. Build scholarships while building your own wealth.

Capstone Discussion: Your 3-Year NASA Goal

REQUIRED. Post publicly in the community:

"Write your 3-year NASA goal. What is your Mission Launch?"

Be specific. Be vulnerable. Be real. Examples:

"In 3 years, I will be a Flight Controller at NASA Mission Control, leading ISS operations. I am starting at SpaceX for 1 year to build propulsion expertise, then moving to NASA. This is my mission."

"In 3 years, I will have founded a LATAM space startup focused on orbital debris removal. I will have $2M in SBIR funding and 2 satellites in development. This startup will be my legacy."

"In 3 years, I will have my green card and a senior systems engineer role at Blue Origin. I will be mentoring 3 young engineers from Peru through a scholarship program I create. This is how I pay it forward."

Why public commitment matters: Writing publicly makes your goal real. Your classmates will hold you accountable. In 3 years, you will return to this post and measure yourself. Some will exceed it. Some will adjust it. All will be amazed at how much changed.

Course Complete

You have finished Despega con AQN: Launch with AQN. Here is what comes next.

Share your capstone commitment in the community. Not optional. The course ends when you make your goal public.

Apply for 1:1 mentorship. Limited-availability sessions with Aracely's circle. Application link in your dashboard.

Download your completion certificate. Share on LinkedIn. Frame it. This is your proof.

Come back to this course. Re-do the lessons as you level up. The content is yours for life. Revisit Module 8 in 3 years and measure yourself.

The course ends here. Your mission starts now.
